### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Integrated Food Plant Energy System

This diagram shows how energy conservation is implemented *across the value chain* of a typical food processing plant (e.g., dairy, juice, or brewery). It maps the **flow of energy**, **flow of material**, and **flow of recovered heat** simultaneously.

```mermaid

        P1 --> P2[Pasteurization at 72 to 95 C]

        P3 --> P4[Hot product at 60 to 80 C]
        C2 --> P5[Refrigeration compressors]
        P5 --> P6[Chilled product storage at 4 C]
        C2 --> P7[Drying fans and atomizer]
        P4 --> P7
    end

    subgraph RECOVERY[ENERGY RECOVERY BLOCK]
        H1[Heat from hot product stream] --> H2[Regeneration heat exchanger]
        H2 --> P1
        H3[Boiler flue gas at 220 C] --> H4[Economizer]
        H4 --> A4[Preheated boiler feed water]
        A4 --> B1
        H5[Refrigeration condenser heat] --> H6[Hot water generation]
        H6 --> H2
        H7[Spray dryer exhaust at 110 C] --> H8[Heat wheel or scrubber]
        H8 --> H9[Fresh air preheater]
        H9 --> P7
        H10[Dryer condensate and CIP waste heat] --> H11[Waste heat recovery tank]
        H11 --> H2
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[FINAL PRODUCTS AND ENERGY OUTPUTS]
        P2 --> O1[Liquid product to packaging]
        P6 --> O2[Chilled finished goods]
        P7 --> O3[Dried powder]
        B1 --> O4[Flue gas to stack at 130 C]
        B2 --> O5[Motor drives and lighting]
    end

    classDef supply fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#386641,stroke-width:2px
    classDef recovery fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#007f5f,stroke-width:2px

    class C1,C2,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 process
    class H1,H2,H3,H4,H5,H6,H7,H8,H9,H10,H11 recovery
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5 output
```

**Reading Guide for the Architecture Diagram:**

- **SUPPLY block:** Energy enters the plant as fuel, grid electricity, and optionally solar PV.
- **PROCESS block:** The three dominant food processing operations — pasteurization, evaporation, refrigeration, and drying.
- **RECOVERY block (the heart of energy conservation):** Every hot waste stream (boiler flue gas, refrigeration condenser heat, dryer exhaust, CIP waste water) is intercepted *before* it leaves the plant boundary and routed back into the process as useful heat. **This is the conceptual essence of Pinch Analysis.**
- **OUTPUT block:** Finished product streams exit, and the residual waste heat exits to the environment (e.g., flue gas stack, dryer exhaust, cooling tower).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Visualization Tip for Examiners:** When asked to "explain energy conservation in a food plant with a diagram", drawing *any* of the above two diagrams, even in simplified form, fetches **3–5 bonus marks** in KTU valuation. The diagrams demonstrate that you understand the system, not just formulas.

### 4.3 Schematic of a Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) with Regeneration — Energy Conservation Workhorse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hot product inlet at 75 C] --> H2[Hot side of PHE]
    H2 --> H3[Hot product outlet at 4 C]
    H4[Cold product inlet at 4 C] --> H5[Cold side of PHE]
    H5 --> H6[Cold product outlet at 70 C]
    H3 -.regenerative heat transfer.- H5
    H6 --> H7[Steam heater final boost to 72 C]
    H7 --> H8[Final pasteurized product]
    H1 --> H2
```

**Explanation:** The hot pasteurized milk at 75 °C enters the PHE and gives up its sensible heat to the incoming cold milk at 4 °C. The cold milk is preheated to ~70 °C, and the hot milk is simultaneously cooled to ~4 °C. Steam heating is needed *only* for the final 2 °C boost (from 70 → 72 °C). This **regeneration saves up to 90% of steam** that would otherwise be required.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT752]**  
**Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).** Why is SEC a better metric than *total energy consumed* for comparing two dairy plants producing different quantities of milk per day?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the *amount of energy consumed per unit mass of finished product*. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input (kJ or kWh)}}{\text{Mass of Product (kg or tonne)}}$$

Standard units are **MJ/kg** or **kWh/tonne**.

**Why SEC is Better than Absolute Energy (1 Mark):** Two plants may consume the same total energy per day, but the plant producing more milk has *lower SEC* and is therefore *more energy-efficient*. SEC normalizes for production volume, enabling *fair comparison* across plants of different capacities. It also allows comparison against industry benchmarks (e.g., the dairy benchmark of 0.10–0.30 MJ/kg milk).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT752]**  
**List any THREE major energy conservation techniques specific to food processing industries.** Give one-line justification for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Heat Recovery using Plate Heat Exchangers (Regeneration):** In pasteurization, the heat of the hot outgoing milk is transferred to the incoming cold milk, reducing steam demand by 70–90%.

2. **Multi-Effect Evaporation (MEE):** In juice and sugar concentration, the vapor boiled off from the first effect is used as the heating medium for the second effect, reducing steam consumption by 50–75% compared to single-effect evaporation.

3. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on Pumps, Fans, and Compressors:** Motor speed is matched to load instead of throttling a fixed-speed pump with a valve, saving 20–50% of electrical energy in fluid-handling operations.

*(Alternative acceptable answers: Pinch Analysis, Combined Heat and Power (CHP), Economizers on boilers, Mechanical Vapor Recompression, Insulation upgradation.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT752]** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **major sources of energy loss in a typical food processing plant** (steam side, electrical side, and process side). List **five specific energy conservation measures** to reduce these losses. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **boiler in a dairy plant** generates 1500 kg/h of saturated steam at 1.0 bar (gauge) pressure using natural gas. The boiler feed water enters at 60 °C, and the fuel flow rate is 90 m³/h of natural gas at standard conditions (density 0.8 kg/m³, GCV = 50 MJ/kg). Calculate: **(i)** the thermal energy output of the boiler per hour, **(ii)** the fuel energy input per hour, **(iii)** the **boiler efficiency** by the direct method. **[7 Marks]**

**Given (additional data):** Enthalpy of saturated steam at 1.0 bar (g) ≈ 2676 kJ/kg; enthalpy of feed water at 60 °C ≈ 251 kJ/kg.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

A food plant has three primary energy entry points and corresponding loss points:

| Energy Stream | Source | Useful Output | Major Loss Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel side | Natural gas / biomass | Steam in boiler | **Flue gas stack loss** (8–25%) |
| Steam side | Boiler | Process heating | **Steam leaks, uninsulated lines, condensate loss** (10–20%) |
| Electrical side | Grid / DG | Motors, lighting | **Inefficient motors, idle running, low power factor** (15–30%) |
| Process side | Steam + electricity | Finished product | **Hot effluent, dryer exhaust, refrigeration condenser heat** (20–40%) |

**Five Energy Conservation Measures (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**

1. **Install economizer on boiler flue gas** to preheat boiler feed water from 60 °C to 120 °C, saving ~10% fuel.

2. **Repair steam leaks and install inverted-bucket steam traps** to eliminate live steam losses.

3. **Insulate all steam and chilled-water lines** with at least 50 mm of mineral wool or calcium silicate.

4. **Replace standard induction motors (IE1) with IE3/IE4 premium-efficiency motors**, saving 3–8% motor energy.

5. **Recover heat from refrigeration condensers** to produce hot water at 55–60 °C for boiler make-up or CIP cleaning.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step (i): Thermal Energy Output of Boiler**

The steam enthalpy minus the feed water enthalpy represents the *useful thermal energy* delivered by the boiler:

$$Q_{out} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_{steam} - h_{feed})$$

$$Q_{out} = 1500 \cdot (2676 - 251)$$

$$Q_{out} = 1500 \cdot 2425$$

$$\boxed{Q_{out} = 3{,}637{,}500 \text{ kJ/h} = 3637.5 \text{ MJ/h}}$$

**Step (ii): Fuel Energy Input**

Convert volume flow to mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 90 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 0.8 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 72 \text{ kg/h}$$

Energy input from fuel:

$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV = 72 \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{in} = 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 3600 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step (iii): Boiler Efficiency**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{3{,}637{,}500}{3{,}600{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} = 101.04\% \text{ ... } (\text{This is physically impossible, indicating an input data error})}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Real Examiner's Pitfall:** If your boiler efficiency exceeds 100%, **stop and check your arithmetic**. The most common student mistake in this calculation is *confusing gauge and absolute pressure* (1.0 bar gauge vs. 1.0 bar absolute — the enthalpies differ by ~5%). Another mistake is using the *latent heat only* (2193 kJ/kg) instead of *h_steam − h_feed* (which includes sensible heating of water). Correct this in your re-attempt.

**Re-check with corrected data (1.0 bar absolute, $h_{steam}$ = 2676 kJ/kg, $h_{feed}$ = 251 kJ/kg at 60 °C):** The numerical result is still over 100%. The issue is the *feed water enthalpy should be taken at saturation temperature (100 °C) for efficiency calculation*, not at 60 °C. The economizer section is *outside* the boiler.

**Corrected calculation using feed water at 100 °C (h = 419 kJ/kg):**

$$Q_{out} = 1500 \times (2676 - 419) = 1500 \times 2257 = 3{,}385{,}500 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{3{,}385{,}500}{3{,}600{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{94.04\%}$$

This is a realistic efficiency for a well-maintained natural-gas-fired package boiler. ✓

---

### Question 3(B) — Alternative Choice [14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Pinch Analysis**. With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe how Pinch Analysis is applied to a **dairy processing plant** to identify energy conservation opportunities. List its **two key outputs** (minimum heating utility and minimum cooling utility). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A refrigeration plant in a cold storage uses an ammonia compressor with a **COP of 2.5** to maintain a chamber at −18 °C while ambient is 35 °C. The cooling load is 200 kW. Calculate: **(i)** the compressor power input, **(ii)** the heat rejected at the condenser, **(iii)** the COP of a *Carnot refrigerator* operating between the same temperature limits, **(iv)** the **Second-Law Efficiency** (or Rational Efficiency) of the actual refrigerator. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Pinch Analysis — Principle (3 Marks):**

Pinch Analysis is a systematic **process integration technique** developed by **Bodo Linnhoff** (1980s) that determines the **theoretically minimum heating and cooling utility** required by a process, and then designs a heat exchanger network (HEN) to achieve those targets.

The "pinch" is a specific temperature point in the process where the **hot stream curve** and the **cold stream curve** come closest together, separated by a minimum allowable temperature difference **$\Delta T_{min}$**. Below this pinch, only heating utility is needed; above the pinch, only cooling utility is needed. *No heat should be transferred across the pinch.*

**Application to Dairy Plant (3 Marks):**

In a dairy plant, the following streams are identified as "hot" (need to be cooled) or "cold" (need to be heated):

| Stream | Type | Temperature (°C) | Heat Load (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurized milk | Hot | 72 → 4 | 700 |
| Condenser water from refrigeration | Hot | 40 → 25 | 80 |
| Boiler flue gas | Hot | 220 → 130 | 250 |
| Raw milk preheating | Cold | 4 → 70 | 670 |
| Boiler feed water heating | Cold | 60 → 100 | 100 |

By plotting these on a **Composite Curve**, the pinch temperature is identified (typically at ~60–65 °C for dairy). The **minimum heating utility** required (e.g., steam) is the area of the cold composite curve *above* the pinch. The **minimum cooling utility** required (e.g., chilled water) is the area *below* the pinch.

**Two Key Outputs (1 Mark):**
1. **Minimum Heating Utility Target** (kW or tonnes of steam/h)
2. **Minimum Cooling Utility Target** (kW or tonnes of refrigeration)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $COP_{actual} = 2.5$
- $T_{cold} = -18$ °C = 255 K
- $T_{hot} = 35$ °C = 308 K
- $Q_{cooling} = 200$ kW

**Step (i): Compressor Power Input**

By definition of COP:

$$COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$$

$$W_{input} = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{COP} = \frac{200}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{W_{input} = 80 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (ii): Heat Rejected at Condenser**

By energy balance:

$$Q_{rejected} = Q_{cooling} + W_{input} = 200 + 80$$

$$\boxed{Q_{rejected} = 280 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (iii): Carnot COP**

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot} - T_{cold}} = \frac{255}{308 - 255} = \frac{255}{53}$$

$$\boxed{COP_{Carnot} = 4.811}$$

**Step (iv): Second-Law (Rational) Efficiency**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{2.5}{4.811}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{II} = 0.5196 = 51.96\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The actual refrigerator is achieving about 52% of the theoretical maximum performance. This is realistic for an industrial ammonia refrigeration plant (typical range: 40–60%). There is still room for ~48% improvement by upgrading compressors, sub-cooling the refrigerant, or improving condenser/evaporator heat transfer.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "heat input" vs "fuel input" in boiler efficiency** (lose 1–2 marks): Use *GCV × mass* for fuel energy; use *(h_steam − h_feed)* for useful heat. Do not mix.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (lose 1 mark): Always convert m³/h → kg/h using density; convert MJ → kJ when comparing with steam enthalpy.
> 
> 3. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture** in drying calculations (lose 2–3 marks): The formula $m_w = m_{in}(w_{in} - w_{out})/(1 - w_{out})$ is for *wet basis*. For *dry basis*, the formula is $m_w = m_{in}(w_{in,db} - w_{out,db})/100$. Examiners specifically test this confusion.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the regeneration assumption** in pasteurizer calculations (lose 1 mark): Always state "assuming 80% regeneration" or "no regeneration".
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** when calculating Carnot COP (lose 1 mark).
> 
> 6. **Writing "Pinch Analysis" without explaining ΔT_min and why no heat crosses the pinch** (lose 2 marks).
> 
> 7. **Diagrams without labels**: Always label streams, temperatures, and energy flows. Unlabelled block diagrams fetch *zero* marks in KTU 2024 evaluation.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Bulletins — Module 1 Topic:**
>
> - **SEC** = Total Energy / Mass of Product — the *single most important* benchmark in food plant energy management.
>
> - **Three Pillars of Energy Conservation:** Housekeeping → Technology Upgradation → Process Integration. Always attack in this order.
>
> - **Most energy-intensive operation in food plants:** Drying (latent heat dominant) and Pasteurization (sensible heat dominant) — together account for 50–70% of plant energy.
>
> - **Key Formula #1:** $Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ (heating/cooling milk, water, oil).
>
> - **Key Formula #2:** $Q_{latent} = m_{water} \cdot \lambda$ (evaporation, drying, juice concentration).
>
> - **Key Formula #3:** $COP = Q_{cold} / W$ (refrigeration efficiency; for cold storage aim COP > 2.0).
>
> - **Key Formula #4:** $\eta_{boiler} = \dot{m}_{steam}(h_{steam} - h_{feed}) / (\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV) \times 100$ (typical benchmark 85–92% for gas-fired; 70–80% for solid fuel).
>
> - **Pinch Analysis** is the *most powerful* energy conservation technique — gives minimum heating and cooling utility targets.
>
> - **Plate Heat Exchangers with Regeneration** can save 70–90% of steam in pasteurization.
>
> - **Multi-Effect Evaporators (MEE)** reduce steam consumption in sugar/juice industry by 50–75% per effect added.
>
> - **Boiler Flue Gas Loss** = 8–25% of fuel energy. Recover with **economizer**.
>
> - **VFDs on pumps and fans** save 20–50% electrical energy.
>
> - **Second-Law Efficiency** = $COP_{actual}/COP_{Carnot}$. Typical refrigeration: 40–60%.
>
> - **Benchmark SEC values to memorize:** Milk pasteurization ~0.1–0.3 MJ/kg; Spray-dried milk powder ~3–5 MJ/kg; Cold storage ~0.8–1.5 kWh/tonne·day.
>
> - **Always convert °C to K** when dealing with Carnot equations or any thermodynamic efficiency involving temperature ratios.
>
> - **Always specify wet basis vs dry basis** in any drying or moisture-related calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies Highlighting Successful Energy Management Practices in Food Industry
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies Highlighting Successful Energy Management Practices in Food Industry

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Energy Management in the Food Industry** is the systematic, data-driven process of planning, operating, monitoring, and optimizing the acquisition, conversion, distribution, and utilization of energy (thermal, electrical, mechanical, and renewable) across all unit operations of a food processing facility — from raw material reception to finished product dispatch — to achieve the lowest possible **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** while maintaining product quality, safety, and regulatory compliance.

In the context of **Module 1** of the KTU 2024 syllabus, *case studies* are real-world documented implementations of energy management systems (EnMS) — aligned with **ISO 50001** — that demonstrate measurable improvements in energy performance indicators (**EnPIs**) in food sector plants such as dairy, bakery, brewery, sugar, edible oil, and cold-chain facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (PEFTT752, Module 1):** Case studies are not just descriptive stories. In the KTU valuation scheme, they are evidence-based reports evaluated on (a) identification of baseline energy use, (b) the intervention technology adopted, (c) quantitative savings (kWh/tonne, % SEC reduction), and (d) payback economics.

## 1.2 Key Performance Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** | Energy used per unit of finished product | GJ/tonne, kWh/kg |
| **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** | Quantifiable metric reflecting energy performance | kWh, GJ, % |
| **EnB (Energy Baseline)** | Reference quantity that serves as benchmark | kWh/year |
| **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** | Process to confirm savings using IPMVP protocol | % accuracy |
| **ktoe** | Kilotonnes of oil equivalent (1 ktoe = 41.868 GJ) | toe |
| **MEDA** | Major Energy Data Analysis | — |
| **ESCO** | Energy Service Company | — |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Household Kitchen vs. Food Factory"**
> Imagine your home kitchen as a micro-food factory. You notice your LPG cylinder lasts only 10 days instead of 15. You then:
> 1. **Measure** (baseline) — 1 cylinder / 10 days.
> 2. **Diagnose** — a leaking burner, an inefficient pressure cooker, and over-boiling water.
> 3. **Intervene** — fix the leak, switch to induction, use a covered vessel.
> 4. **Verify** (post-retrofit) — 1 cylinder / 18 days.
>
> The **% savings = (10 − 18)/10 × 100 = −80%? No, (10−18/10) → saving of 8 days → ~44% reduction**.
> That is precisely how an **Energy Audit + Implementation + M&V** cycle works in a dairy, brewery, or sugar factory. The "leak" may be steam trap failure, oversized motors, lack of condensate recovery, or a neglected VFD on a chilled-water pump.

### 1.4 Why Case Studies Matter in the Food Sector

The **food industry is the second-largest energy consumer** in India's organized manufacturing sector (after textiles) and accounts for roughly **8–10% of total industrial energy demand**. Energy typically represents **5–15% of the production cost** in food plants, making it a critical profit lever. Successful case studies provide:

- **Replicable models** (proven, not theoretical).
- **Quantified ROI** (payback < 3 years is the KTU benchmark for "successful").
- **Technology mapping** (heat pumps, VFDs, cogeneration, CHP, pinch analysis).
- **Policy alignment** (PAT Scheme, Perform-Achieve-Trade, BEE ratings, ISO 50001).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a "Successful" Energy Management Case Study (KTU Lens)

A KTU 2024 Scheme examiner expects a case study answer to follow this **5-element logical chain**:

1. **Plant Profile** — sector, capacity, products, geographical location.
2. **Baseline Energy Audit (BEA)** — fuel mix (coal, diesel, biomass, electricity, steam), annual energy bill, identification of major energy-consuming equipment.
3. **Energy-Saving Interventions (ESIs)** — technology, process, behavioural, or fuel-substitution measures.
4. **Quantified Results** — % SEC reduction, kWh/tonne improvement, CO₂ avoided, payback in months/years.
5. **M&V and Continual Improvement** — IPMVP option applied, ISO 50001 EnMS loop, or PDCA cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 50001 — The Backbone of Every Modern Case Study**
> All post-2015 documented success stories in food plants follow the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle. The EnB is fixed in the "Plan" stage, EnPIs are tracked in "Do," deviations are analyzed in "Check," and corrective actions close the loop in "Act." Always mention this when a question asks about the "framework" of a case.

## 2.2 Major Energy-Consuming Equipment in Food Plants (Cross-Sector)

| Process Sector | Top 3 Energy Hogs | Typical Energy Carrier |
|---|---|---|
| **Dairy** | Boiler, refrigeration compressor, evaporator | Steam + electricity |
| **Bakery** | Tunnel oven, proofing chamber, mixers | LPG + electricity |
| **Brewery** | Boiling kettle, mash tun, CIP, refrigeration | Steam + electricity |
| **Sugar** | Mill drives, boiling house, evaporators | Bagasse + electricity |
| **Edible Oil** | Refinery deodorizer, boiler, screw press | Steam + electricity |
| **Cold Storage** | Compressors (NH₃/CO₂), condensers | Electricity |
| **Fruit/Veg Processing** | Blancher, dryer, IQF, retort | Steam + electricity |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | GJ/tonne, kWh/kg |
| 2 | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{SEC_{base} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{base}} \times 100$ | Percentage improvement in SEC | % |
| 3 | $E_{thermal} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load | kJ or kWh |
| 4 | $E_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat load (boiling, drying) | kJ |
| 5 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam}(h_{s}-h_{fw})}{m_{fuel}\cdot GCV}$ | Boiler efficiency | % |
| 6 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{heat}}{W_{input}}$ | Heat pump coefficient of performance | dimensionless |
| 7 | $PBP = \dfrac{CapEx}{Annual\ Savings}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 8 | $CO_{2e} = E_{saved} \times EF$ | CO₂-equivalent emissions avoided | kg CO₂e |
| 9 | $\eta_{cogen} = \dfrac{W_{elec}+Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Cogeneration efficiency | % |
| 10 | $NPSV = \sum \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} - CapEx$ | Net Present Savings (IPMVP) | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for Table Formatting:** In the KTU Formula Sheet, vertical bars `|x|` are rendered as `\vert x \vert` to preserve the markdown table. The **Standard Emission Factor (EF)** for the Indian grid (CEA 2022 baseline) is **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** — examiners award bonus marks when this constant is cited.

## 2.4 Theoretical Framework — Why Some Plants Succeed and Others Fail

Drawing from published case studies (TERI–BEE, UNIDO, IFC, CII, ISO 50001 case archives), the success of an energy management intervention is governed by the **5-M model**:

- **Management commitment** (top-down ISO 50001 policy + Energy Manager appointment).
- **Measurement infrastructure** (sub-metering, smart sensors, EMS software).
- **Methodology** (Walk-through → Detailed audit → Investment-grade audit).
- **Money** (ESCO financing, PAT white certificates, capital subsidies).
- **Mindset** (workforce training, behavioural programs, Kaizen/5S energy cells).

A case study "fails" in KTU examination parlance when it lacks any one of the first three "M"s — particularly the **quantified baseline** against which savings are claimed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Reduction Proof

The **SEC** is the most cited metric in food-industry case studies. We will derive the % improvement step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define baseline SEC.**

Let the plant produce $M_{1}$ tonnes of finished product annually using $E_{1}$ GJ of total energy (thermal + electrical converted to primary energy using 1 kWh = 0.0036 GJ).

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{E_{1}}{M_{1}} \quad [\text{GJ/tonne}]$$

**Step 2 — Define post-retrofit SEC.**

After the intervention (e.g., VFDs on pumps, heat recovery, LED lighting, boiler automation), the same output $M_{2} = M_{1}$ is produced using $E_{2}$ GJ where $E_{2} < E_{1}$.

$$SEC_{new} = \frac{E_{2}}{M_{2}} = \frac{E_{2}}{M_{1}} \quad [\text{GJ/tonne}]$$

**Step 3 — Express absolute energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} \quad [\text{GJ/year}]$$

**Step 4 — Express percentage savings.**

This is the form demanded in KTU 14-mark case-study answers.

$$\%\text{Savings} = \frac{SEC_{base} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{base}} \times 100 = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{1}} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent monetary savings.**

If the weighted average energy cost is $C$ ₹/GJ, then:

$$Annual\ Savings\ (₹) = \Delta E \times C$$

**Step 6 — CO₂ avoided (emission-reduction proof).**

$$CO_{2e}\ avoided = \Delta E_{electrical} \times 0.716 \quad [\text{kg CO}_2]$$

(where $\Delta E_{electrical}$ is the **electrical** portion saved in kWh, since 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh applies only to grid electricity, not to bagasse or biomass combustion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Kerala Dairy Plant — Module 1, Typical Case):**
> A dairy in Thrissur producing 50,000 tonnes/year of milk powder consumed 250,000 GJ of energy. After installing a **condensate recovery system**, **VFDs on chilled-water pumps**, and **LED lighting**, the consumption dropped to 215,000 GJ.
>
> $SEC_{base} = 250{,}000 / 50{,}000 = 5.00$ GJ/tonne
> $SEC_{new} = 215{,}000 / 50{,}000 = 4.30$ GJ/tonne
> $\%\text{Savings} = (5.00 - 4.30)/5.00 \times 100 = 14\%$
> Annual energy saved = 35,000 GJ
> At ₹800/GJ blended cost → **₹2.8 crore/year savings**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simple Payback Period (SPP) and Return on Investment

The **Payback Period** determines whether a case study is "successful" in KTU judgment.

**Step 1 — Capital expenditure aggregation.**

$$CapEx = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Equipment_i + Installation_i + Commissioning_i)$$

**Step 2 — Annual cash inflow from savings.**

$$CF = \Delta E \times C_{energy} - O\&M_{new}$$

(where $O\&M_{new}$ is the incremental operating and maintenance cost of the new system).

**Step 3 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$SPP = \frac{CapEx}{CF} \quad [\text{years}]$$

**Step 4 — KTU benchmark for "Successful" Case Study.**

$$SPP \le 3\ \text{years} \Rightarrow \text{Successful}$$

**Step 5 — ROI extension (Net Present Savings, IPMVP Option B).**

For multi-year projects, the discount-corrected metric is:

$$NPSV = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}} - CapEx$$

A project is **bankable** when $NPSV > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical — Brewery Case:**
> A brewery in Karnataka invested ₹4.2 crore in:
> - Vapour recompression on the boiling kettle (₹2.0 Cr)
> - CHP from biogas (₹1.5 Cr)
> - Heat recovery from wort boiling (₹0.7 Cr)
>
> Annual savings = ₹1.7 Cr, O&M = ₹0.2 Cr → Net CF = ₹1.5 Cr.
> $SPP = 4.2 / 1.5 = 2.8$ years → **SUCCESSFUL under KTU benchmark**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boiler Efficiency Improvement (Thermal Case)

Many food-plant case studies pivot on boiler performance.

**Step 1 — Direct efficiency (input-output method).**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{m_{s}(h_{s}-h_{fw})}{m_{f}\cdot GCV_{f}} \times 100$$

where:
- $m_{s}$ = mass of steam generated (kg/hr)
- $h_{s}$ = enthalpy of steam (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg)
- $m_{f}$ = mass of fuel (kg/hr)
- $GCV_{f}$ = gross calorific value of fuel (kJ/kg)

**Step 2 — Heat-loss method (cross-check, BEE protocol).**

$$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - (L_{flue\ gas} + L_{rad} + L_{moisture})$$

**Step 3 — Improvement via economizer.**

An **economizer** recovers part of the flue-gas sensible heat to preheat the feed water. If the feed-water temperature rises from 30 °C to 90 °C:

$$\Delta h_{fw} = 4.18 \times (90 - 30) = 251\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

Fuel saving (assuming 1 kg coal = 25,000 kJ GCV):

$$S_{fuel} = \frac{251}{25{,}000} \times 100 \approx 1.0\%\ \text{per 60 °C rise}$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative effect.**

A well-tuned boiler + economizer + automatic combustion control + blow-down heat recovery can push efficiency from **65% → 82%**, a 17-point absolute gain — the single largest EnPI in most dairy, sugar, and brewery case studies.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Five Canonical Food-Industry Case Studies (KTU Module 1 Anchor)

| # | Sector | Intervention (What they did) | Baseline SEC | New SEC | % Savings | Payback (yrs) | CO₂ avoided |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dairy (Cooperative, Gujarat)** | VFDs on refrigeration, condensate recovery, LED, boiler automation | 5.0 GJ/tonne milk powder | 4.3 GJ/tonne | **14%** | 2.1 | 8,500 tCO₂/yr |
| 2 | **Brewery (Karnataka)** | MVR on boiling kettle, biogas CHP, wort heat recovery | 0.50 kWh/L beer | 0.36 kWh/L | **28%** | 2.8 | 4,200 tCO₂/yr |
| 3 | **Sugar (Maharashtra)** | High-pressure cogeneration, vapour bleeding, cooling tower VFD | 6.0 GJ/tonne sugar | 4.2 GJ/tonne | **30%** | 3.0 | 18,000 tCO₂/yr |
| 4 | **Bakery (Industrial, Punjab)** | Tunnel-oven heat recovery, IR-radiant baking, demand-controlled ventilation | 2,800 kWh/tonne bread | 2,200 kWh/tonne | **21%** | 2.4 | 950 tCO₂/yr |
| 5 | **Edible Oil Refinery (Andhra)** | Deodorizer heat recovery, distillation column VFD, thermic fluid heater tuning | 1.8 GJ/tonne oil | 1.35 GJ/tonne | **25%** | 2.6 | 3,400 tCO₂/yr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Generic Energy Management Case-Study Roadmap (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Plant Profile and Scope] --> B[StepB: Baseline Energy Audit BEA]
    B --> C[StepC: Identify Energy Hogs and Losses]
    C --> D[StepD: Techno-Economic Feasibility]
    D --> E[StepE: Select Energy Saving Interventions]
    E --> F[StepF: Implementation Phase]
    F --> G[StepG: Measurement and Verification M and V]
    G --> H{EnPIs Improved?}
    H -- Yes --> I[StepI: Document and Replicate as Case Study]
    H -- No --> J[StepJ: Corrective Action PDCA Act]
    J --> D
    I --> K[StepK: ISO 50001 Continual Improvement Loop]
    K --> A
```

**How to read the diagram (KTU interpretation):**
- Nodes **StepA** to **StepE** mirror the **Plan** phase of PDCA.
- **StepF** is the **Do** phase.
- **StepG–StepJ** is the **Check–Act** phase.
- **StepK** is the **continual improvement** loop that makes a case study "successful" in the long term.

## 4.2 Sector-Specific Intervention Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DairyCluster
        D1[Boiler Steam Optimization]
        D2[Chilled Water VFD]
        D3[Condensate Recovery]
        D4[Milk Pasteurizer Heat Recovery]
    end
    subgraph BreweryCluster
        BR1[Mash Tun Steam Cut]
        BR2[MVR on Wort Boil]
        BR3[Biogas CHP]
        BR4[CIP Optimization]
    end
    subgraph SugarCluster
        S1[Bagasse Cogeneration]
        S2[Multi Effect Evaporator]
        S3[Vapour Bleeding to Pan]
        S4[Mill Drive VFD]
    end
    subgraph BakeryCluster
        BK1[Tunnel Oven IR Burner]
        BK2[Heat Recovery Wheel]
        BK3[Demand Controlled Vent]
        BK4[Proofing Chamber Insulation]
    end
    OutputNode((Common EnPIs: SEC, Payback, CO2 Avoided))
    D1 --> OutputNode
    D2 --> OutputNode
    D3 --> OutputNode
    D4 --> OutputNode
    BR1 --> OutputNode
    BR2 --> OutputNode
    BR3 --> OutputNode
    BR4 --> OutputNode
    S1 --> OutputNode
    S2 --> OutputNode
    S3 --> OutputNode
    S4 --> OutputNode
    BK1 --> OutputNode
    BK2 --> OutputNode
    BK3 --> OutputNode
    BK4 --> OutputNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Sector Map:** Each cluster represents a *technology bundle* that, when deployed together, delivers the highest SEC reduction. KTU examiners award extra credit to answers that identify **bundled** (not isolated) interventions — for example, "boiler + condensate + VFD" is a *bundle* in the dairy cluster.

## 4.3 ISO 50001 PDCA Loop as Applied to a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plan: Energy Review and EnB] --> P2[Plan: Energy Objectives and EnPIs]
    P2 --> P3[Plan: Action Plan]
    P3 --> D1[Do: Implement Interventions]
    D1 --> D2[Do: Operate and Maintain]
    D2 --> C1[Check: Monitor and Measure]
    C1 --> C2[Check: Analyze Energy Performance]
    C2 --> A1[Act: Nonconformity Correction]
    A1 --> A2[Act: Management Review and Continual Improvement]
    A2 --> P1
```

This PDCA loop is the **theoretical skeleton** that should be visible (implicitly) in any case study answer in the 14-mark section.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]
**Define "Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)" and state its units as used in food industry energy management.**

**Model Answer:**
SEC is the amount of energy consumed per unit of finished product, calculated as:

$$SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$$

Typical units are **GJ/tonne, kWh/kg, or MJ/kg**. In Indian food-industry case studies, **GJ/tonne** is the most common unit because it accommodates both thermal and electrical energy on a common scale (using 1 kWh = 0.0036 GJ). SEC is the most widely tracked **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** under ISO 50001.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for unit/example.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]
**What is an "Energy Service Company (ESCO)" and how does it enable energy management success in food industries?**

**Model Answer:**
An **ESCO** is a specialized firm that finances, implements, and guarantees energy-saving projects, recovering its investment from a share of the verified savings (often via Energy Performance Contracts — EPCs). In food industries — particularly SMEs in bakery, dairy, and cold storage — ESCOs remove the upfront capital barrier, perform M&V using IPMVP, and assume performance risk. This model has enabled several documented case-study successes, especially in Kerala's dairy and ice-cream cooperatives.

*(3 marks: 1 for ESCO definition, 1 for EPC mechanism, 1 for relevance to food SMEs.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **No baseline = 0 marks** for "results." Always quote a baseline SEC or kWh before and after.
> 2. **No payback calculation = −3 marks.** Always compute $SPP = CapEx / Annual\ Savings$.
> 3. **Listing technologies without sectors = −2 marks.** Tie every intervention to a specific food sector.
> 4. **Ignoring ISO 50001 PDCA** in a 14-mark answer costs 2–3 marks in 2024 scheme valuations.
> 5. **Using the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables** breaks formatting — use `\vert` instead.

### Question A — 14 Marks (Dairy Sector Focus)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** *With a suitable case study, explain how energy management was successfully implemented in an Indian dairy processing plant. Discuss the baseline audit, interventions, and results.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plant Profile.** A cooperative dairy plant in Gujarat processing **50,000 tonnes/year of milk powder** with 12 MW connected load and a 10 TPH coal-fired boiler.

**Step 2 — Baseline Energy Audit (BEA).** Annual energy mix: 60% thermal (coal), 35% electrical, 5% diesel. Total energy use = 250,000 GJ → $SEC_{base} = 5.0$ GJ/tonne. Key losses identified: blow-down heat (15%), inefficient refrigeration, oversized chilled-water pumps, no condensate recovery.

**Step 3 — Interventions Implemented.**
- **Condensate recovery system** to flash tank → boiler feed.
- **VFDs on chilled-water pumps** (60 kW × 3).
- **LED lighting** across 8,000 m² floor.
- **Boiler combustion control upgrade** + O₂ trim.
- **Heat recovery from pasteurizer** to preheat raw milk.

**Step 4 — Quantified Results.**
- Coal consumption reduced from 7,500 to 6,300 T/year.
- Grid electricity reduced by 1.2 MU/year.
- $SEC_{new} = 4.3$ GJ/tonne → **14% SEC reduction**.
- Annual monetary savings: **₹2.8 crore** at blended ₹800/GJ.
- CapEx: ₹5.9 crore → $SPP = 5.9/2.8 = 2.1$ years.
- CO₂ avoided: ~8,500 tCO₂/yr (using 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh).

**Step 5 — M&V Framework.** IPMVP Option B (retrofitted isolation) with sub-meter verification on pumps and boiler.

---

**(b)** *Compute the percentage reduction in SEC and the simple payback period for a dairy plant where steam condensate recovery saved 6,000 GJ/yr (CapEx ₹1.2 Cr), VFDs saved 1,500 GJ/yr (CapEx ₹80 L), and LED lighting saved 400 GJ/yr (CapEx ₹40 L). Assume blended energy cost = ₹850/GJ.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = 6{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 400 = 7{,}900\ \text{GJ/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Total CapEx.**

$$CapEx = 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.40 = 2.40\ \text{Crore} = 2.40 \times 10^{7}\ \text{₹}$$

**Step 3 — Annual monetary savings.**

$$CF = 7{,}900 \times 850 = 6{,}715{,}000\ \text{₹/yr} = 0.6715\ \text{Crore}$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$SPP = \frac{2.40}{0.6715} = 3.57\ \text{years}$$

**Step 5 — SEC reduction** (assuming original $SEC_{base} = 5.0$ GJ/tonne and $M = 50{,}000$ tonnes/yr, so $E_{base} = 250{,}000$ GJ/yr).

$$\%\text{Savings} = \frac{7{,}900}{250{,}000} \times 100 = 3.16\%$$

**Step 6 — Verdict.** While the percentage is modest (3.16%), the **absolute saving of 7,900 GJ/yr** is significant. The payback (3.57 years) is *just above* the KTU "successful" benchmark of ≤ 3 years — so the case would be classified as **"marginally successful, with scope for further intervention."**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Brewery / Sugar Sector — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** *Discuss with a suitable case study how cogeneration of bagasse (or biogas) has been successfully used in the food industry to achieve energy self-sufficiency. Highlight the EnPIs.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plant Profile.** A 5,000 TCD sugar mill in Maharashtra operating 160 days/year with a **30 MW high-pressure boiler** running on bagasse.

**Step 2 — Baseline.** Sugar mills historically used 22–25 kg of bagasse/tonne cane to meet process heat. Surplus bagasse was wasted. $SEC_{base} = 6.0$ GJ/tonne sugar; grid import = 8 MU/year.

**Step 3 — Intervention.** Installation of a **30 MW high-pressure (87 bar) cogeneration system** with a back-pressure/condensing turbine. Vapour bleeding at 2.5, 5, and 10 bar feeds the evaporators, pan, and centrifugal stations.

**Step 4 — Results.**
- Electrical export: **22 MU/year** to the grid at ₹4.5/kWh.
- Process heat: fully met.
- $SEC_{new} = 4.2$ GJ/tonne → **30% SEC reduction**.
- Annual revenue: ₹99 Cr (electricity export) + ₹12 Cr (fuel saving) = ₹111 Cr.
- CapEx: ₹330 Cr → $SPP = 2.97$ years.
- CO₂ avoided: ~18,000 tCO₂/yr (grid displacement).

**Step 5 — EnPIs Tracked.** kWh exported/tonne cane, kg bagasse/tonne sugar, steam-to-cane ratio, % cogen efficiency (achieved **74%** vs. conventional 52%).

---

**(b)** *A brewery's baseline SEC is 0.50 kWh/L beer. After installing MVR (mechanical vapour recompression) on the wort boiler and CHP from biogas, the new SEC is 0.36 kWh/L beer. Annual production = 100 million litres. Compute (i) the annual energy saved in kWh, (ii) the percentage reduction in SEC, and (iii) the CO₂ emissions avoided. Also, if the CapEx is ₹4.2 Cr and energy cost is ₹7/kWh, compute the payback period.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = (SEC_{base} - SEC_{new}) \times M = (0.50 - 0.36) \times 10^{8}\ \text{L} = 1.4 \times 10^{7}\ \text{kWh/yr} = 14\ \text{MU/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Percentage reduction.**

$$\%\text{Savings} = \frac{0.50 - 0.36}{0.50} \times 100 = 28\%$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ avoided** (using $EF = 0.716$ kg CO₂/kWh).

$$CO_{2e} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 0.716 = 1.0024 \times 10^{7}\ \text{kg} = 10{,}024\ \text{tCO}_2/\text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings.**

$$CF = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 7 = 9.8\ \text{Crore/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$SPP = \frac{4.2}{9.8} = 0.43\ \text{years} \approx 5.2\ \text{months}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict.** Highly successful: 28% SEC reduction, 10,024 tCO₂/yr avoided, payback < 6 months — well within KTU's ≤ 3-year benchmark. (Bonus: 14 MU/yr savings offsets the plant's entire lighting + utility load.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Case Studies)**

- ☐ **Case study = 5 elements:** Plant profile → Baseline audit → Intervention → Quantified result → M&V loop.
- ☐ **SEC unit** to remember: **GJ/tonne** (thermal+electrical) or **kWh/kg** (electrical only).
- ☐ **% Savings formula:** $\%\text{Savings} = (SEC_{base} - SEC_{new})/SEC_{base} \times 100$.
- ☐ **SPP formula:** $SPP = CapEx / Annual\ Savings$; KTU "success" benchmark = **≤ 3 years**.
- ☐ **Grid emission factor (India, CEA 2022):** **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** — write this constant in the exam.
- ☐ **Conversion:** 1 kWh = 0.0036 GJ; 1 toe = 41.868 GJ; 1 ktoe = 41.868 TJ.
- ☐ **Boiler efficiency improvements** of 65% → 82% are the single largest EnPI in dairy/sugar/brewery cases.
- ☐ **Cogeneration (CHP)** is the defining technology for sugar mills and large breweries — target efficiency **70–80%**.
- ☐ **Heat pumps / MVR** are the "trending" interventions cited in modern brewery and dairy case studies.
- ☐ **Pinch analysis** is a methodology — not a technology — frequently referenced in brewery and oil-refining cases.
- ☐ **ISO 50001 PDCA** is the default framework; **IPMVP Option B** is the default M&V method.
- ☐ **ESCOs** resolve the capital barrier for food SMEs — always mention for 1–2 bonus marks.
- ☐ **Behavioural / Kaizen / 5S energy cells** add an additional **3–5% savings** with negligible CapEx — mention these.
- ☐ **Kerala-relevant sectors:** Coconut processing, spice processing, seafood/cold-chain, dairy cooperatives — local context gets valuation credit.
- ☐ **Avoid** in answers: vague phrases like "energy was saved" — always quantify in **GJ, kWh, ₹, or tCO₂**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Energy: Solar PV Systems: Components, Sizing, and Installation Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Energy: Solar PV Systems: Components, Sizing, and Installation Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an engineered assembly of solid-state semiconductor devices that directly convert incident solar irradiance into direct current (DC) electrical energy via the **photovoltaic effect**, discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839 and first demonstrated in practical silicon cells at Bell Laboratories in 1954. In the context of the food industry, solar PV systems are increasingly deployed for **on-site renewable power generation**, **cold-chain refrigeration**, **solar drying of agricultural produce**, and **water pumping for irrigation and processing**, thereby reducing dependence on grid electricity and diesel-based captive power.

A complete grid-tied or off-grid PV installation comprises the **PV array** (modules + mounting), **power conditioning units** (inverters, charge controllers, MPPT units), **energy storage subsystem** (battery bank), and the **Balance of System (BOS)** — cabling, protection devices, earthing, and monitoring instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEFTT752, Module 2):**
> The student must be able to (i) identify and describe each PV system component, (ii) perform a sizing calculation for a given electrical load, and (iii) justify the choice of tilt angle, orientation, and mounting based on geographical latitude and load profile.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a solar PV module as a **"solar sandwich"** — but instead of cheese and tomato, it has ultra-thin layers of specially treated silicon (a semiconductor). When sunlight (photons) hits the top layer, it knocks loose electrons from the silicon atoms. The internal structure of the cell — a **p-n junction** — acts like a one-way valve that funnels these freed electrons out through metal contacts, creating a flow of direct current.

**A simple analogy for the whole system:**

Imagine a **rainwater harvesting system**:
- The **roof** of a house = the **solar PV array** (catches the resource — sunlight instead of rain).
- The **gutter and pipe network** = the **wiring and DC cables** (channels the energy).
- The **storage tank** = the **battery bank** (stores it for cloudy days/night).
- The **water purifier and tap** = the **inverter and load** (converts raw DC to usable AC for appliances).

Just as you must size your roof and tank correctly to harvest enough rain for a family, you must size your PV array and battery bank to match the food industry's daily energy demand (e.g., a cold-storage load of 5 kW running 16 hours).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants and standard test metrics are essential for KTU numerical problems:

- **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance $G_{STC} = 1000 \, \text{W/m}^2$, Cell temperature $T_{STC} = 25^\circ \text{C}$ (i.e., 298.15 K), Air Mass **AM 1.5**
- **Solar constant (extra-terrestrial irradiance):** $I_{SC} \approx 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$
- **Charge of an electron:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Band gap of crystalline silicon:** $E_g \approx 1.12 \, \text{eV}$
- **Average daily peak sun hours (PSH)** for Kerala: $\approx 4.5$ to $5.0 \, \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Peak Sun Hours (PSH):** The equivalent number of hours per day during which solar irradiance is assumed to be a constant $1000 \, \text{W/m}^2$. For example, Kerala receiving $5 \, \text{kWh/m}^2$ of daily irradiation is said to have $\text{PSH} = 5 \, \text{h/day}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photovoltaic Effect — Operational Logic

The PV effect is a quantum mechanical phenomenon that occurs in **p-n junction diodes** made of semiconductor materials (predominantly crystalline silicon — c-Si).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Photon absorption:** A photon with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes the silicon crystal. If $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ (the band-gap energy), it is absorbed and generates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.
2. **Charge separation:** The built-in electric field at the p-n junction sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side.
3. **Current extraction:** A conductive metal grid on the top surface collects the electrons, which flow through the external load and return to the p-side, producing useful **DC electrical power**.
4. **Recombination loss:** Any EHP that recombines before being separated is lost as heat — this is the primary mechanism limiting **quantum efficiency** (typically 15–22% for commercial c-Si cells).

The **I-V characteristic** of a PV cell is governed by the **single-diode model** (the foundational equation for all PV engineering analysis):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:

- $I$ = output current of the cell (A)
- $V$ = terminal voltage (V)
- $I_{ph}$ = photo-generated current (A), proportional to irradiance $G$
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 to 2 for c-Si)
- $V_T = \dfrac{k_B T}{q}$ = thermal voltage (≈ 25.85 mV at 25 °C)
- $R_s$ = series resistance (Ω) — losses in contacts and bulk
- $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance (Ω) — leakage across the p-n junction

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Parameters of a PV Module:**
> - **Short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$):** current when $V = 0$.
> - **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$):** voltage when $I = 0$.
> - **Maximum Power Point ($P_{mpp} = V_{mpp} \times I_{mpp}$):** the operating point that delivers peak power.
> - **Fill Factor (FF):** $\text{FF} = \dfrac{V_{mpp} I_{mpp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ (typically 0.6–0.85 for good modules).
> - **Conversion efficiency ($\eta$):** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mpp}}{G \times A}$, where $A$ is the module area.

## 2.2 Components of a Solar PV System — Functional Classification

A complete PV plant is divided into **four functional blocks**:

### Block A — Energy Capture Subsystem
| Component | Function | Engineering Specification |
|-----------|----------|---------------------------|
| **PV Module** | Converts sunlight → DC electricity | c-Si / poly-Si / thin-film; $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{max}$ at STC |
| **Mounting Structure** | Holds modules at tilt/azimuth | Galvanized iron / aluminum; wind-load rated to **150–180 km/h** |
| **Module-level electronics (MLPE)** | Per-panel MPPT / rapid shutdown | DC optimizers, micro-inverters |

### Block B — Power Conditioning Subsystem
| Component | Function | Key Specification |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Charge Controller (off-grid only)** | Regulates battery charging; prevents over-charge / deep discharge | PWM or MPPT type; ratings in A and V |
| **Inverter** | Converts DC → AC (and grid-sync for grid-tied) | Pure sine wave; efficiency 95–98%; THD $<$ 3% |
| **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** | Continuously adjusts operating voltage to extract peak power | Perturb & Observe (P&O) or Incremental Conductance algorithm |

### Block C — Energy Storage Subsystem (off-grid / hybrid)
| Component | Function | Typical for Food Industry |
|-----------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Battery bank** | Stores DC energy for night / cloudy use | Lead-acid (VRLA / tubular) or **Li-ion (LFP)**; 48 V or 96 V strings |
| **Battery Management System (BMS)** | Cell balancing, SOC/SOH monitoring, protection | Critical for cold-chain reliability |

### Block D — Balance of System (BOS)
| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **DC & AC cables** | Power transmission with minimal voltage drop (target $<$ 2%) |
| **String combiner box** | Combines parallel module strings; fuses / DC disconnects |
| **AC distribution board (DB)** | Routes AC to loads with MCB + ELCB protection |
| **Earthing & lightning arrestor** | Safety; typically $<$ 5 Ω earth resistance |
| **Energy meter (net-meter)** | Measures export/import in grid-tied systems |
| **SCADA / monitoring** | Logs generation, performance ratio, fault alarms |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{array} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}}$ | Required PV array energy output (accounting for system losses) | kWh/day |
| 2 | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\text{PSH} \times \eta_{sys}}$ | PV array peak power rating | kWp |
| 3 | $N_{modules} = \dfrac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}}$ | Number of PV modules in the array | integer |
| 4 | $N_{series} = \dfrac{V_{DC,bus}}{V_{mp,module}}$ | Modules in series per string | integer |
| 5 | $N_{parallel} = \dfrac{I_{DC,total}}{I_{mp,module}}$ | Strings in parallel | integer |
| 6 | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \times D_{aut}}{\eta_{bat} \times V_{bat} \times DoD}$ | Battery bank capacity | Ah |
| 7 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{cable} \times \eta_{bat} \times \eta_{temp}$ | Overall system efficiency (typically 0.65–0.80) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{temp} = 1 - \gamma \left( T_{cell} - 25 \right)$ | Temperature derating factor; $\gamma \approx 0.004$–$0.005$ /°C for c-Si | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{cell} = T_{amb} + \left( \dfrac{\text{NOCT} - 20}{0.8} \right) \times G$ | Cell temperature from ambient | °C |
| 10 | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{theoretical}}$ | Performance Ratio (good system $>$ 0.75) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\beta_{opt} = \phi - 15^\circ$ (Kerala) | Optimum tilt angle approximation | degrees |
| 12 | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \times L \times I \times \rho}{A}$ | Cable voltage drop (single-phase, two-way) | V |

Where:
- $\text{PSH}$ = Peak Sun Hours
- $D_{aut}$ = Days of autonomy (typically 1–3 for hybrid systems)
- $DoD$ = Depth of Discharge (0.5 for lead-acid, 0.8 for LFP)
- $\text{NOCT}$ = Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (≈ 45 °C)
- $\phi$ = Latitude of the site (Kerala ≈ 8° to 12° N)
- $E_{load}$ = Daily energy consumption of the food unit

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry

The sizing formulas above are directly applied to the following food-industry scenarios:

1. **Solar-powered cold storage for fish/milk:** 5 kW refrigeration load × 16 h/day → 80 kWh/day requirement.
2. **Solar drying of spices/grains:** Off-grid 2 kW PV + battery powering blower + heater backup.
3. **Solar water pumping for irrigation:** Direct-drive submersible pump with MPPT-driven variable frequency drive (VFD).
4. **Net-metered rooftop PV on dairy processing plants:** Cuts grid bills via export to KSEB under the **Kerala Solar Energy Policy 2013** and **PM-KUSUM** framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PV Array Sizing for a Given Daily Load

**Problem statement (typical KTU 14-mark):** A food processing unit in Thrissur (latitude $\phi = 10.5^\circ$ N) operates a 3 kW refrigeration load for 10 hours/day. The system uses 330 Wp mono-crystalline modules, a 96% efficient inverter, 95% efficient charge controller, 90% battery round-trip efficiency, and operates at 80% depth of discharge with 3 days of autonomy. Kerala PSH = 5.0 h/day. Size the PV array and battery bank.

**Given data:**
- $P_{load} = 3 \, \text{kW}$
- $t_{op} = 10 \, \text{h/day}$
- $P_{module} = 330 \, \text{Wp}$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$
- $\eta_{charge} = 0.95$
- $\eta_{bat} = 0.90$
- $DoD = 0.80$
- $D_{aut} = 3$ days
- $V_{bus} = 48 \, \text{V}$ (assumed standard)
- $\text{PSH} = 5.0 \, \text{h/day}$

### Step 1 — Compute the daily energy demand

$$E_{load} = P_{load} \times t_{op}$$

$$E_{load} = 3 \, \text{kW} \times 10 \, \text{h/day} = 30 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

**Logic:** The total energy the refrigeration system draws from the storage/battery each day, assuming it runs steadily. This is the input to the entire sizing chain.

### Step 2 — Account for system efficiency

$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{charge} \times \eta_{bat}$$

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.96 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8208$$

**Logic:** Not all energy generated by the panels reaches the load. Roughly 18% is lost as heat in the inverter, charge controller, and battery round-trip. We compensate by oversizing the array.

### Step 3 — Compute required PV array energy

$$E_{array} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}}$$

$$E_{array} = \frac{30 \, \text{kWh/day}}{0.8208} = 36.55 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

**Logic:** This is the energy the PV array must produce *at the panel output* so that 30 kWh is delivered to the load.

### Step 4 — Compute PV array peak power

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{array}}{\text{PSH}}$$

$$P_{PV} = \frac{36.55 \, \text{kWh/day}}{5.0 \, \text{h/day}} = 7.31 \, \text{kWp}$$

**Logic:** Since one peak-sun-hour is defined as 1 kW/m² equivalent, dividing the required daily energy by PSH gives the array size.

### Step 5 — Number of PV modules

$$N_{modules} = \frac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}}$$

$$N_{modules} = \frac{7310 \, \text{Wp}}{330 \, \text{Wp}} = 22.15 \rightarrow 23 \, \text{modules (rounded up)}$$

**Logic:** Always round **up** because we cannot install a fractional module. Practical array configurations for a 48 V bus:

- **Series per string (typical 330 W module has $V_{mp} \approx 33.5$ V):** $N_s = 48/33.5 = 1.43 \rightarrow 2$ modules per string.
- **Number of parallel strings:** $N_p = 23/2 = 11.5 \rightarrow 12$ strings.

In practice, a designer may select a different bus voltage (e.g., 96 V or 120 V) to reduce cable current; the calculation logic remains identical.

### Step 6 — Battery bank sizing

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times D_{aut}}{\eta_{bat} \times V_{bat} \times DoD}$$

$$C_{bat} = \frac{30 \times 3}{0.90 \times 48 \times 0.80}$$

$$C_{bat} = \frac{90}{34.56} = 2.604 \, \text{kAh} = 2604 \, \text{Ah at 48 V}$$

**Logic:** With $DoD = 0.80$, we only use 80% of the battery's nameplate capacity to prolong cycle life. 3 days of autonomy means the battery must cover the load for 72 continuous hours without sun.

For practical implementation, this would be a **2 × 24 V (1500 Ah) string** in series for 48 V, or **4 × 12 V (1500 Ah) blocks** in series-parallel.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Derating of PV Output

**Step 1 — Compute cell temperature from ambient**

$$T_{cell} = T_{amb} + \left( \frac{\text{NOCT} - 20}{0.8} \right) \times G$$

$$T_{cell} = 35 + \left( \frac{45 - 20}{0.8} \right) \times 0.85$$

$$T_{cell} = 35 + 31.25 \times 0.85 = 35 + 26.56 = 61.56 \, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Logic:** NOCT (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature) is measured at $G = 800 \, \text{W/m}^2$ and $T_{amb} = 20^\circ\text{C}$. The factor 0.8 in the denominator scales the heating linearly with irradiance.

**Step 2 — Compute temperature derating factor**

$$\eta_{temp} = 1 - \gamma (T_{cell} - 25)$$

$$\eta_{temp} = 1 - 0.004 \times (61.56 - 25)$$

$$\eta_{temp} = 1 - 0.004 \times 36.56 = 1 - 0.1462 = 0.8538$$

**Logic:** For every 1 °C above STC (25 °C), a c-Si module loses approximately 0.4% of its rated power. At 61.56 °C, the loss is 14.6%.

**Step 3 — Revised PV array power**

$$P_{PV,revised} = \frac{P_{PV}}{\eta_{temp}}$$

$$P_{PV,revised} = \frac{7.31}{0.8538} = 8.56 \, \text{kWp}$$

**Logic:** In hot summer conditions in Kerala (April–May), the array must be oversized by 17% to compensate for thermal losses — this is a critical step that KTU examiners expect.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component Sizing Decision Table

| Load Profile | Recommended Topology | PV/Battery Ratio | Inverter Sizing |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Daytime only** (e.g., grain milling) | Grid-tied or direct-drive | No battery; 1.2 × load | $1.2 \times P_{load}$ |
| **Day + night** (cold storage 16 h) | Hybrid (PV + battery + grid) | 1.3–1.5 × daily E | $1.0 \times P_{load}$ |
| **24×7 critical** (dairy pasteurization) | Hybrid with grid backup | 1.8 × daily E; $D_{aut} = 3$ days | $1.0 \times P_{load}$ |
| **Remote water pumping** | Direct PV with VFD pump | $1.3 \times P_{pump}$ | VFD rated to pump |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete Grid-Tied Solar PV System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUN: Solar Irradiance G] --> B[PV Array: Modules in Series-Parallel]
    B --> C[String Combiner Box: Fuses, DC Disconnect]
    C --> D[DC Distribution: SPD, MCB]
    D --> E[Inverter: DC to AC with MPPT]
    E --> F[AC Distribution Board: MCB, ELCB]
    F --> G1[Food Industry Load 1: Cold Storage]
    F --> G2[Food Industry Load 2: Solar Dryer]
    F --> G3[Food Industry Load 3: Water Pump]
    F --> H[Net Meter: Bi-directional]
    H --> I[KSEB Grid: Export / Import]
    E --> J[SCADA Monitoring: PR, kWh, Faults]
    E --> K[Earthing and Lightning Protection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram shows a **grid-tied (net-metered)** topology typical of a rooftop installation on a dairy or rice mill in Kerala. The **battery and charge controller blocks are absent** — this is because grid-tied systems use the KSEB grid as a "virtual battery."

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Off-Grid Hybrid PV System for Food Cold-Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Solar PV Modules] --> B1[MPPT Charge Controller]
    B1 --> C1[Battery Bank: LFP 48 V]
    A1 --> D1[Hybrid Inverter: PV + Battery + Grid]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[AC Loads: Refrigeration, Lighting]
    D1 --> F1[Grid: KSEB Backup]
    D1 --> G1[Generator: Diesel Backup Optional]
    B1 --> H1[BMS: SOC, SOH, Cell Balancing]
    D1 --> I1[Monitoring: Cloud Telemetry]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solar modules feed the **MPPT charge controller** (regulates battery) **AND** feed the **hybrid inverter** (serves AC loads directly during sunshine). The battery is a parallel source that powers the inverter at night. The KSEB grid and diesel generator act as tertiary backups.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: PV System Sizing Methodology (KTU Logic Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Define Daily Load E_load in kWh] --> S2[Apply System Efficiency eta_sys]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Required Array Energy E_array]
    S3 --> S4[Divide by PSH to get P_PV in kWp]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Temperature Derating eta_temp]
    S5 --> S6[Determine N_modules = P_PV / P_module]
    S6 --> S7{Grid-Tied or Off-Grid?}
    S7 -- Grid-Tied --> S8[Select Inverter = 1.2 x P_PV]
    S7 -- Off-Grid --> S9[Compute Battery Capacity C_bat]
    S9 --> S10[Add Charge Controller and BMS]
    S8 --> S11[End: Final BoQ]
    S10 --> S11
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Marks are awarded for the **sequence of formulas**, not just the final answer. Follow the chain **Load → Efficiency → Array energy → Peak power → Module count → Battery** for full credit.

## 4.4 Tilt Angle and Orientation Visual Logic (Kerala-specific)

For Kerala (latitude $\phi = 8.3^\circ$ to $12.8^\circ$ N):

| Parameter | Optimum Value for Kerala | Reasoning |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Tilt angle ($\beta$)** | $\beta = \phi - 5^\circ$ to $\phi - 10^\circ \approx 3^\circ$ to $7^\circ$ | Low latitude → sun is nearly overhead most of the year |
| **Azimuth** | $0^\circ$ (true south) | Northern hemisphere convention |
| **Seasonal boost** | $\beta_{winter} = \phi + 15^\circ$ to maximize winter generation for winter-dry spices | Adjustable mounting preferred |
| **Ground clearance** | $\geq 0.5$ m to avoid shading by grass & splash | Reduces soiling losses |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List the main components of a solar PV system and state the function of the inverter.**

**Model Answer:**

A solar PV system consists of (i) **PV modules** (convert sunlight to DC), (ii) **mounting structure** (supports the array at the required tilt), (iii) **inverter** (converts DC to AC), (iv) **charge controller** (regulates battery charging in off-grid systems), (v) **battery bank** (stores electrical energy), and (vi) **BOS** — cabling, protection devices, earthing, and meters.

The **inverter** is a power-electronic device that converts the **DC output** of the PV array into **AC** at the grid frequency (50 Hz in India). It also performs MPPT, synchronizes with the grid (in grid-tied systems), and provides anti-islanding protection. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define "Peak Sun Hours" (PSH) and state its typical value for Kerala.**

**Model Answer:**

**Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** is defined as the equivalent number of hours per day during which the solar irradiance is constant at **1000 W/m²**, delivering the same total daily energy as the actual variable insolation.

$$\text{PSH} = \frac{\text{Daily global irradiation on tilted surface} \, [\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}]}{1 \, \text{kW/m}^2}$$

For Kerala, the typical daily global irradiation on a horizontal surface is **4.5 to 5.5 kWh/m²/day**, corresponding to **PSH ≈ 4.5 to 5.5 h/day**. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working of a **grid-tied solar PV system** with net metering. Identify the role of MPPT, anti-islanding, and the bidirectional meter. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A small-scale **solar drying unit** in Palakkad requires 1.5 kW of thermal-equivalent electrical power for the blower and heater-fan, operating 8 hours per day. The system uses:
- 250 Wp poly-crystalline modules
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.90$, $\eta_{charge} = 0.94$
- Battery: $V_{bat} = 48$ V, $DoD = 0.70$, $D_{aut} = 2$ days
- $\text{PSH} = 5.2$ h/day

**Compute (i) the daily energy requirement, (ii) the array size in kWp, (iii) the number of modules, and (iv) the battery capacity in Ah.** *(7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the **single-diode equivalent circuit model** of a PV cell. Derive the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ expression using the diode equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **installation considerations** for a rooftop solar PV system on a food processing unit in Kerala. Cover the following points: tilt angle selection, azimuth orientation, shading analysis, cable sizing, earthing, and lightning protection. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to Q.A (a) — Block Diagram & Working

A grid-tied solar PV system (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) operates as follows:

1. **PV array** generates DC electricity proportional to incident irradiance.
2. **MPPT algorithm** (typically Perturb & Observe) continuously adjusts the inverter's input voltage to keep the array operating at its maximum power point.
3. **Inverter** converts DC to 230 V / 50 Hz AC synchronized with the KSEB grid.
4. **Anti-islanding protection:** Inverter automatically disconnects within 0.1 to 2 seconds of a grid outage to prevent back-feeding power into a "dead" grid and endangering line workers.
5. **Net meter** records the **net energy** = (Energy exported to grid) − (Energy imported from grid). The consumer is billed only for the **net import** under the **net-metering tariff** of the Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for net-metering concept)*

### Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical Sizing

**Given:**
- $P_{load} = 1.5 \, \text{kW}$, $t_{op} = 8 \, \text{h/day}$
- $P_{module} = 250 \, \text{Wp}$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.90$, $\eta_{charge} = 0.94$
- $V_{bat} = 48$ V, $DoD = 0.70$, $D_{aut} = 2$ days
- $\text{PSH} = 5.2 \, \text{h/day}$

**Step (i) — Daily energy requirement**

$$E_{load} = 1.5 \times 8 = 12.0 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step (ii) — Array size in kWp**

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.94 = 0.8037$$

$$E_{array} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{12.0}{0.8037} = 14.93 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{array}}{\text{PSH}} = \frac{14.93}{5.2} = 2.871 \, \text{kWp}$$

**Step (iii) — Number of modules**

$$N_{modules} = \frac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}} = \frac{2871}{250} = 11.48 \rightarrow 12 \, \text{modules}$$

**Step (iv) — Battery capacity**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times D_{aut}}{\eta_{bat} \times V_{bat} \times DoD}$$

$$C_{bat} = \frac{12.0 \times 2}{0.90 \times 48 \times 0.70} = \frac{24.0}{30.24} = 0.7937 \, \text{kAh} = 793.7 \, \text{Ah at 48 V}$$

Practical implementation: 4 strings of 12 V / 200 Ah batteries in series for 48 V nominal.

*(7 marks — 1 each for steps i–iv, 3 for formula justification and units)*

## 5.4 Model Solution Outline for Question B

### Q.B (a) — Single Diode Model
The single-diode model represents the PV cell as a **current source** $I_{ph}$ (photo-generated) in parallel with a **diode** (representing the p-n junction), a **series resistance** $R_s$ (ohmic losses), and a **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage). Applying KCL at the output node:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

Substituting the Shockley diode equation $I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$ and $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ yields the **characteristic equation**. Setting $I = 0$ (open circuit) gives:

$$V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right) \approx n V_T \ln\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} \right)$$

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for equation, 2 for $V_{oc}$ derivation)*

### Q.B (b) — Installation Considerations
| Consideration | Engineering Recommendation for Kerala |
|---------------|---------------------------------------|
| Tilt angle | $\beta = \phi - 10^\circ \approx 0^\circ$ to $3^\circ$ (low-latitude, near-flat is fine); use $15^\circ$ for winter-dominant loads |
| Azimuth | True south (0°); up to ±15° east/west deviation acceptable with $<2\%$ loss |
| Shading | Use solar pathfinder or PVsyst; even 5% shading can cause 50% output drop due to bypass-diode activation |
| Cable sizing | Limit voltage drop to $\leq 2\%$; use the formula $A = \dfrac{2 L I \rho}{V_{drop}}$ with $\rho_{cu} = 0.0172 \, \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$ |
| Earthing | $\leq 5 \, \Omega$ earth resistance; use chemical/GI-pipe earth electrodes; one for DC, one for AC, one for lightning |
| Lightning arrestor | Class II SPD at the DC side and AC distribution; conventional Franklin rod with down-conductor for tall structures |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per point with one-line justification)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\eta_{sys}$ term** — Students often directly divide $E_{load}$ by PSH. You will lose 2 marks. Always show the system efficiency calculation explicitly.
> 2. **Rounding down module count** — Always round **up** ($N = 11.48 \rightarrow 12$), not to the nearest integer. A fractional module is not a product.
> 3. **Using $V_{oc}$ instead of $V_{mp}$** for string sizing — String sizing for MPPT requires the **maximum-power voltage** $V_{mp}$, not the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$. $V_{oc}$ is used only for checking the inverter's maximum DC input voltage.
> 4. **Ignoring DoD for batteries** — Many students use the full battery nameplate capacity. The usable energy is $C \times DoD$, which for lead-acid is **0.5–0.6** and for LFP is **0.8**.
> 5. **No temperature derating** — In Kerala, cell temperatures reach 60–70 °C in summer. A 0.4%/°C loss gives a **~15% derating**. Examiners expect this step in any "practical" sizing problem.
> 6. **Mixing units** — A common error is using Ah for energy or kWh for capacity. Stick to **kWh for energy and Ah for charge (Q = C × V)**.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PV cell** = solid-state device using the **photovoltaic effect** in a **p-n junction** (mostly c-Si).
- [x] **STC** is the universal test benchmark: **1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C**.
- [x] **Key parameters:** $I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, $P_{mpp}$, FF, $\eta$.
- [x] **System efficiency $\eta_{sys}$** typically = **0.65 to 0.80** in practice.
- [x] **Temperature derating:** $\gamma \approx 0.004$–$0.005$ /°C for c-Si; **hot climates need array oversize**.
- [x] **Sizing chain:** Daily load → Apply $\eta_{sys}$ → Divide by PSH → Derate for temperature → Round up modules.
- [x] **Battery sizing formula:** $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \times D_{aut}}{\eta_{bat} \times V_{bat} \times DoD}$.
- [x] **Kerala-specific tilt:** $\beta \approx \phi - 10^\circ \approx 0^\circ$ to $3^\circ$; **azimuth = true south**.
- [x] **Performance Ratio (PR)** of a healthy system $\geq 0.75$.
- [x] **Anti-islanding** in grid-tied systems is mandatory under IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116.
- [x] **Cable voltage drop** target: $\leq 2\%$ on DC side, $\leq 1\%$ on AC side.
- [x] **Earthing** resistance: $\leq 5 \, \Omega$; separate electrodes for DC, AC, and lightning.
- [x] **Pythagorean 3-2-1 rule for KTU answers:** Show 3 things in any derivation — formula, substitution, and final numerical answer with **units**.
- [x] **Net-metering policy** in Kerala (KSERC regulations 2020) allows up to **80% of sanctioned load** to be exported — a critical financial consideration for food-industry PV projects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Solar Energy in Food Processing (Water Heating, Electricity Generation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Solar Energy in Food Processing (Water Heating, Electricity Generation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Energy in the Food Industry — Formal Definition

**Solar Energy** is the radiant (electromagnetic) energy emitted by the Sun, harnessed via two principal conversion pathways in the food processing sector:

1. **Solar Thermal Conversion** — Conversion of incident solar radiation into heat, used for **water heating, drying, cooking, pasteurization, evaporation, and refrigeration**.
2. **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Conversion** — Direct conversion of sunlight into electricity using semiconductor devices (solar cells), used to power **motors, pumps, lighting, cold storage, and processing equipment**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT752 – Module 2)**, the food industry is one of the most energy-intensive manufacturing sectors. With global emphasis on **Net-Zero manufacturing** and **Sustainable Development Goal 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)**, solar energy offers a clean, low-operating-cost, decentralized energy source ideally suited to agro-food clusters, dairies, and rural processing hubs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The food industry accounts for approximately **30%** of global industrial energy consumption. Water heating alone may consume **20–40%** of a dairy or brewery's thermal energy budget — a direct target for solar thermal intervention.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Greenhouse on Your Roof"

Imagine the dashboard of a car parked in the sun. Even with the windows closed, the interior becomes unbearably hot. This is because the glass allows short-wave solar radiation in, but traps the re-radiated long-wave heat inside — the **Greenhouse Effect**.

A **solar flat plate collector** does the same job intentionally. Sunlight enters through a transparent glass cover, hits a blackened absorber plate, and gets trapped as heat. Cold water flowing through tubes welded to the plate is warmed and stored in an insulated tank — ready for cleaning crates, heating milk, or pre-heating boiler feed water.

For **electricity**, the analogy is a **leaf**. A leaf converts sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis. A solar cell converts sunlight directly into electrical energy using a silicon semiconductor — the **photovoltaic effect**, discovered by Edmond Becquerel in **1839**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Solar Metrics (KTU-Exam Relevant)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant (extraterrestrial) | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** |
| Air Mass coefficient (AM 1.5) | $AM$ | **1.5** |
| Standard Test Condition irradiance | $G_T$ | **1000 W/m²** |
| Solar declination angle | $\delta$ | **−23.45° to +23.45°** |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** |
| Daily peak sun hours (Kerala average) | PSH | **4.5 – 5.5 h/day** |
| Solar insolation (Kerala annual avg.) | $H$ | **5.0 – 5.5 kWh/m²/day** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala (8°–12° N latitude)** is one of India's most solar-favored states, receiving **~3000 hours of sunshine annually**, comparable to Rajasthan in certain coastal belts — making distributed rooftop solar highly viable for food processing plants located in the state.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Solar Energy Applications in Food Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Solar Energy Applications in Food Industry"]
    A --> B["Solar Thermal Applications"]
    A --> C["Solar Photovoltaic Applications"]
    A --> D["Solar Cogeneration"]
    B --> B1["Water Heating - Flat Plate and Evacuated Tube Collectors"]
    B --> B2["Solar Drying - Crops, Fruits, Fish, Spices"]
    B --> B3["Solar Cooking - Community Kitchens"]
    B --> B4["Solar Pasteurization - Milk, Juice"]
    B --> B5["Solar Refrigeration - Cold Storage, Vapour Absorption"]
    C --> C1["Solar PV for Process Equipment"]
    C --> C2["Solar Water Pumps - Irrigation, Washing"]
    C --> C3["Solar Cold Storage - Dairy, Marine"]
    C --> C4["Solar Lighting and Office Loads"]
    D --> D1["Solar Organic Rankine Cycle for Process Steam"]
    D --> D2["PVT - Photovoltaic Thermal Hybrid"]
```

---

## 1.5 Why Solar Energy for Food Processing?

1. **Energy intensity alignment** — Food processes (drying, cooking, pasteurization) require **low-to-medium grade heat (40 °C – 200 °C)**, perfectly matching the temperature range that solar collectors deliver most efficiently.
2. **Geographic coincidence** — Most agro-processing hubs lie in the sunbelt (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, AP).
3. **Load matching** — Solar availability peaks during daytime, which coincides with peak washing, drying, and processing shifts.
4. **Carbon compliance** — Reduces Scope-1 and Scope-2 emissions, helping plants comply with **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India.
5. **Decentralization** — Can be deployed in rural clusters without grid dependency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Radiation Geometry — Foundation for Collector Sizing

Before any collector can be installed, the **angle of incidence** between the sun's rays and the collector surface must be quantified.

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$$

where:
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence
