# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**List any three real-world case studies of solar-integrated food processing facilities and identify the specific solar technology used in each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Solar tunnel dryer (Hohenheim design)** for mango, chilli and turmeric drying in Karnataka / Andhra Pradesh → uses **transparent polyethylene tunnel with biomass back-up**.
2. **Scheffler-dish concentrator** for steam generation in dairy pasteurization plants in Gujarat → uses **concentrating solar dish with seasonal tracking**.
3. **Solar PV cold storage** for perishables in Maharashtra / Telangana → uses **PV array + battery + vapour-absorption refrigeration (VAR)**.
4. (Any other valid case — e.g., greenhouse dryers for spices in Kerala, CST for rice parboiling in AP.)

> *Each carries 1 mark — 3 marks total for naming three with correct technology.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Differentiate between "Solar Fraction" and "Collector Efficiency" with reference to case-study evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Solar Fraction (SF)** = ratio of *useful solar energy delivered to the process* to the *total process energy demand*. Indicates what fraction of the food-process energy is *solar*. Dimensionless, 0–1.
- **Collector Efficiency (ηc)** = ratio of *useful heat extracted from the collector* to the *solar energy incident on the collector aperture*. Indicates how well the *hardware* converts incoming radiation. Dimensionless, 0–1, typically 40–75 %.

> *Students must write both definitions + state that SF is a system metric while ηc is a hardware metric. Missing this distinction costs the third mark.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the design and operation of a **solar tunnel dryer** for spice processing. Include a neat block diagram, list the components, and explain the role of each component. Discuss why tunnel dryers are preferred over open-sun drying for export-quality spices.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A spice processing unit in Kerala uses a solar tunnel dryer to dry **500 kg of fresh turmeric** from an initial moisture content of **75 % (wb)** to a final moisture content of **8 % (wb)**. The dryer has an effective collector area of **25 m²**, average daily insolation of **5.5 kWh/m²/day**, and operates for **4 effective sun-hours** over **3 days**. Take the drying efficiency as **25 %**. Calculate the **total water evaporated**, the **useful solar energy captured**, and the **solar fraction** if the auxiliary biomass back-up supplies the remainder. Take $h_{fg}$ = 2400 kJ/kg.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

A **solar tunnel dryer** is an enclosed polythene-covered tunnel in which a fan draws ambient air through a flat-bed absorber (heater section) and then through a perforated bed of product. Components:

1. **Transparent polyethylene roof** — admits shortwave solar radiation while trapping longwave re-radiation (greenhouse effect), typically 100–200 micron UV-stabilized film.
2. **Black absorber floor** — converts solar radiation to sensible heat, raising the air temperature by 15–25 °C.
3. **DC fan (PV-powered)** — induces forced convection; capacity typically 0.5–1.5 m/s face velocity.
4. **Drying tunnel with perforated trays** — supports the product; perforations allow uniform air distribution.
5. **Chimney / exhaust** — expels moisture-laden air; creates the draft.
6. **Biomass back-up combustor** (optional) — supplies additional heat during cloudy periods and night drying.

**Why tunnel dryer is preferred over open-sun drying for export-quality spices:**
- **Hygiene:** Closed tunnel excludes dust, insects, bird droppings and fungal spores — critical for aflatoxin-sensitive spices like turmeric and chilli.
- **Uniform drying:** Forced air circulation reduces case-hardening and uneven moisture, giving a marketable colour.
- **Faster drying:** 30–50 % faster than open-sun, reducing microbial growth window.
- **Higher volatile-oil retention:** Lower temperature and shorter exposure retains aroma — directly commanding 20–40 % price premium in export markets.
- **Better rehydration ratio and curcumin content** (turmeric) due to controlled drying.

> *A clean block diagram (similar to Section 4.3) earns 1 mark; component list with role — 3 marks; comparative discussion — 3 marks.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Bone-dry mass**

$$d = (1 - M_{wb,i}) \cdot w_i = 0.25 \times 500 = 125 \text{ kg solids}$$

**Step 2 — Final mass at 8 % wb**

$$0.08 = \frac{w_f - 125}{w_f} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.92 \, w_f = 125 \;\Rightarrow\; w_f = 135.87 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Water evaporated**

$$\boxed{m_w = 500 - 135.87 = 364.13 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Total latent heat demand**

$$Q_{\text{lat}} = m_w \cdot h_{fg} = 364.13 \times 2400 = 873{,}913 \text{ kJ} \approx 873.9 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Total incident solar radiation on collector**

Effective sun-hours = 4 × 3 = 12 h; convert 5.5 kWh/m²/day to kJ/m²/s-equivalent or directly:

$$Q_{\text{inc}} = A_c \cdot \bar{H}_T \cdot \text{days} = 25 \times 5.5 \times 3 \cdot 3600 = 1{,}485{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

(Here $5.5 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day} \times 3600 = 19{,}800 \text{ kJ/m}^2/\text{day}$.)

**Step 6 — Useful solar heat captured (ηd = 25 %)**

$$Q_u = \eta_d \cdot Q_{\text{inc}} = 0.25 \times 1{,}485{,}000 = 371{,}250 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 7 — Solar fraction**

$$SF = \frac{Q_u}{Q_{\text{lat}}} = \frac{371{,}250}{873{,}913} = 0.4248 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{SF \approx 42.5\%}$$

**Step 8 — Auxiliary energy**

$$Q_{\text{aux}} = Q_{\text{lat}} - Q_u = 873{,}913 - 371{,}250 = 502{,}663 \text{ kJ}$$

> *Mark split: Step 1 — 1 mark; Steps 2–3 — 1 mark; Step 4 — 1 mark; Step 5 — 1 mark; Step 6 — 1 mark; Step 7 — 1 mark; Final numerical answer with units — 1 mark.*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **experiences and lessons learned** from documented case studies of **solar PV cold storage systems** for food processing. Cover at least three real-world examples and highlight: (i) system architecture, (ii) measured performance ratio, (iii) barriers encountered, (iv) recommended improvements.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A solar PV system rated **5 kWp** feeds a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) cold room** of storage capacity **10 tonnes** for onion storage at **0–4 °C**. Daily electrical load of the VAR is **18 kWh**. Average solar insolation = **4.8 kWh/m²/day**, system derate factor = 0.75. Compute: (i) the daily PV energy generation, (ii) the **solar fraction** for the cold room (assuming no battery, full direct drive), and (iii) the **autonomy days** if a 48 V, 600 Ah battery bank (depth-of-discharge = 0.6, round-trip efficiency = 0.85) is added.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Example 1 — Maharashtra Onion Cold Storage (off-grid, ~10 kWp PV + lead-acid battery)**
- *Architecture:* PV array → MPPT charge controller → 48 V battery → inverter → single-effect ammonia-water VAR.
- *PR:* 0.72–0.78 in summer; drops to 0.55 in monsoon due to both low irradiance and high humidity (increased condenser load).
- *Barriers:* Battery replacement every 3–4 years ate into economics; operator unfamiliarity caused over-discharge; ambient temperatures > 42 °C in summer reduced COP of VAR.
- *Improvements:* Shift to lithium-ion (DoD 80 %, life 10 yr); variable-speed DC fans; reflective roof coating.

**Example 2 — Telangana Mango Cold Chain (grid-tied PV with net metering, 25 kWp)**
- *Architecture:* Grid-tied PV; excess feeds dairy processing during day; grid supports night operation.
- *PR:* 0.80; CUF 0.18.
- *Barriers:* Frequent grid outages in rural areas defeat net-metering premise.
- *Improvements:* Hybrid inverter with 30 % battery backup.

**Example 3 — Coastal Karnataka Fish Cold Room (off-grid, 2 kWp + lithium battery)**
- *Architecture:* Small modular cold rooms per landing centre; remote IoT monitoring.
- *PR:* 0.75; very low maintenance.
- *Barriers:* Salt corrosion; vandalism; theft.
- *Improvements:* Stainless enclosures; community ownership model.

> *Common theme across all three: battery technology is the dominant cost and failure driver; the *thermal* part of VAR is reliable.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — Daily PV energy generation**

$$\text{PSH} = 4.8 \text{ h/day (since 4.8 kWh/m}^2/\text{day} / 1 \text{ kW/m}^2)$$

$$E_{\text{PV,daily}} = P_{\text{STC}} \times \eta_{\text{sys}} \times \text{PSH} = 5 \times 0.75 \times 4.8 = 18 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Solar Fraction (no battery, direct drive)**

Assuming the cold room operates ~16 hours/day and PV runs ~5 effective sun-hours, only PV-hours reduce the load directly:

$$E_{\text{PV direct}} = 18 \text{ kWh (generated, of which essentially all can be used as VAR runs continuously but only daytime solar powers it)}$$

If VAR operates only when PV is producing (worst case):

$$SF = \frac{18}{18} = 1.0 \quad \text{(theoretical)}$$

However, if the cold room must operate 24 h, the load = 18 kWh/day, PV supplies 18 kWh/day → **SF = 1.0 only if the *load exactly matches generation*.** In reality, the cold room's load is **continuous** at ~0.75 kW. So:

$$SF = \frac{E_{\text{PV}}}{E_{\text{load}}} = \frac{18}{18} = 1.0 \;\;\text{(since 5 kWp × 0.75 × 4.8 = 18 kWh)}$$

$$\boxed{SF = 100\% \text{ for direct-drive, but practical SF} \approx 0.65\text{–}0.80 \text{ due to intermittency}}$$

> *Examiner note: full 7/7 requires showing both the theoretical maximum and the de-rated realistic value.*

**Step 3 — Battery autonomy**

Usable capacity = $48 \times 600 \times 0.6 = 17{,}280 \text{ Wh} = 17.28 \text{ kWh}$

Effective output (after round-trip) = $17.28 \times 0.85 = 14.69 \text{ kWh}$

Daily load = 18 kWh.

$$\boxed{\text{Autonomy} = \frac{14.69}{18} = 0.816 \text{ days} \approx 19.6 \text{ hours}}$$

> *This explains why such systems need ~2× battery for full 24 h autonomy — typically a 48 V, 1200 Ah bank is deployed in practice.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Module 2 case-study questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $SF$ and $\eta_c$.** Always state: *SF is process-fraction*; *ηc is hardware efficiency*.
> 2. **Ignoring the auxiliary energy back-up.** A solar system *without* back-up is not a "case study" — it is a *scenario*. Real case studies always involve hybrid systems (solar + biomass, solar + grid, solar + diesel).
> 3. **Failing to convert units.** $h_{fg}$ must be in kJ/kg when mass is in kg. PSH must be in hours, irradiance in kW/m². Mixing kWh and kJ without $3600$ factor = zero marks for the numerical sub-part.
> 4. **Sketching without labels.** Block diagrams in KTU valuation *must* carry labels on every box and at least one flow direction. A bare 3-box diagram earns ≤ 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring microbial safety.** Dried food case studies that omit $a_w$ and FSSAI specifications are marked down by ~2 marks.
> 6. **No payback period.** A case study that does not discuss economics is incomplete — always quote payback, subsidy, and LCOE.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Case Studies**
> - Case studies are classified along **three axes**: technology (PV / thermal / hybrid), process (drying / pasteurization / cold chain / steam), and geography (Kerala / pan-India / international).
> - Six analytical questions must be answered for every case: **demand, resource, sizing, performance, economics, learnings.**
> - **Solar Fraction (SF)** = useful solar energy ÷ total process energy demand. Typical SF: 30–80 % for hybrid systems, 95–100 % for off-grid PV with battery.
> - **Drying efficiency** realistic range: **20–45 %** for solar dryers. Values > 50 % usually indicate an arithmetic or unit error.
> - **Hohenheim solar tunnel dryer** is the canonical reference for spice / chilli / mango drying. Key components: PE cover, black absorber, DC fan, perforated tray, chimney.
> - **Scheffler dish** is preferred over flat-plate collectors for steam generation in dairy / rice parboiling because of its seasonal tracking and higher concentration ratio.
> - **Solar PV + VAR** is the standard off-grid cold-chain architecture; battery is the bottleneck, not the VAR itself.
> - **Performance Ratio (PR)** for grid-tied PV food processing: typically **0.70–0.85**; **CUF** of 0.15–0.22 is MNRE-acceptable.
> - **Payback period** of solar food processing systems in India: **2–6 years** depending on subsidy and capacity utilization.
> - **Quality gains** of solar drying vs open-sun: 1.4–1.8× vitamin retention; 30–50 % time reduction; 60–90 % contamination reduction.
> - **$a_w \le 0.60$** is the FSSAI-cited microbial safety threshold for dried foods.
> - Always pair solar with **biomass / LPG / grid back-up** — purely solar thermal food processing rarely achieves > 60 % SF at industrial scale.
> - **Common exam traps:** unit-conversion errors between kWh and kJ (× 3600); PSH vs. day-length hours; bone-dry vs. wet-basis moisture content.
> - **Key constants to memorize:** $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg (at 100 °C) or 2400 kJ/kg (at 55 °C); $G_{\text{STC}} = 1000$ W/m²; PSH-Kerala ≈ 4.5–5.0 h/day.
> - **FSSAI + BIS + MNRE** are the three regulatory anchors every case-study answer should reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Energy: Overview of Biomass Energy Sources (Biogas, Biomass Boilers)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biomass Energy: Overview of Biomass Energy Sources (Biogas, Biomass Boilers)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biomass Energy** is the chemical energy stored in organic, non-fossil biological material (biomass) that is released as useful heat, electricity, or fuel upon conversion. In the food industry, biomass is unique because it is simultaneously a **waste-to-energy** resource and a **renewable carbon-neutral fuel** when sourced from agricultural residues, food processing by-products, animal manure, and dedicated energy crops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** Biomass refers to any organic matter derived from living or recently living organisms. Biomass energy is the usable form of energy (thermal, electrical, or chemical) obtained by converting such organic matter through biochemical (fermentation, anaerobic digestion) or thermochemical (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis) routes.

### 1.2 Sub-Category 1 — Biogas

**Biogas** is a renewable gaseous biofuel produced by the **anaerobic digestion (AD)** of organic biomass by a consortium of bacteria in the absence of oxygen. It is a mixture primarily of **methane (CH₄, 50–70%)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂, 30–40%)**, with trace amounts of H₂S, NH₃, and water vapor.

**Standard Calorific Value Reference (at STP, dry basis):**
- **Pure CH₄ : 55.5 MJ/kg** (HHV) or **50.0 MJ/kg** (LHV)
- **Typical Biogas (60% CH₄) : ≈ 22 MJ/m³**
- **Density of CH₄ at STP : 0.717 kg/m³**

### 1.3 Sub-Category 2 — Biomass Boilers

A **biomass boiler** is a closed pressure vessel in which solid biomass fuel (e.g., bagasse, rice husk, wood chips, sawdust, groundnut shell) is combusted to release its chemical energy as **high-temperature flue gas**, which transfers heat to water/steam for process heating, power generation, or space heating in food plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Stomach vs Furnace" Model:**
> Imagine your **digestive system** (anaerobic digestion/biogas) and a **wood-fired camp stove** (biomass boiler) side by side.
> - In your **stomach**, microbes break down food in an **oxygen-free, slow, cold** environment, releasing a combustible gas (methane) — this is **biogas**.
> - In a **camp stove**, wood burns in an **oxygen-rich, fast, hot** flame, releasing heat directly into a pot of water — this is a **biomass boiler**.
> Both consume organic "leftovers," but the path from waste to energy is fundamentally different: one is **biological (low temperature)**, the other is **thermal (high temperature)**.

### 1.4 Classification of Biomass Energy Sources (Food Industry Perspective)

| Source Category | Examples in Food Industry | Energy Conversion Route |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet biomass** (moisture > 60%) | Food processing wastewater, dairy effluent, slaughterhouse waste | Anaerobic Digestion → Biogas |
| **Dry lignocellulosic biomass** | Bagasse, rice husk, coconut shell, groundnut shell | Direct Combustion → Steam |
| **Agro-industrial residues** | Olive cake, fruit peels, brewery spent grain | Co-combustion / Gasification |
| **Energy crops** | Napier grass, switchgrass, sugarcane (dedicated) | Combustion / Biogas |
| **Animal residues** | Cattle/poultry manure | Biogas (covered lagoon / CSTR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Biomass Matters in Food Industry Energy Management:**
> 1. Displaces furnace oil/LPG → reduces Scope 1 emissions
> 2. Converts a disposal cost (waste tipping fees) into a revenue stream
> 3. Closes the carbon loop (CO₂ released ≈ CO₂ absorbed during growth)
> 4. Provides baseload renewable heat for steam-intensive processes
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Biochemical Pathway — Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas Formation)

Anaerobic digestion proceeds in **four sequential stages** carried out by different microbial consortia:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂ by fermentative bacteria.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols are oxidized to acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate, H₂, and CO₂ are converted into **CH₄** by two distinct archaeal groups:
   - **Acetoclastic methanogens** (≈70% of CH₄): CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂
   - **Hydrogenotrophic methanogens** (≈30% of CH₄): CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O

**Net simplified reaction (from glucose):**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$

**Why each stage matters (for KTU):**
- Stage 1 is **rate-limiting** for high-cellulose biomass (e.g., bagasse).
- Stage 4 is **rate-limiting** for protein/lipid-rich wastes (dairy, slaughterhouse).
- The **carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio** of feedstock must be **20–30 : 1** for balanced microbial activity; food wastes are typically N-rich, while crop residues are C-rich — co-digestion solves this.

### 2.2 The Thermochemical Pathway — Biomass Boiler Combustion

In a biomass boiler, the dry biomass (CxHyOz) reacts with excess air in three overlapping zones:

1. **Drying Zone** (100–200 °C): Moisture evaporates (latent heat loss).
2. **Pyrolysis/Volatilization Zone** (200–600 °C): Volatiles (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars) escape; char remains.
3. **Char Combustion Zone** (600–1200 °C): Solid carbon reacts with O₂ → CO₂ + CO.

**Generalized combustion equation:**

$$C_xH_yO_z + \nu O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + \text{heat}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Output of Biomass Boiler | $E_{out} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV_{fuel} \times \eta_{boiler}$ | kW or MJ/h | Sizing boiler for plant heat demand |
| 2 | Calorific Value of Biogas | $CV_{biogas} = f_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4}$ | MJ/m³ | Estimating methane-richness of digester output |
| 3 | Dulong's Formula (HHV of solid biomass) | $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ | MJ/kg | Predicting boiler heat release from ultimate analysis |
| 4 | LHV from HHV | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \frac{9H}{100} \times \frac{100 - M}{100}$ | MJ/kg | Adjusting for latent heat of water vapor in flue gas |
| 5 | Stoichiometric Air Requirement | $A_{st} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ (kg air/kg fuel) | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion air calculation |
| 6 | Excess Air Ratio | $\lambda = \frac{A_{actual}}{A_{st}}$ | dimensionless | Boiler tuning for efficiency |
| 7 | Theoretical CH₄ Yield (Buswell) | $Y_{CH_4} = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}$ for $C_nH_aO_b$ | mol CH₄ / mol substrate | Anaerobic digester design |
| 8 | Boiler Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Performance benchmarking |
| 9 | Steam Generation Rate | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV \times \eta}{h_{fg}}$ | kg/h | Matching boiler to process steam load |
| 10 | Energy Recovery from Biogas | $E_{biogas} = V_{biogas} \times CV_{biogas} \times \eta_{engine}$ | MJ/day | CHP sizing for food plant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the numbers come from (memorize these constants):**
> - **2.442 MJ/kg** = latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C (used in LHV correction)
> - **0.717 kg/m³** = density of CH₄ at STP (101.325 kPa, 273.15 K)
> - **22.4 L/mol** = molar volume of any ideal gas at STP
> - **Typical bagasse CV ≈ 18–20 MJ/kg**; rice husk ≈ 14–15 MJ/kg; wood chips ≈ 17–19 MJ/kg
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calorific Value of Biogas from Methane Fraction

A food industry anaerobic digester produces biogas with the following volumetric composition on a dry basis:
- CH₄ = 65%, CO₂ = 32%, N₂ = 2%, H₂S = 1%

**Step 1 — Identify calorific contributions of each component.**
CO₂, N₂, and H₂S are non-combustible. Only CH₄ contributes energy.

**Step 2 — Recall the calorific value of pure methane at STP.**
The Higher Heating Value (HHV) of CH₄ at STP is **55.5 MJ/kg** and the volumetric value is **37.7 MJ/m³**.

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of CH₄ in 1 m³ of biogas.**
The molar fraction of CH₄ is 0.65, so moles of CH₄ in 1 m³ at STP:

$$n_{CH_4} = \frac{0.65 \times 1000 \text{ L}}{22.4 \text{ L/mol}} = 29.018 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of CH₄:

$$m_{CH_4} = 29.018 \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 464.3 \text{ g} = 0.4643 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy content of 1 m³ of biogas.**

$$CV_{biogas} = 0.4643 \text{ kg} \times 55.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 25.77 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using volumetric shortcut.**

$$CV_{biogas} = 0.65 \times 37.7 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 24.51 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

The two values differ by ~5% due to the HHV vs. LHV assumption and rounding. The board-accepted value is **≈ 25 MJ/m³** for 65% CH₄ biogas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling CH₄ fraction from 50% → 70% increases the calorific value of biogas from **18 MJ/m³ to 26 MJ/m³** (≈ 44% rise), which directly determines whether a CHP engine can run economically on it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Methane Yield via Buswell Equation

For an organic substrate of empirical formula $C_nH_aO_b$ (with no N or S for simplicity), the complete anaerobic digestion is:

**Step 1 — Write the general Buswell stoichiometry.**

$$C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$$

**Step 2 — Apply to glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, n=6, a=12, b=6).**
Moles of CH₄ produced per mole of glucose:

$$Y_{CH_4} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$

This recovers the well-known result: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$.

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric yield at STP.**
Moles of substrate per kg = 1000/180 = 5.556 mol (glucose MW = 180 g/mol).
Total CH₄ moles per kg glucose:

$$n_{CH_4}^{total} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.67 \text{ mol/kg}$$

Volume at STP:

$$V_{CH_4} = 16.67 \times 22.4 \text{ L} = 373.3 \text{ L CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy potential.**

$$E_{glucose} = 373.3 \text{ L} \times \frac{55.5 \text{ MJ/kg}}{16 \text{ g/mol}} \times \frac{16 \text{ g/mol}}{22.4 \text{ L/mol}} = 0.3733 \text{ m}^3 \times 37.7 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 14.08 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

This is **≈ 70% of the HHV of glucose (20.1 MJ/kg)**, with the remaining energy retained in the liquid effluent (digestate).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boiler Sizing for a Sugar Mill (Worked Example)

A sugar mill generates **50 tonnes/day of bagasse** (moisture 50%, ash 2.5%, CV of dry bagasse = 19 MJ/kg). The boiler is to produce **process steam at 10 bar (h_fg = 2014 kJ/kg)** with an overall thermal efficiency of **75%**.

**Step 1 — Mass of dry bagasse per day.**

$$m_{dry} = 50{,}000 \times 0.50 = 25{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 1041.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Effective energy input to boiler.**

$$Q_{in} = 1041.7 \text{ kg/h} \times 19 \text{ MJ/kg} = 19{,}792 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Useful heat available for steam generation.**

$$Q_{useful} = Q_{in} \times \eta = 19{,}792 \times 0.75 = 14{,}844 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Steam generation rate.**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{14{,}844 \text{ MJ/h} \times 1000 \text{ kJ/MJ}}{2014 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 7370 \text{ kg/h} \approx 7.37 \text{ TPH (tonnes per hour)}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent power output (electrical, assuming 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ and 30% conversion to electricity).**

$$P_{electrical} = \frac{14{,}844}{3.6} \times 0.30 = 1237 \text{ kW} \approx 1.24 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A single 50 TPD sugar mill bagasse stream can power ~1.2 MW of continuous electricity — enough to run the mill itself plus export surplus. This is the foundation of **cogeneration (cogen)** in the food industry.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Biogas vs Biomass Boiler Comparison

| Parameter | Biogas (AD) | Biomass Boiler (Combustion) |
|---|---|---|
| **Conversion route** | Biochemical (enzymatic) | Thermochemical (oxidation) |
| **Operating temperature** | 30–55 °C (mesophilic) or 50–60 °C (thermophilic) | 800–1200 °C (flame/bed) |
| **Moisture tolerance** | High (works with > 90% moisture wastes) | Low (must be < 55% moisture to auto-ignite) |
| **Feedstock** | Wet organic waste, manure, effluents | Dry bagasse, husk, wood, pellets |
| **Energy density** | Low (≈ 22 MJ/m³) | High (≈ 18 MJ/kg = 18,000 MJ/m³) |
| **Capex** | High (digesters, gas holders, scrubbers) | Moderate (boiler, grate, ID/FD fans, ESP) |
| **Opex** | Low (ambient operation) | Moderate (ash handling, fuel preparation) |
| **Net CO₂** | Near-zero | Near-zero (if sustainably sourced) |
| **By-product** | Digestate (biofertilizer) | Bottom ash + fly ash |
| **Food industry examples** | Dairy effluent, distillery spent wash, poultry litter | Sugar mills (bagasse), rice mills (husk), coconut shell |
| **KTU board weightage** | High (definitions, Buswell equation, C/N) | High (Dulong, boiler efficiency, combustion stoichiometry) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Biomass Conversion Pathways (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Biomass Feedstock] --> B1{High Moisture Wastes}
    A1 --> B2{Dry Lignocellulosic Wastes}
    B1 --> C1[Anaerobic Digester 30 to 55 C]
    B1 --> C2[Fermentation Ethanol]
    B1 --> C3[Landfill Gas Recovery]
    B2 --> D1[Direct Combustion Boiler]
    B2 --> D2[Gasification Syngas]
    B2 --> D3[Pyrolysis Biochar Bio-oil]
    C1 --> E1[Biogas 60 percent CH4]
    C1 --> E2[Digestate Biofertilizer]
    C1 --> E3[Effluent Treated Water]
    D1 --> F1[Steam or Hot Water]
    D1 --> F2[Bottom Ash and Fly Ash]
    D2 --> F3[Producer Gas CH4 CO H2]
    D3 --> F4[Biochar and Pyrolysis Oil]
    E1 --> G1[CHP Engine or Boiler]
    E1 --> G2[Upgrading to Biomethane]
    F1 --> H1[Process Heat Food Plant]
    F1 --> H2[Turbine for Electricity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Left side = biochemical routes (low-temperature, wet); Right side = thermochemical routes (high-temperature, dry). A single food plant may run **both** — e.g., a dairy uses biogas for steam via CHP and rice husk for direct combustion in another boiler.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis Polymers to Monomers] --> S2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis Monomers to VFAs and Alcohols]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis VFAs to Acetate and H2 and CO2]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Methanogenesis Acetate and H2 to CH4]
    S4 --> S5[Biogas Output CH4 60 percent CO2 40 percent]
    S4 --> S6[Digestate Output Nitrogen Rich Biofertilizer]
    H1[Optimum pH 6.8 to 7.4] --> S1
    H1 --> S2
    H1 --> S3
    H1 --> S4
    T1[Temperature Mesophilic 35 C OR Thermophilic 55 C] --> S1
    T1 --> S2
    T1 --> S3
    T1 --> S4
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Biomass Boiler System Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FB1[Fuel Storage Silo Bagasse or Husk] --> FC1[Fuel Conveyor and Crusher]
    FC1 --> FB2[Boiler Combustion Grate]
    AIR1[Forced Draft Fan] --> FB2
    FB2 --> HG1[Hot Flue Gas Path]
    HG1 --> ECO1[Economizer Preheats Feedwater]
    ECO1 --> EVP1[Evaporator Generates Steam]
    EVP1 --> SH1[Superheater Raises Steam to Process Temperature]
    SH1 --> STM1[Steam to Process Header 10 bar and 180 C]
    FB2 --> ASH1[Bottom Ash Hopper]
    HG1 --> ASH2[Baghouse or ESP for Fly Ash Capture]
    ASH2 --> ID1[Induced Draft Fan]
    ID1 --> STK1[Stack to Atmosphere with Particulate Monitor]
    FW1[Feedwater Pump] --> ECO1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** When a boiler question appears, always label (i) fuel handling, (ii) combustion air, (iii) heat transfer surfaces (economizer → evaporator → superheater), (iv) ash handling, and (v) draft system (FD/ID). Examiners award partial marks for completeness of the block diagram.

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Energy Flow from Biomass to Food Plant Utilities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BM1[Biomass Source Food Waste or Crop Residue] --> CV1[Calorific Value MJ per kg]
    CV1 --> CVR1[Conversion Device Boiler or Digester or Engine]
    CVR1 --> EL1[Energy Losses Flue Gas and Ash and Sensible Heat]
    CVR1 --> ELO1[Useful Energy Steam or Power or Hot Water]
    ELO1 --> FDP1[Food Plant Utilities Boiling Drying Pasteurization Sterilization]
    FDP1 --> PRD1[Finished Food Products]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define biomass energy. List any **four** categories of biomass sources relevant to the food industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Biomass energy** is the usable form of thermal, electrical, or chemical energy derived from organic non-fossil biological material through biological or thermochemical conversion.

Four categories of biomass sources relevant to the food industry:
1. **Wet organic wastes** — dairy effluent, distillery spent wash, slaughterhouse waste, food processing wastewater.
2. **Dry lignocellulosic residues** — bagasse, rice husk, groundnut shell, coconut shell.
3. **Agro-industrial by-products** — fruit peels, olive cake, brewery spent grain.
4. **Animal manures** — cattle dung, poultry litter, piggery waste.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the four sources = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model Paper]**
What is the typical composition of biogas? State its approximate calorific value.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Biogas is the gaseous product of anaerobic digestion of organic matter, with the following typical composition:

- **Methane (CH₄): 50–70%**
- **Carbon dioxide (CO₂): 30–40%**
- **Trace gases: H₂S (0.1–0.5%), NH₃ (<0.1%), water vapor (saturated)**

**Approximate calorific value:** 18–26 MJ/m³ (depending on CH₄ fraction); for a 60% CH₄ biogas, CV ≈ **22 MJ/m³**.

*(1 mark for composition ranges + 1 mark for trace gases + 1 mark for CV = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The two sub-parts are usually one design/derivation and one descriptive/comparison question.

---

### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A sugar mill generates **80 tonnes/day of bagasse** (moisture content 50%, calorific value of dry bagasse = **19 MJ/kg**). The bagasse-fired boiler operates at an overall thermal efficiency of **70%** and supplies saturated steam at **10 bar (latent heat of vaporization h_fg = 2014 kJ/kg)**. Calculate:
(i) The useful heat available per hour.
(ii) The steam generation rate in kg/h.
(iii) The equivalent electrical power output in kW if a 30% efficient turbo-alternator is used downstream. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle and main components of a biomass boiler system** used in food processing industries. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Given:**
- Daily wet bagasse = 80,000 kg/day → dry bagasse = 40,000 kg/day
- Dry bagasse flow rate: $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 40{,}000 / 24 = 1666.7$ kg/h
- CV = 19 MJ/kg
- η_boiler = 70% = 0.70
- h_fg = 2014 kJ/kg
- η_turbine = 30% = 0.30

**(i) Heat input rate:**

$$Q_{in} = 1666.7 \text{ kg/h} \times 19 \text{ MJ/kg} = 31{,}667 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Useful heat rate:**

$$Q_{useful} = 31{,}667 \times 0.70 = 22{,}167 \text{ MJ/h} = 22{,}167{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**(ii) Steam generation rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{22{,}167{,}000}{2014} = 11{,}007 \text{ kg/h} \approx 11 \text{ TPH}$$

**(iii) Electrical power output:**

$$P_{electrical} = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam} \times h_{fg} \times \eta_{turbine}}{3600} = \frac{11{,}007 \times 2014 \times 0.30}{3600} = 1846 \text{ kW} \approx 1.85 \text{ MW}$$

**Final answers (boxed):**
- (i) Q_useful = **22,167 MJ/h**
- (ii) Steam rate = **11,007 kg/h**
- (iii) Electrical power = **1846 kW**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

A **biomass boiler system** converts solid biomass into usable thermal energy (steam/hot water) through controlled combustion. Its main functional blocks are:

1. **Fuel Storage & Handling** — Silo, conveyors, magnetic separators, hammer mills for size reduction (typical size: 10–30 mm).
2. **Combustion Chamber** — Fixed grate, travelling grate, or fluidized bed where fuel burns at 800–1200 °C with excess air (λ = 1.2–1.4).
3. **Air Supply System** — FD fan supplies combustion air; secondary air injection reduces CO and NO_x.
4. **Heat Transfer Surfaces** — Three-zone configuration:
   - **Economizer** — recovers waste heat to preheat feedwater.
   - **Evaporator** — converts water to saturated steam (water-tube design preferred for high pressure).
   - **Superheater** — raises steam temperature to 250–450 °C for process use.
5. **Draft System** — ID fan maintains negative furnace pressure, preventing hot gas leakage.
6. **Ash Handling** — Bottom ash removed via ash hopper; fly ash captured by ESP or baghouse.
7. **Stack & Emission Control** — Particulate monitors, scrubbers for SO₂/HCl where needed.
8. **Control System** — DCS-based combustion control maintaining O₂ in flue gas at 3–5%.

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.3 above).**

*(Full marks awarded for: neat block diagram 3 marks + component explanation 3 marks + working principle 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Using the **Buswell equation**, derive the theoretical yield of methane from **1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** during anaerobic digestion. Assume STP conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **biogas (anaerobic digestion) and biomass combustion (biomass boiler)** as energy recovery routes for food industry wastes. Highlight **at least six parameters** of comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Write the general Buswell stoichiometry** for a substrate $C_nH_aO_b$:

$$C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$$

**Step 2 — Substitute n = 6, a = 12, b = 6 for glucose:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + \left(6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(3 - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(3 + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4}\right)CH_4$$

Simplifying the coefficients:

- Water: $6 - 3 - 3 = 0$ (no net water consumed/produced)
- CO₂: $3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$
- CH₄: $3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$

**Balanced equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$

**Step 3 — Moles of glucose in 1 kg:**

$$n_{glucose} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = 5.556 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of CH₄ produced:**

$$n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.67 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Volume at STP (molar volume = 22.4 L/mol):**

$$V_{CH_4} = 16.67 \times 22.4 = 373.3 \text{ L CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of CH₄ produced:**

$$m_{CH_4} = 16.67 \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 266.7 \text{ g} = 0.2667 \text{ kg per kg glucose}$$

**Step 7 — Energy content (using HHV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg):**

$$E_{CH_4} = 0.2667 \times 55.5 = 14.80 \text{ MJ/kg glucose}$$

**Final answer (boxed):** Theoretical methane yield = **373.3 L/kg glucose** (or 0.267 kg/kg), with an energy content of **14.8 MJ/kg glucose**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

| Parameter | Biogas (AD) | Biomass Combustion (Boiler) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Process type** | Biochemical (microbial) | Thermochemical (oxidation) |
| **2. Operating temperature** | 30–55 °C (ambient-like) | 800–1200 °C (high heat) |
| **3. Moisture tolerance of feed** | High (works with 80–95% moisture) | Low (must be dried to < 55%) |
| **4. Energy form produced** | Gaseous fuel (CH₄–CO₂ mixture) | Direct heat (steam, hot water) |
| **5. Capex & footprint** | High capex, large digesters | Moderate capex, compact boiler |
| **6. By-product utility** | Digestate (organic fertilizer) | Ash (cement, road aggregate) |
| **7. Suitability for food industry** | Wet wastes — dairy, distillery, slaughterhouse | Dry residues — bagasse, husk, shell |

*(1 mark per comparison point × 6 points = 6 marks; 1 mark for concluding remark on which route suits which waste = 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse moisture content and water of combustion.** Boiler efficiency penalizes feed moisture (latent heat loss), while AD actually **needs** water for hydrolysis.
> 2. **HHV vs LHV:** Boiler heat balance uses **LHV** if flue water vapor escapes (which it usually does). Using HHV inflates efficiency by 5–8%.
> 3. **Dulong's formula constants** (0.3491, 1.1783, etc.) must be cited as **MJ/kg per % of element**. Students often write them as dimensionless, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Buswell equation** requires N, S terms to be set to zero unless explicitly given; otherwise examiners deduct 1 mark for incomplete balancing.
> 5. **Block diagrams must be drawn — not just narrated.** A 7-mark question expecting a diagram without one gets capped at 4 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Biomass energy** is the chemical energy of organic, non-fossil matter converted via **biochemical (biogas)** or **thermochemical (combustion/gasification/pyrolysis)** routes.
- **Biogas** = product of **anaerobic digestion (AD)** in 4 stages (Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis).
- **Standard biogas composition:** CH₄ 50–70%, CO₂ 30–40%; CV ≈ **22 MJ/m³** at 60% CH₄.
- **Buswell equation** gives the stoichiometric CH₄ yield: for glucose, $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ → **373 L CH₄/kg glucose at STP**.
- **C/N ratio of 20–30 : 1** is optimum for AD; imbalance causes "sour" (acidic) or "ammonia-toxic" digesters.
- **Biomass boiler** = closed pressure vessel that combusts dry biomass to produce steam/hot water.
- **Dulong's formula** for HHV: $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ (MJ/kg, where C, H, S, O, N, A are weight percentages).
- **LHV = HHV − 2.442 × (9H/100) × ((100−M)/100)** to correct for hydrogen-bound water.
- **Energy output of boiler:** $E_{out} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV \times \eta_{boiler}$ — always list all three parameters with units.
- **Boiler efficiency** typically 70–85%; **excess air ratio** λ = 1.2–1.4 for solid biomass.
- **Food industry examples** to memorize: **bagasse** (sugar mills) → boiler; **dairy/manure/slaughterhouse waste** → biogas.
- **Cogeneration (CHP)** in sugar mills can reach 80%+ total energy efficiency by sequential use of steam for power and process heat.
- **Carbon neutrality** holds only if biomass regrowth rate ≥ harvest rate — a recurring KTU short-answer point.
- **Key food-industry biomass CV values:** bagasse 18–20 MJ/kg, rice husk 14–15 MJ/kg, wood 17–19 MJ/kg, coconut shell 18–20 MJ/kg, cow dung (for AD) 16–18 MJ/m³ of biogas.
- **Comparison rule of thumb:** *Wet waste + low temp → Biogas; Dry waste + high temp → Boiler.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilization of Biomass for Heat and Power Generation in Food Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Utilization of Biomass for Heat and Power Generation in Food Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Biomass in the Context of Food Processing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Biomass refers to any organic, non-fossil biological material derived from living or recently living organisms that can be converted into useful energy carriers such as heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous fuels. In the food processing industry, biomass predominantly consists of agricultural residues (rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, groundnut shells), animal wastes (cattle manure, slaughterhouse offal), and post-consumer/process food waste (fruit peels, whey, vegetable trimmings, spent grains).

**Key Properties of Food-Industry Biomass:**

| Property | Typical Value/Range | Significance |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| Moisture Content | **10–80 % (wet basis)** | High moisture lowers net calorific value |
| Volatile Matter | **60–85 %** | Indicates ease of ignition |
| Fixed Carbon | **5–25 %** | Contributes to char combustion |
| Ash Content | **1–20 %** | Causes slagging in boilers |
| Calorific Value | **8–20 MJ/kg** | Energy released on combustion |
| C:N Ratio | **20:1 – 30:1 (ideal for AD)** | Critical for anaerobic digestion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** or Lower Heating Value (LHV) is the most widely used metric for biomass in KTU calculations, defined at constant pressure with water as vapour. **LHV = GCV − 2.442 × (9·H + M)** where H and M are hydrogen and moisture mass fractions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Biomass as the Food Industry's "Energy Piggy Bank"

Think of a food processing factory as a human body. The body consumes food (raw material), processes it, and generates energy (biomass residues) as a by-product. Just as we can re-convert stored fat into useful work during exercise, the food industry can convert its organic waste streams back into usable heat and electricity. The "piggy bank" is fattened by the food industry itself — every tonne of paddy processed yields ~220 kg of rice husk; every tonne of sugarcane crushed yields ~300 kg of bagasse. These are **in-situ fuels** that eliminate transport costs and provide energy security.

The three "golden pathways" through which this piggy bank can be "spent" are:

1. **Thermal Route** — Direct burning to make steam → heat (and optionally power).
2. **Biochemical Route** — Microbial digestion → biogas (CH₄ + CO₂).
3. **Thermochemical Route** — Heat-driven conversion in low-oxygen environments → producer gas, bio-oil, or biochar.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **"Biomass is Solar Energy Stored as Sugar."** Photosynthesis stores ~**3 × 10²¹ J/year** globally, of which food industry residues represent a substantial recoverable fraction.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Biomass Conversion Technologies

The utilization of biomass for heat and power follows a strict hierarchy of conversion pathways. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **match feedstock characteristics to the appropriate technology**.

### A. Thermochemical Conversion (Heat-Dominant)

**1. Direct Combustion** *(Most commercially mature)*
- Reaction: CₘHₙOₚ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + Heat
- Operating temperature: **800 – 1200 °C**
- Air-to-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric): typically **6–7 kg air/kg dry biomass**
- Equipment: Fixed-bed grates, fluidized-bed combustors, spreader-stokers
- Application in food industry: Rice husk-fired boilers in rice mills; bagasse-fired boilers in sugar mills.

**2. Gasification** *(Higher efficiency, syngas production)*
- Reaction: C + ½O₂ → CO (exothermic partial oxidation)
- Followed by: C + CO₂ → 2CO (Boudouard reaction, endothermic)
- C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (water-gas reaction, endothermic)
- Operating temperature: **700 – 900 °C**
- Equivalence Ratio (ER): **0.20 – 0.40** (sub-stoichiometric)
- Producer gas calorific value: **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³**
- Application: Decentralized power via dual-fuel engines in coconut processing units.

**3. Pyrolysis** *(Bio-oil and biochar production)*
- Reaction in absence of oxygen at **300 – 600 °C**
- Products: Biochar (solid, ~30 %), Bio-oil (liquid, ~40 %), Pyrogas (~30 %)

**4. Torrefaction** *(Pre-treatment for densification)*
- Mild pyrolysis at **200 – 320 °C**
- Yields a hydrophobic, energy-dense solid fuel

### B. Biochemical Conversion

**5. Anaerobic Digestion (AD)**
- Sequence: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis
- Operating temperature: **Mesophilic (30–40 °C)** or **Thermophilic (50–55 °C)**
- Biogas yield: **0.2 – 0.5 m³/kg VS** (volatile solids)
- Biogas composition: **CH₄ (50–70 %)**, CO₂ (30–40 %), H₂S (<2 %)
- Calorific value: **18 – 26 MJ/m³**
- Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT): **15 – 40 days**

**6. Fermentation → Bioethanol**
- Sugar/starch-rich biomass (molasses, whey) → ethanol via *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*
- Ethanol yield: ~**0.51 g ethanol/g glucose** (theoretical)

### C. Chemical Conversion

**7. Transesterification → Biodiesel**
- Oil/fat + CH₃OH → FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) + Glycerol
- Molar ratio oil:methanol = **1:6**
- Catalyst: NaOH or KOH (**1 % w/w**)
- Biodiesel calorific value: **~37 MJ/kg**

## 2.2 Biomass-to-Energy Conversion Routes in Food Industry — Architecture

```
        BIOMASS (Feedstock)
              │
   ┌──────────┼──────────────┐
   │          │              │
   ▼          ▼              ▼
 DRY        MOIST         OIL/FAT
 (<15% H₂O) (>60% H₂O)   
   │          │              │
   ▼          ▼              ▼
Combustion  Anaerobic    Trans-
Gasification Digestion  esterification
Pyrolysis   Fermentation
   │          │              │
   ▼          ▼              ▼
HEAT/POWER  BIOGAS/     BIODIESEL
(Steam/    BIOETHANOL  (FAME)
Syngas)
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** required for KTU ESE numericals on biomass energy.

| # | Formula/Quantity | Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary/Condition |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** | $HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | C,H,O,N,S,A = mass % (dry) | Dulong's formula |
| 2 | **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** | $LHV = HHV - 2.442\,M\,(9H + M)$ | H = hydrogen fraction, M = moisture fraction | Water as vapour |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric Air Requirement** | $A_{st} = \dfrac{8.89\,C + 26.7\,H + 3.33\,S - 3.33\,O}{23.2}$ | Mass of O₂ needed per kg fuel | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | **Actual Air-Fuel Ratio** | $A_{act} = A_{st} \times \lambda$ | λ = excess air ratio (1.2–1.5 typical) | Combustion only |
| 5 | **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}$ | For gasification: ER = 0.2–0.4 | ER < 1 ⇒ gasification |
| 6 | **Combustion Efficiency** | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{T_{flame} - T_{flue}}{T_{flame} - T_{amb}} \times 100$ | T in °C or K | Boiler analysis |
| 7 | **Biogas Yield (Buswell equation)** | $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CO_2$ | Stoichiometric CH₄ & CO₂ | Theoretical max |
| 8 | **Methane Yield** | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.25 \times VS_{added} \times \eta_{digestion}$ | VS in kg, η ≈ 0.6–0.85 | Empirical |
| 9 | **Energy from Biogas** | $E_{biogas} = V_{biogas} \times LHV_{biogas} \times \eta_{engine}$ | V in m³/day, LHV ≈ 22 MJ/m³ | CHP analysis |
| 10 | **Power from Steam Turbine** | $P_{elec} = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_{in} - h_{out}) \times \eta_{turb} \times \eta_{gen}$ | ṁ in kg/s, h in kJ/kg | Rankine cycle |
| 11 | **Transesterification Yield** | $Y_{biodiesel} = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | Mass-based % | ≥ 95 % commercial |
| 12 | **Overall CHP Efficiency** | $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{thermal} + \eta_{electrical}$ | Two outputs combined | η_CHP > 80 % |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance:** In the Indian sugar industry, **bagasse-based cogeneration** provides **~3500 MW** of installed power, of which ~**2200 MW is exported** to the grid during crushing season (Oct–Mar). Rice mills in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh use rice-husk boilers to produce **2.5–4.0 tonnes of steam per tonne of husk**, displacing furnace oil and saving ₹15–25 lakhs/year per medium-scale mill.

| Food Industry Sector | Typical Biomass Feedstock | Preferred Technology |
|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Rice milling | Rice husk | Combustion boiler |
| Sugar industry | Bagasse | Cogeneration (Rankine) |
| Coconut processing | Coir pith, shells | Gasification + IC engine |
| Dairy industry | Whey, cattle dung | Anaerobic digestion |
| Edible oil industry | Spent bleaching earth, oil cake | Transesterification (biodiesel) |
| Fruit processing | Pomace, peels | Combustion / pyrolysis |
| Brewery / Distillery | Spent grain, effluents | AD + biogas engine |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Hot Point:** The examiner specifically tests the link between **feedstock moisture** and **conversion route**. **Rule of Thumb:** Moisture < 15 % → Combustion; Moisture 15–50 % → Gasification (with drying); Moisture > 60 % → Anaerobic Digestion.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Methane Yield from Anaerobic Digestion (Buswell Equation)

This derivation is a **KTU board favourite** for the 14-mark Part B questions because it tests both **stoichiometric clarity** and **chemical engineering depth**.

### Problem Setup
A dairy processing unit generates **1000 kg/day** of cheese whey (composition approximated as lactose, C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁). The anaerobic digester operates at **mesophilic conditions** with a **digestion efficiency of 70 %**. Compute:
(a) Theoretical volume of CH₄ produced per day at STP.
(b) Energy content of the biogas (assume 65 % CH₄ + 35 % CO₂ by volume).
(c) Equivalent electrical energy if a CHP engine runs at 35 % electrical + 45 % thermal efficiency.

### Step 1 — Apply Buswell's General Equation to Lactose

For lactose C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁, identify: n = 12, a = 22, b = 11.

$$
C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Water Coefficient

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} &= 12 - \frac{22}{4} - \frac{11}{2} \\
&= 12 - 5.5 - 5.5 \\
&= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

So **1 mole of water** is consumed per mole of lactose.

### Step 3 — Compute the Methane Coefficient

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} &= \frac{12}{2} - \frac{22}{8} + \frac{11}{4} \\
&= 6 - 2.75 + 2.75 \\
&= 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

So **6 moles of CH₄** are produced per mole of lactose.

### Step 4 — Compute the Carbon Dioxide Coefficient

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} &= 6 + 2.75 - 2.75 \\
&= 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

So **6 moles of CO₂** are also produced. This gives the **theoretical maximum CH₄:CO₂ = 50:50**, but in practice the ratio shifts due to incomplete degradation.

### Step 5 — Write the Balanced Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O &\rightarrow 6\,CH_4 + 6\,CO_2 \\
\text{(lactose)} &\quad \text{(biogas, 50:50)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert Mass of Lactose to Moles

Molecular weight of lactose: $M_{lac} = (12)(12) + (1)(22) + (16)(11) = 342\ \text{g/mol}$.

$$
n_{lac} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3\ \text{g}}{342\ \text{g/mol}} = 2923.97\ \text{mol}
$$

### Step 7 — Theoretical CH₄ Moles

$$
n_{CH_4,\,theory} = 6 \times 2923.97 = 17543.8\ \text{mol}
$$

### Step 8 — Apply Digestion Efficiency (70 %)

$$
n_{CH_4,\,actual} = 0.70 \times 17543.8 = 12280.7\ \text{mol/day}
$$

### Step 9 — Convert Moles to Volume at STP (22.414 L/mol)

$$
V_{CH_4} = 12280.7 \times 22.414 = 2.753 \times 10^5\ \text{L/day} = 275.3\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

### Step 10 — Total Biogas Volume (CH₄ + CO₂ at 65:35)

$$
V_{biogas} = \frac{275.3}{0.65} = 423.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

### Step 11 — Energy Content of Biogas

Using LHV of methane ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{biogas} &= 0.65 \times 35.8 + 0.35 \times 0 \approx 23.27\ \text{MJ/m}^3 \\
E_{biogas} &= 423.5 \times 23.27 = 9855\ \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 12 — Electrical Output from CHP

$$
P_{elec} = 9855 \times 0.35 = 3449\ \text{MJ/day} \approx 958\ \text{kWh/day}
$$

$$
P_{thermal} = 9855 \times 0.45 = 4435\ \text{MJ/day} \approx 1232\ \text{kWh}_{th}/\text{day}
$$

### Final Result Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Theoretical CH₄ yield | **275.3 m³/day** |
| Total biogas | **423.5 m³/day** |
| Biogas energy | **9 855 MJ/day** |
| Electrical energy | **958 kWh/day** |
| Thermal energy | **1232 kWh/day** |
| Combined CHP efficiency | **80 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The 65:35 split is a **practical assumption** used because real digesters rarely hit the 50:50 theoretical maximum — Volatile Fatty Acid (VFA) accumulation and ammonia inhibition reduce the CH₄ fraction. Examiners award full marks only when both the **stoichiometric derivation** and the **practical correction** are shown.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Conversion Technology

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy Tip:** A 7-mark sub-question may simply ask "Suggest the most suitable biomass conversion technology for a given food industry waste." Use this matrix as your decision tool.

| Decision Criterion | Combustion | Gasification | Anaerobic Digestion | Pyrolysis | Transesterification |
|-------------------|------------|-------------|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Feedstock Moisture | < 15 % | 15–30 % | 60–95 % | < 15 % | < 5 % (oil only) |
| Capital Cost | Low–Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| Operating Cost | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Energy Output Form | Heat/Power | Syngas/Power | Biogas/Power | Bio-oil | Liquid fuel |
| Energy Efficiency | 60–85 % | 70–85 % | 30–60 % | 60–75 % | 85–95 % |
| Feedstock Type | Dry lignocellulose | Dry lignocellulose | Wet organic | Dry lignocellulose | Triglycerides |
| Food Industry Example | Rice husk, bagasse | Coconut shells | Whey, slaughter waste | Spent coffee grounds | Used cooking oil |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bagasse Feedstock] --> B[Bagasse Yard and Storage]
    B --> C[Boiler Feed System]

    F --> G[Alternator 25 MWe]
    G --> H[Step up Transformer]
    H --> I[Grid Export and Plant Load]
    F --> J[Condenser]

    L --> M[Boiler Feed Pump]
    M --> D
    D --> N[Flue Gas to ESP]
    N --> O[Stack Emission Control]
    D --> P[Ash Handling System]
    P --> Q[Ash Disposal or Sale]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each arrow represents either a mass flow (bagasse, water, ash) or an energy flow (steam, electricity). The closed loop **J → K → L → M → D** represents the **condensate-recirculation loop** that makes the Rankine cycle regenerative. The **condenser (J)** rejects low-grade waste heat — KTU examiners often test this as **"pinch point" analysis**.

## 4.4 Heat and Power Integration in a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIOMASS_BLOCK[Biomass Conversion Block]
        B1[Bagasse Husk or Whey] --> B2[Conversion Technology]
        B2 --> B3[Steam or Biogas]
    end
    subgraph DELIVERY_BLOCK[Energy Delivery Block]
        B3 --> D1[Steam Turbine or Gas Engine]
        D1 --> D2[Electricity]
        D1 --> D3[Process Steam or Hot Water]
    end
    subgraph FOOD_BLOCK[Food Processing Block]
        D2 --> F1[Compressors Motors Refrigeration]
        D3 --> F2[Boiling Evaporation Blanching Sterilization]
        F1 --> F3[Finished Food Product]
        F2 --> F3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation Guide:** This diagram illustrates the **"Energy Cascade"** principle — high-grade energy (electricity) is used for work-intensive tasks (compressors, motors), while low-grade energy (low-pressure steam, hot water) is used for heat-intensive tasks (evaporation, blanching). This cascading maximizes the **exergy efficiency** of the biomass-derived energy.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define biomass. List any four biomass resources generated by the food processing industry.**

**Model Answer:**
Biomass is any organic material of biological origin, excluding fossil fuels, that can be used as a source of energy. In the food processing industry, four major biomass resources are:

1. **Rice husk** — from rice milling (≈ 220 kg per tonne paddy)
2. **Sugarcane bagasse** — from sugar mills (≈ 300 kg per tonne cane)
3. **Whey** — from dairy processing (≈ 80 % of milk volume converted to whey during cheese-making)
4. **Coconut shells / coir pith** — from coconut processing units

> [!TIP]
> Each answer should mention **specific quantity or composition** wherever possible to score full marks.

---

**Q2. State the Buswell equation. What is its significance in anaerobic digestion?**

**Model Answer:**
The Buswell equation gives the **stoichiometric theoretical yield of CH₄ and CO₂** from the anaerobic digestion of any organic compound of the form $C_n H_a O_b$:

$$
C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2
$$

**Significance:**
- Predicts the **maximum methane yield** from any biomass of known composition.
- Determines the **optimal C:H:O ratio** for biogas production.
- Helps in **digester sizing** and **biogas engine selection** for food industry AD plants.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**
> *Internal choice — answer any ONE full question.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Combustion-Based Cogeneration

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

A sugar mill processes **3000 tonnes of sugarcane per day** and generates **bagasse at 30 % by mass** of cane crushed. The bagasse has a moisture content of **50 % (wet basis)** and a calorific value of **8.5 MJ/kg (dry basis)**.

**(a)** Calculate the total energy content (in GJ/day) of the bagasse generated, accounting for moisture. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If a cogeneration plant burns the bagasse in a boiler with **thermal efficiency 70 %** and drives a steam turbine-generator set with **overall electrical efficiency of 25 %** (of the chemical energy input), compute: **(i)** the heat output in GJ/day, **(ii)** the electrical power output in kW (assuming 24-hour operation), and **(iii)** the exportable electricity after meeting an auxiliary consumption of **8 %** of generated power. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Energy Content Calculation**

Bagasse generated per day:
$$
m_{bagasse,\,wet} = 0.30 \times 3000 = 900\ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

Dry bagasse mass:
$$
m_{dry} = 900 \times (1 - 0.50) = 450\ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

Apply LHV correction (assume hydrogen fraction in bagasse H ≈ 6 %):

$$
LHV_{wet} = HHV_{dry} - 2.442 \times M \times (9H + M)
$$

$$
LHV_{wet} = 8.5 - 2.442 \times 0.50 \times (9 \times 0.06 + 0.50) = 8.5 - 0.7326 \approx 7.77\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

Total daily energy:
$$
E_{total} = 450\,000\ \text{kg} \times 7.77\ \text{MJ/kg} = 3.50 \times 10^6\ \text{MJ/day} = 3497\ \text{GJ/day}
$$

**Part (b) — Heat and Power Output**

(i) Heat output:
$$
Q_{useful} = 3497 \times 0.70 = 2448\ \text{GJ/day}
$$

(ii) Electrical output (over 24 h = 86400 s):
$$
E_{elec,\,gross} = 3497 \times 0.25 = 874.2\ \text{GJ/day}
$$

$$
P_{elec,\,gross} = \frac{874.2 \times 10^9}{86400} = 1.012 \times 10^7\ \text{W} \approx 10.12\ \text{MW}
$$

(iii) Auxiliary consumption = 8 % of gross = 0.81 MW

Exportable power:
$$
P_{export} = 10.12 \times 0.92 = 9.31\ \text{MW}
$$

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Total biomass energy | **3497 GJ/day** |
| Useful heat | **2448 GJ/day** |
| Gross electrical | **10.12 MW** |
| Exportable power | **9.31 MW** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Common Error #1:** Students forget to convert moisture from **wet basis to dry basis**. Always specify "wet" or "dry" explicitly.
> 2. **Common Error #2:** Using HHV instead of LHV. KTU examiners give partial credit only if the student **mentions why LHV is preferred** (water exits as vapour).
> 3. **Common Error #3:** Failing to convert GJ/day to MW using the 86400 s factor.
> 4. **Marks Distribution Trap:** Part (a) carries **3 marks** for correct mass calculation, **2 marks** for LHV correction, **2 marks** for final energy. Part (b) carries **2 marks each** for the three sub-results. Always present results in a **boxed table** for clarity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Anaerobic Digestion in Dairy Industry

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

A cheese manufacturing unit generates **50 tonnes/day of cheese whey** with the following characteristics:
- Total Solids (TS): **6 %**
- Volatile Solids (VS): **85 % of TS**
- Digestion efficiency: **75 %**
- Biogas composition: **65 % CH₄ + 35 % CO₂**
- CHP engine electrical efficiency: **35 %**

**(a)** Using the Buswell equation (assume whey carbohydrates ≈ lactose C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁), calculate the **theoretical daily methane volume at STP** and the **total energy content of the biogas** in MJ/day. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If **30 % of the methane is upgraded to biomethane** (99 % CH₄, LHV = 50 MJ/kg) and the rest is combusted in a boiler with **80 % thermal efficiency** to generate process steam at **2.0 MPa** (enthalpy of saturated steam = 2799 kJ/kg, feedwater enthalpy = 504 kJ/kg), compute: **(i)** the mass of upgraded biomethane in kg/day, **(ii)** the steam generation rate in tonnes/day from the remaining CH₄. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Methane Yield and Energy**

Volatile solids load:
$$
m_{VS} = 50\,000\ \text{kg} \times 0.06 \times 0.85 = 2550\ \text{kg/day}
$$

Effective carbohydrate mass (assume all VS is lactose-equivalent):
$$
m_{lac} = 2550\ \text{kg/day}
$$

Moles of lactose per day:
$$
n_{lac} = \frac{2550 \times 10^3}{342} = 7456\ \text{mol/day}
$$

Theoretical methane (6 mol per mol lactose):
$$
n_{CH_4} = 6 \times 7456 = 44\,736\ \text{mol/day}
$$

Apply 75 % efficiency:
$$
n_{CH_4,\,actual} = 0.75 \times 44\,736 = 33\,552\ \text{mol/day}
$$

Volume at STP:
$$
V_{CH_4} = 33\,552 \times 22.414 = 7.52 \times 10^5\ \text{L/day} = 752\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Total biogas volume:
$$
V_{biogas} = \frac{752}{0.65} = 1157\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Energy content (LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³):
$$
E_{biogas} = 752 \times 35.8 = 26\,922\ \text{MJ/day} \approx 26.9\ \text{GJ/day}
$$

**Part (b) — Biomethane and Steam Generation**

(i) Methane for upgrading (30 %):
$$
V_{CH_4,\,upgraded} = 0.30 \times 752 = 225.6\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Density of CH₄ at STP = 0.717 kg/m³:
$$
m_{biomethane} = 225.6 \times 0.717 = 161.8\ \text{kg/day}
$$

(ii) Methane for boiler (70 %):
$$
V_{CH_4,\,boiler} = 0.70 \times 752 = 526.4\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Energy to boiler:
$$
E_{boiler} = 526.4 \times 35.8 = 18\,845\ \text{MJ/day}
$$

Useful heat (80 % efficiency):
$$
Q_{useful} = 0.80 \times 18\,845 = 15\,076\ \text{MJ/day}
$$

Steam enthalpy rise:
$$
\Delta h_{steam} = 2799 - 504 = 2295\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Steam generation:
$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{15\,076 \times 10^3}{2295} = 6569\ \text{kg/day} \approx 6.57\ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Theoretical CH₄ volume | **752 m³/day** |
| Total biogas energy | **26.9 GJ/day** |
| Upgraded biomethane | **161.8 kg/day** |
| Steam generation | **6.57 tonnes/day** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Common Error #1:** Students forget to apply the **digestion efficiency (75 %)** before calculating volume. This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Common Error #2:** Using **gross calorific value of CH₄ (55.5 MJ/m³)** instead of LHV. Since boiler exhaust gases carry water vapour, LHV is correct.
> 3. **Common Error #3:** Using the **wrong methane density**. At STP it is **0.717 kg/m³**, not 0.656 kg/m³ (which is for NTP).
> 4. **Common Error #4:** Students often use **0.85 (VS fraction) twice** — once for carbohydrate load and once for "real" carbohydrate mass. The 85 % VS already accounts for carbohydrate content.
> 5. **Time Trap:** Allocate **8 minutes for setup**, **5 minutes for derivation**, and **1 minute for final answer verification**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Memorize These:**

- ✅ Biomass = **organic, non-fossil, biological material** convertible to energy.
- ✅ Three conversion families: **Thermochemical, Biochemical, Chemical**.
- ✅ Feedstock moisture dictates technology: **< 15 % → Combustion, > 60 % → AD**.
- ✅ Buswell equation is the **stoichiometric backbone of AD** calculations.
- ✅ Bagasse LHV (50 % moisture) ≈ **7.5–8.0 MJ/kg**; Rice husk LHV ≈ **13–15 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ Biogas composition: **50–70 % CH₄, 30–40 % CO₂**; LHV ≈ **18–26 MJ/m³**.
- ✅ Biodiesel LHV ≈ **37 MJ/kg**; transesterification uses **1:6 oil:methanol molar ratio**.
- ✅ Cogeneration CHP efficiency can exceed **80 %** (electrical + thermal).
- ✅ Excess air ratio (combustion) = **1.2–1.5**; Equivalence Ratio (gasification) = **0.2–0.4**.
- ✅ HHV uses Dulong's formula: $0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$.
- ✅ LHV = HHV − **2.442 × M × (9H + M)** (water leaves as vapour).
- ✅ Stoichiometric air: $A_{st} = (8.89C + 26.7H + 3.33S - 3.33O)/23.2$ kg/kg fuel.
- ✅ Anaerobic digestion steps: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- ✅ Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) for AD: **15–40 days**; optimum C:N ratio = **20:1–30:1**.
- ✅ **Dulong–Petit formula** is for HHV; **Schmidt formula** is for approximate combustion temperature.
- ✅ Food industry biomass: rice husk, bagasse, whey, coconut shells, spent grain, oil cake, pomace.
- ✅ Always **state units** in every numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** per missing unit.
- ✅ Show **all intermediate steps** in Buswell-equation problems — partial marking is generous if logic is shown.
- ✅ Mention **practical vs theoretical yield** in AD problems — KTU expects a 65:35 CH₄:CO₂ ratio correction.
- ✅ Final answer should be presented in a **boxed/summary table** for clarity in 14-mark questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic:** **"C-G-P-B-A-T"** — *Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis, Biochemical (AD), Bioethanol, Transesterification* — the six conversion pathways a KTU student must remember for any biomass question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Considerations and Sustainability Aspects of Biomass Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Considerations and Sustainability Aspects of Biomass Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biomass Energy Environmental Sustainability** refers to the systematic evaluation of biomass-derived fuels (solid, liquid, and gaseous) across their entire value chain—cultivation, harvesting, pre-processing, conversion, distribution, and end-use combustion—against three pillars of sustainability:

1. **Environmental Integrity** — Net Greenhouse Gas (GHG) balance, air and water emissions, soil health, biodiversity preservation.
2. **Social Acceptability** — Food security, rural livelihoods, land tenure rights.
3. **Economic Viability** — Cost-competitiveness, supply chain reliability, market access.

A biomass energy system is deemed *sustainable* only when the **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** > 1, **GHG abatement** is positive versus the fossil-fuel baseline, and the **carbon payback time** is bounded within an ecologically acceptable horizon (typically **≤ 20–30 years**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Biomass energy sustainability is **NOT** synonymous with "carbon neutral by default." True sustainability must be empirically demonstrated through quantified **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** following ISO 14040/14044 standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Closed-Loop Tree Bank"

Imagine a tree as a **biological carbon bank account**:

- **Deposit (Growth Phase):** The tree "deposits" atmospheric CO₂ into its woody tissues via photosynthesis. This is the **carbon sequestration** leg.
- **Withdrawal (Combustion Phase):** When burned, the tree "withdraws" that exact CO₂ back into the atmosphere.
- **Sustainability Check:** A biomass energy project is sustainable **only if new deposits (re-growth) outpace withdrawals (combustion) within one rotation cycle**, and the auxiliary inputs (fertilizers, diesel for harvesting, transport fuel) do not cause net withdrawals from another "bank" (the fossil carbon reservoir).

If, for example, we burn an old-growth forest, the "withdrawal" happens today, but the "deposit" (re-growth) takes **60–100 years**—this is **unsustainable**. However, burning agricultural residue or dedicated energy crops with annual/seasonal rotations achieves near-perfect balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Food Industry Context:** In the food processing sector, biomass sustainability is uniquely favorable because **agricultural residues** (rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, coconut shells, wheat straw) are *co-products*—using them for energy does NOT displace food production. This gives the food industry a strategic advantage in the bioenergy economy.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (Board-Exam Critical)

The following metrics are **universally tested** in KTU ESE and must be memorized:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon content of dry biomass | $C_{bio}$ | 0.45 – 0.50 | kg C / kg dry biomass |
| Net calorific value (woody biomass) | $NCV_{wood}$ | 16 – 19 | MJ/kg |
| Photosynthetic CO₂ fixation rate | $\eta_{PS}$ | ~ 1.83 | g CO₂ per g glucose |
| Global Warming Potential (CO₂ reference) | $GWP_{CO_2}$ | **1** | dimensionless |
| GWP of methane | $GWP_{CH_4}$ | **28** (100-yr, AR5) | dimensionless |
| Stoichiometric O₂ for biomass combustion | — | ~ 1.07 | kg O₂ / kg dry biomass |
| Average biomass heating value | $Q_{b}$ | 17 | MJ/kg |
| Fossil fuel heating value (diesel equiv.) | $Q_{f}$ | 45 | MJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to memorize:** **$GWP_{CH_4} = 28$** (IPCC AR5, 100-year horizon). Methane matters because anaerobic digestion and incomplete biomass combustion release CH₄, which is **28× more potent** than CO₂.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Biomass Sustainability

Biomass sustainability evaluation is structured around **five interconnected pillars**. Each pillar is assessed using specific Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that appear repeatedly in KTU examinations.

#### **Pillar 1 — Carbon Balance and GHG Accounting**

The fundamental question: *Does biomass energy reduce atmospheric CO₂ compared to the fossil fuel it displaces?*

- **Carbon Neutrality Assumption:** Biomass combustion releases biogenic carbon—carbon that was recently fixed from atmospheric CO₂ by photosynthesis. Therefore, the combustion emission is theoretically balanced by re-growth uptake.
- **Reality Check:** The neutrality assumption breaks when:
  - Fossil inputs are used in cultivation, harvesting, and transport (diesel, fertilizers).
  - Land Use Change (LUC) releases soil carbon stocks.
  - Combustion is incomplete, releasing CH₄ and CO.
- **Net GHG saving formula:**

$$\text{GHG Saving} (\%) = \frac{E_{fossil} - E_{bio}}{E_{fossil}} \times 100$$

where $E_{fossil}$ and $E_{bio}$ are life-cycle GHG emissions (g CO₂eq/MJ) of the fossil and biomass systems, respectively.

#### **Pillar 2 — Air Quality and Pollutant Emissions**

Biomass combustion, especially in inefficient stoves, emits:
- **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀)** — respiratory hazard.
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — fuel-bound N from agricultural residues (e.g., rice straw has 0.5–1.5% N).
- **Sulfur Oxides (SOₓ)** — generally low for biomass (< 0.1% S) vs. coal (~2% S).
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** and **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — indicators of incomplete combustion.
- **Dioxins and Furans** — at low temperatures (< 850 °C), chlorine-bearing biomass (e.g., MSW, some crop residues) can form these carcinogens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Control:** Modern biomass boilers operating at **≥ 850 °C** with adequate residence time (> 2 s) and good air staging can reduce CO emissions to **< 60 mg/Nm³** and PM to **< 30 mg/Nm³**, complying with EPA and EU IED standards.

#### **Pillar 3 — Land Use and Food Security ("Food vs Fuel" Debate)**

- **Direct Land Use Change (dLUC):** Conversion of forest/grassland to energy crop plantation releases 200–600 t CO₂/ha.
- **Indirect Land Use Change (iLUC):** Displacing food crops to non-food land causes market-driven expansion elsewhere.
- **Mitigation:** Use **residues and wastes** (food industry by-products), **degraded/marginal lands**, and **multi-purpose crops** (e.g., sweet sorghum—grain for food, stalk for ethanol, bagasse for power).

#### **Pillar 4 — Water Footprint and Soil Health**

- Water requirement for energy crops: **500–3000 liters per liter of bioethanol** (vs. ~ 100 L/L for gasoline).
- Soil erosion risk: Intensive harvesting of residues (> 40% removal) depletes soil organic carbon and increases erosion by **30–60%**.
- Best Practice: **Sustainable Removal Rate** ≤ 30–40% of crop residues to maintain soil cover.

#### **Pillar 5 — Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services**

- Monoculture plantations (e.g., oil palm, eucalyptus) reduce biodiversity by **50–80%** versus natural forest.
- Native, mixed-species energy crops with low input regimes support **higher biodiversity** and pollinator populations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NER} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy in biofuel}}{\text{Fossil energy input}}$ | Net Energy Ratio; NER > 1 indicates net energy producer |
| 2 | $\text{Carbon Payback} = \dfrac{C_{release}}{R_{seq}}$ | Time (years) to repay carbon debt from LUC |
| 3 | $E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12}$ | CO₂ emission from combustion (kg CO₂); 44/12 = molar mass ratio CO₂/C |
| 4 | $\text{GHG Saving} = \dfrac{E_{fossil} - E_{bio}}{E_{fossil}} \times 100$ | % GHG reduction vs. fossil baseline |
| 5 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{m_{fuel} \times NCV}{m_{fuel} \times NCV + \text{Losses}}$ | Combustion efficiency (%) |
| 6 | $ER = \dfrac{\text{Air supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}}$ | Equivalence Ratio; ER = 1 is stoichiometric |
| 7 | $\text{PM emission} = C_{PM} \times \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{E_{gen}}$ | Particulate matter per unit energy (g/MJ) |
| 8 | $\text{CO}_2\text{eq} = m_{CO_2} \times 1 + m_{CH_4} \times 28 + m_{N_2O} \times 265$ | Equivalent CO₂ using AR5 GWP factors |
| 9 | $W_f = V_{water} / E_{bio}$ | Water footprint (L/MJ of bioenergy) |
| 10 | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | System Efficiency (cultivation → conversion → end-use) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Formatting Rule:** All absolute-value and ratio notations use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking KTU markdown rendering. Example: $ER = \vert \frac{\text{Air supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}} \vert$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

In food processing plants (sugar mills, rice mills, oil extraction units, dairies), biomass sustainability manifests as:

- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** from bagasse, rice husk, or coconut shell—achieving **75–85% system efficiency**.
- **Net-zero waste-to-energy loops** where effluents become biogas via anaerobic digestion.
- **Compliance with EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED II)** for export of "green" food products with low-carbon certification labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carbon Dioxide Emission from Biomass Combustion

**Problem Statement:** A bagasse-fired boiler burns 1000 kg/hr of dry bagasse (carbon fraction = 0.46). Determine the mass of CO₂ released per hour from biogenic carbon alone.

**Step 1 — Establish the Combustion Reaction (per kg of C):**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Determine the Mass of Carbon Burned:**

$$m_C = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} = 1000 \text{ kg/hr} \times 0.46 = 460 \text{ kg C/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Stoichiometric Mass Ratio:**

$$m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C}$$

where $M_{CO_2} = 44 \text{ g/mol}$ and $M_C = 12 \text{ g/mol}$.

$$m_{CO_2} = 460 \times \frac{44}{12} = 460 \times 3.6667$$

$$\boxed{m_{CO_2} = 1686.67 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{hr}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This entire 1686.67 kg/hr is *biogenic* CO₂—carbon that the sugarcane plant fixed from the atmosphere during the previous growing season. Under carbon-neutrality assumption, the next crop re-fixes this amount, completing the cycle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Energy Ratio (NER) for a Biogas Plant

**Problem Statement:** A food-industry anaerobic digestion plant produces 5000 m³/day of biogas (60% CH₄). The energy input is: 200 kWh/day electricity for mixing/pumping + 50 L/day diesel for feedstock transport. Compute the NER.

**Step 1 — Energy Output from Biogas:**

Calorific value of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg = 35.8 MJ/m³ (at NTP).

$$E_{out} = V_{biogas} \times x_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4}$$
