$$E_{out} = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.60 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

$$E_{out} = 107{,}400 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Energy Output to kWh:**

$$E_{out} = 107{,}400 \text{ MJ/day} \times \frac{1 \text{ kWh}}{3.6 \text{ MJ}} = 29{,}833.33 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Energy Input (Diesel LHV = 36 MJ/L):**

$$E_{in, diesel} = 50 \text{ L/day} \times 36 \text{ MJ/L} = 1800 \text{ MJ/day} = 500 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{in, total} = 200 + 500 = 700 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Compute NER:**

$$\text{NER} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{29{,}833.33}{700}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NER} \approx 42.62}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** NER ≫ 1 confirms the plant is a **massive net energy producer**. Sustainable biogas plants typically have NER > 5; food industry AD plants often exceed 20 due to free feedstock and waste heat recovery.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — GHG Saving Calculation for Rice Husk vs. Diesel Boiler

**Problem Statement:** Compare a rice husk boiler vs. a diesel boiler producing 1 MJ of useful heat. Use the data below.

| Parameter | Rice Husk System | Diesel System |
|---|---|---|
| Cultivation/Extraction GHG | 5 g CO₂eq/MJ | 8 g CO₂eq/MJ |
| Transport & Pre-processing | 3 g CO₂eq/MJ | 2 g CO₂eq/MJ |
| Combustion CO₂ | 0 (biogenic, neutral) | 74 g CO₂eq/MJ |
| Methane leakage (fugitive) | 1 g CO₂eq/MJ | 0 |

**Step 1 — Total GHG for Each System:**

$$E_{bio} = 5 + 3 + 0 + 1 = 9 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/MJ}$$

$$E_{fossil} = 8 + 2 + 74 + 0 = 84 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply GHG Saving Formula:**

$$\text{GHG Saving} = \frac{E_{fossil} - E_{bio}}{E_{fossil}} \times 100$$

$$\text{GHG Saving} = \frac{84 - 9}{84} \times 100 = \frac{75}{84} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{GHG Saving} \approx 89.29\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The rice husk system delivers an **89% GHG abatement** over diesel. This value qualifies the rice husk project for **carbon credit eligibility** under Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) and CDM mechanisms.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Carbon Payback Time for an Energy Crop Plantation

**Problem Statement:** A 100-hectare oil palm plantation is established on degraded grassland (soil C loss = 50 t C/ha). Annual carbon sequestration by the palms = 8 t C/ha/yr (above-ground). Determine the carbon payback time.

**Step 1 — Total Carbon Debt from LUC:**

$$C_{debt} = 100 \text{ ha} \times 50 \text{ t C/ha} = 5000 \text{ t C}$$

Convert to CO₂ equivalent:

$$C_{debt, CO_2} = 5000 \times \frac{44}{12} = 18{,}333.33 \text{ t CO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Annual Carbon Sequestration Rate:**

$$R_{seq} = 100 \text{ ha} \times 8 \text{ t C/ha/yr} = 800 \text{ t C/yr}$$

Convert to CO₂ equivalent:

$$R_{seq, CO_2} = 800 \times \frac{44}{12} = 2933.33 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Carbon Payback Time:**

$$T_{payback} = \frac{C_{debt, CO_2}}{R_{seq, CO_2}} = \frac{18{,}333.33}{2933.33}$$

$$\boxed{T_{payback} \approx 6.25 \text{ years}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A payback time of **6.25 years** is considered highly sustainable (acceptable threshold: ≤ 30 years). However, if the same plantation replaced **primary tropical forest** (soil C loss = 250 t C/ha), the payback time would balloon to **> 31 years**, making it unsustainable.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative LCA of Energy Sources

| Indicator | Coal | Natural Gas | Diesel | Bagasse CHP | Rice Husk Boiler | Biogas CHP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GHG (g CO₂eq/MJ) | 95–105 | 55–65 | 75–85 | 5–15 | 8–20 | 10–25 |
| PM (g/MJ) | 0.04 | 0.001 | 0.02 | 0.03 | 0.05 | 0.005 |
| NOₓ (g/MJ) | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.08 | 0.08 | 0.10 | 0.01 |
| SOₓ (g/MJ) | 0.20 | 0.001 | 0.05 | 0.01 | 0.01 | 0.005 |
| Water footprint (L/MJ) | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.8 | 50–100 | 30–80 | 5–20 |
| Carbon Payback (yr) | — | — | — | 1–3 | 1–2 | 0.5–2 |
| Renewability | No | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Matrix:** Biomass is **not automatically cleaner** than fossil fuels for *local* air pollutants (PM, NOₓ). The decisive advantage is **life-cycle GHG** and **renewability**, not stack emissions alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) Framework for Biomass Energy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete cradle-to-grave LCA boundary** for a biomass energy project in a food industry context. This is the **most important diagram** for KTU Module 2 and is frequently drawn in board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Biomass Cultivation] --> A1[Sunlight CO2 Water Nutrients]
    A1 --> A2[Above-ground Biomass]
    A1 --> A3[Below-ground Roots]
    A --> B[Stage 2: Harvesting Collection]
    B --> B1[Manual Mechanical]
    B1 --> B2[Transport to Plant]
    B2 --> C[Stage 3: Pre-processing]
    C --> C1[Drying]
    C --> C2[Chopping / Pelletizing]
    C --> C3[Storage Silo]
    C3 --> D[Stage 4: Conversion Technology]
    D --> D1[Direct Combustion]
    D --> D2[Gasification]
    D --> D3[Pyrolysis]
    D --> D4[Anaerobic Digestion]
    D --> D5[Fermentation to Bioethanol]
    D1 --> E[Stage 5: Energy Output]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> E1[Heat]
    E --> E2[Electricity]
    E --> E3[Biofuels]
    E --> F[Stage 6: End-use Combustion]
    F --> F1[Biogenic CO2 Release]
    F1 --> G[Carbon Cycle Re-growth]
    G --> A
    A --> H[Environmental Impact Categories]
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    H --> H1[GHG Balance]
    H --> H2[Air Quality]
    H --> H3[Water Use]
    H --> H4[Soil Degradation]
    H --> H5[Biodiversity Loss]
    H --> I[Sustainability Assessment]
    I --> I1[ISO 14040 14044 LCA]
    I --> I2[RED II Compliance]
    I --> I3[Carbon Certification ISCC RSB]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sustainability Decision Matrix

This diagram maps the **decision logic** for determining whether a biomass project is sustainable, applying the **carbon payback + NER + GHG saving** triad.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Biomass Project Evaluation] --> Q1{Feedstock Type}
    Q1 -->|Residues Wastes| P1[High Sustainability Score]
    Q1 -->|Dedicated Energy Crops| Q2{Land Use Type}
    Q1 -->|Forest Wood| Q3{Forest Age Type}
    Q2 -->|Marginal Degraded Land| P2[Acceptable]
    Q2 -->|Prime Agricultural Land| P3[Food vs Fuel Risk]
    Q3 -->|Secondary Plantation| P4[Conditional Approval]
    Q3 -->|Old-growth Primary| F1[Reject Unsustainable]
    P1 --> Check{NER greater than 1}
    P2 --> Check
    P3 --> Check
    P4 --> Check
    F1 --> Stop1[Stop and Reject Project]
    Check -->|Yes| GHG{GHG Saving greater than 50 percent}
    Check -->|No| Stop2[Reject Energy Negative]
    GHG -->|Yes| Payback{Carbon Payback less than 30 yr}
    GHG -->|No| Stop3[Insufficient Climate Benefit]
    Payback -->|Yes| Approve[Approve as Sustainable Biomass Project]
    Payback -->|No| Stop4[Reject Long Payback]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Food Industry Biomass Resource Map

This figure contextualizes biomass feedstocks **available within the food industry value chain**, demonstrating how the food sector becomes a strategic bioenergy hub.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Food Industry Operations] --> B[Plant-based Processing]
    A --> C[Animal-based Processing]
    A --> D[Aquatic Processing]
    B --> B1[Sugar Mills: Bagasse Molasses]
    B --> B2[Rice Mills: Rice Husk Straw]
    B --> B3[Oil Mills: Palm Shell Cake]
    B --> B4[Grain Processing: Husks Spent Grain]
    B --> B5[Fruit Vegetables: Peels Pomace]
    C --> C1[Dairy: Whey Manure]
    C --> C2[Slaughterhouses: Blood Bone Tallow]
    C --> C3[Poultry: Feather Meal Litter]
    D --> D1[Fish: Offal Wastewater]
    D --> D2[Seaweed: Algal Biomass]
    B1 --> E[Bioenergy Conversion]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    B5 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Combustion for Steam]
    E --> E2[Biogas via Anaerobic Digestion]
    E --> E3[Biodiesel from Tallow Oil]
    E --> E4[Bioethanol from Sugars]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** For KTU board exams, examiners often award **2–3 marks** for a well-labeled LCA flow diagram. Memorize the **six-stage LCA boundary** (Cultivation → Harvesting → Pre-processing → Conversion → End-use → Carbon Recycle) as the canonical answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "Carbon Neutrality" in the context of biomass energy. Discuss its limitations with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carbon neutrality of biomass refers to the assumption that the CO₂ released during biomass combustion is fully re-absorbed by the next generation of biomass through photosynthesis, resulting in a net-zero CO₂ contribution to the atmosphere over one rotation cycle.

**Limitations:**
- The neutrality assumption ignores **fossil energy inputs** in cultivation (fertilizers, diesel, irrigation).
- It fails for **land-use change** scenarios, especially when forests or peatlands are converted to energy plantations.
- It does not account for **non-CO₂ GHGs** like CH₄ from incomplete combustion and N₂O from fertilizers.

*Example:* Burning rice husk (annual residue) is essentially carbon-neutral, but burning wood from old-growth forests releases carbon that takes 60–100 years to re-sequester — clearly unsustainable.

---

#### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain any three Key Sustainability Indicators (KPIs) for biomass energy systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** Ratio of energy output to fossil energy input. A sustainable biomass system must have NER > 1; values > 5 indicate highly sustainable systems.

2. **GHG Saving (%):** Percentage reduction in life-cycle GHG emissions compared to the fossil fuel baseline. EU RED II mandates minimum 50–70% saving for biomass to qualify as renewable.

3. **Carbon Payback Time (years):** Time required for a biomass plantation to re-sequester the carbon released by land-use change. Sustainable systems must achieve payback within 20–30 years.

*(Optional 4th indicator: Particulate Matter emissions per MJ of useful energy — must comply with WHO and EU emission standards.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** methodology for evaluating the environmental sustainability of a biomass energy project, highlighting the ISO 14040/14044 framework stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food processing unit generates 800 kg/day of food waste, which is sent to an anaerobic digester. The digester produces 120 m³/day of biogas (65% CH₄) and consumes 15 kWh/day of electricity. Calculate the **Net Energy Ratio (NER)** and comment on sustainability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The LCA methodology for biomass sustainability follows **ISO 14040/14044** and comprises four iterative phases:

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope Definition (2 marks)**
- Define functional unit (e.g., 1 MJ of useful heat or 1 kWh of electricity).
- Define system boundary (cradle-to-grave, cradle-to-gate, or gate-to-gate).
- Identify impact categories: GHG, eutrophication, acidification, water depletion.

**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) (2 marks)**
- Quantify all inputs (energy, water, fertilizers) and outputs (emissions, co-products) across stages:
  - Stage 1: Cultivation inputs (N, P, K, pesticides, diesel, irrigation water).
  - Stage 2: Harvesting and transport fuel.
  - Stage 3: Pre-processing electricity (drying, pelletizing).
  - Stage 4: Conversion technology efficiency.
  - Stage 5: Stack emissions (CO₂, CH₄, NOₓ, PM, SOₓ).
  - Stage 6: End-use emissions and disposal.

**Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) (2 marks)**
- Convert inventory data to impact categories:
  - **Climate Change:** kg CO₂eq/MJ using IPCC AR5 GWP factors.
  - **Respiratory Inorganics:** kg PM₂.₅eq/MJ.
  - **Acidification:** kg SO₂eq/MJ.
  - **Land Use:** m²·year/MJ.
- Apply characterization, normalization, and weighting.

**Phase 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)**
- Identify significant contributors (hotspot analysis).
- Conduct sensitivity analysis on key parameters.
- Issue recommendations: improve cultivation efficiency, switch to residues, add pollution control.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Energy Output from Biogas:**

LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³ (at NTP).

$$E_{out} = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.65 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

$$E_{out} = 2792.4 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{out} = 2792.4 \div 3.6 = 775.67 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Energy Input:**

$$E_{in} = 15 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute NER:**

$$\text{NER} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{775.67}{15}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NER} \approx 51.71}$$

**Step 4 — Sustainability Comment (1 mark):**

With NER ≈ 51.7, the digester is a **highly sustainable system** — a hallmark of food industry AD plants. Such a system:
- Avoids methane emissions from landfilling (each kg of food waste would otherwise emit ~ 0.5 m³ CH₄).
- Produces renewable electricity and heat.
- Generates nutrient-rich digestate as biofertilizer, closing the nutrient loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to convert m³ CH₄ to MJ using **LHV (35.8 MJ/m³)**, not HHV (39.8 MJ/m³). Using the wrong calorific value loses 1 mark. Also, do **not** apply stoichiometric air or combustion temperatures here — this is anaerobic digestion, **not combustion**.

---

#### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **"Food vs Fuel" debate** in biomass energy with reference to first-generation and second-generation biofuels. Suggest three engineering strategies to mitigate the food-versus-fuel conflict. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rice mill generates 2 tonnes/day of rice husk. The husk is burned in a boiler with NCV = 14 MJ/kg and boiler efficiency 70%. Calculate the daily steam energy output and the avoided CO₂ emissions if the same energy were produced from a diesel-fired boiler (diesel NCV = 42 MJ/kg, CO₂ emission factor = 74 g CO₂eq/MJ). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**The Food vs Fuel Debate (4 marks):**

The Food vs Fuel debate concerns whether arable land should be used to grow energy crops (fuel) or food crops (food). It gained prominence after the 2007–2008 food price crisis, when large-scale biofuel mandates (US Ethanol Program, EU Biofuels Directive) coincided with rising maize and wheat prices.

**First-generation biofuels** (sugarcane ethanol, corn ethanol, palm oil biodiesel, soy biodiesel) compete directly with food production because they use food crops as feedstocks. This causes:
- Rising global food prices.
- Deforestation (e.g., Indonesian palm oil expansion into rainforest).
- Water scarcity from irrigation-intensive crops.
- Soil nutrient depletion.

**Second-generation biofuels** (cellulosic ethanol from rice straw, bagasse gasification, algae biodiesel, waste-to-energy) mitigate these concerns by using:
- Agricultural residues and forestry waste.
- Non-food energy crops grown on marginal lands.
- Municipal solid waste and food waste.

**Mitigation Strategies (3 marks):**
1. **Promote 2nd and 3rd-gen biofuels** using residues, lignocellulose, algae — these do not displace food crops.
2. **Use degraded/marginal lands** for energy plantations rather than prime cropland.
3. **Co-product utilization** — extract protein from distillers' grains (DDGS), or use oil cake as animal feed, maximizing the value of every hectare.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Energy Input from Rice Husk:**

$$m_{husk} = 2000 \text{ kg/day}, \quad NCV = 14 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$E_{input} = 2000 \times 14 = 28{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Useful Steam Energy Output:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = 0.70$$

$$E_{steam} = 28{,}000 \times 0.70 = 19{,}600 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Diesel Required:**

$$m_{diesel} = \frac{19{,}600 \text{ MJ/day}}{42 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 466.67 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Avoided CO₂ from Diesel Substitution:**

$$E_{CO_2, avoided} = 19{,}600 \text{ MJ/day} \times 74 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/MJ}$$

$$E_{CO_2, avoided} = 1{,}450{,}400 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{CO_2, avoided} \approx 1.45 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{eq/day}}$$

**Step 5 — Sustainability Interpretation (1 mark):**

Avoiding 1.45 t CO₂/day translates to **~ 530 tonnes CO₂eq/year** — equivalent to removing ~ 115 passenger vehicles from the road. The rice husk boiler is a textbook example of a **circular bioeconomy** intervention in the food industry, with NER ≫ 1 and high GHG abatement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not** compute CO₂ from the rice husk combustion itself — the question asks for **avoided CO₂** from the diesel baseline. Awarding full credit requires using the diesel emission factor (74 g/MJ) with the **steam energy output** (19,600 MJ), not the husk input energy. Wrong use of $E_{input}$ loses 2 marks.
> - **Unit consistency:** Keep MJ throughout. Do not mix kWh and MJ mid-calculation.
> - **CO₂ emission factor for diesel:** Must be cited as 74 g CO₂eq/MJ per IPCC/DEFRA; using 3.2 kg CO₂/L (which is also valid) requires showing the conversion to MJ.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Biomass is NOT automatically carbon-neutral** — must be proved via LCA with system boundaries clearly defined.
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Sustainability:** Environmental, Social (Food Security), Economic.
- [ ] **Carbon Neutrality Equation:** $C_{released} = C_{re-sequestered}$ within one rotation cycle.
- [ ] **GWP Constants to Memorize:** CO₂ = **1**, CH₄ = **28**, N₂O = **265** (IPCC AR5, 100-year).
- [ ] **Combustion Stoichiometry:** $C_{frac}$ of dry biomass ≈ **0.45–0.50**; molar conversion factor **44/12**.
- [ ] **Net Energy Ratio (NER):** Must be **> 1**; food industry AD plants routinely achieve **> 20**.
- [ ] **GHG Saving Threshold:** EU RED II mandates **≥ 50%** (existing plants) and **≥ 70%** (new plants from 2021).
- [ ] **Carbon Payback:** Acceptable if **≤ 30 years**; old-growth forest LUC payback can exceed 100 years — REJECT.
- [ ] **Food vs Fuel Resolution:** Use **residues, wastes, marginal lands, and 2nd/3rd-gen feedstocks**.
- [ ] **Sustainable Residue Removal:** Maximum **30–40%** to prevent soil erosion and SOC depletion.
- [ ] **Six-Stage LCA Boundary:** Cultivation → Harvesting → Pre-processing → Conversion → End-use → Carbon Recycle.
- [ ] **Key Emission Limits (Biomass Boilers):** PM < 30 mg/Nm³, NOₓ < 250 mg/Nm³, CO < 250 mg/Nm³ (EU IED).
- [ ] **Combustion Temperature Rule:** > **850 °C** with residence time > **2 s** to destroy dioxins and VOCs.
- [ ] **Common LHV Values (must memorize):** Wood ≈ 17 MJ/kg, Rice Husk ≈ 14 MJ/kg, Bagasse ≈ 19 MJ/kg, Biogas ≈ 20 MJ/m³, Methane ≈ 35.8 MJ/m³.
- [ ] **Food Industry Biomass Resources:** Bagasse, rice husk, coconut shell, palm shell cake, spent grain, whey, tallow, food waste, molasses, fruit pomace.
- [ ] **Certification Systems:** **ISCC** (International Sustainability & Carbon Certification), **RSB** (Roundtable on Sustainable Biomaterials), **Bonsucro** (sugarcane).
- [ ] **Water Footprint:** Bioethanol ~ **500–3000 L/L** vs. gasoline ~ **100 L/L** — water is the hidden cost.
- [ ] **Equivalence Ratio:** ER < 1 (fuel-rich), ER = 1 (stoichiometric), ER > 1 (lean) — biomass boilers typically operate at ER = 1.2–1.5.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Environmental Considerations and Sustainability Aspects of Biomass Energy**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Management Systems (EnMS) and Standards
Overview of ISO 50001:2018 Standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Overview of ISO 50001:2018 Standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ISO 50001:2018** is the second edition of the international standard titled *"Energy management systems — Requirements with guidance for use,"* published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in August 2018. It supersedes the first edition (ISO 50001:2011). The standard specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an **Energy Management System (EnMS)**, the intended outcome of which is to enable an organization to take a **systematic approach** to achieving continual improvement of **energy performance** and the **energy management system**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives. > [!NOTE] > **Why ISO 50001:2018 matters in the Food Industry:** > The food processing industry is one of the most energy-intensive manufacturing sectors — using significant thermal energy for **boiling, pasteurization, sterilization, drying, baking**, and electrical energy for **refrigeration, cold storage, milling, pumping, and packaging**. ISO 50001:2018 provides the *structural roadmap* to systematically reduce this energy intensity while maintaining product quality and food safety. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have decided to **lose 10 kg of body weight** in 6 months. What would you do? 1. You would **measure** your current weight (Energy Baseline). 2. You would **plan** a diet and exercise routine (Energy Objectives & Action Plans). 3. You would **do** — actually follow the plan (Implementation & Operation). 4. You would **check** your progress every week by weighing yourself (Monitoring, Measurement & Analysis). 5. You would **act** on what you learned — adjust the diet if not working (Continual Improvement). 6. You would also get a **doctor's checkup** every quarter to make sure your plan is healthy (Internal Audit & Management Review). **ISO 50001:2018 works exactly the same way — but for the "energy health" of a factory, bakery, dairy plant, or food processing unit.** Instead of a weighing scale, the organization uses **meters and Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**. Instead of a diet plan, it uses an **Energy Action Plan**. Instead of a doctor, it uses an **internal auditor and top management review**. > [!TIP] > **One-line memory hook for exams:** > "**ISO 50001:2018 is the PDCA wheel that turns the EnMS bicycle of an organization toward lower energy use and better energy performance.**" --- ### 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Memorize | Term | Definition (as per ISO 50001:2018) | |---|---| | **Energy Performance** | Measurable result(s) related to energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption. | | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | Quantitative value or measure of energy performance as defined by the organization. | | **Energy Baseline (EnB)** | Quantitative reference(s) providing a basis for comparison of energy performance. | | **Significant Energy Use (SEU)** | Energy use accounting for substantial energy consumption and/or offering considerable potential for energy performance improvement. | | **Energy Review** | Determination of the organization's energy profile, including Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs), based on data and other information, identification of opportunities for improvement. | | **Energy Objective** | Result or outcome specified by the organization's energy policy that is to be achieved. | | **Energy Target** | Quantified value or metric of an energy objective. | | **Energy Management System (EnMS)** | Management system used to establish and achieve energy objectives and energy targets. | | **HLS / Harmonized Structure** | The 10-clause common structure shared by all modern ISO management system standards. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ### 2.1 The Big Picture: The PDCA Cycle at the Heart of ISO 50001:2018 ISO 50001:2018 is built on the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle**, also called the **Deming Cycle**. This is the same engine that drives ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 14001 (Environment). The reason is that ISO has aligned all modern management standards to a single **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)**. > [!NOTE] > **PDCA applied to EnMS:** > - **Plan** → Establish the energy policy, objectives, targets, action plans, energy review, EnPIs, and EnB. > - **Do** → Implement the action plans, train people, operate controls, manage SEUs. > - **Check** → Monitor, measure, analyze energy performance; conduct internal audits. > - **Act** → Management review, take corrective actions, drive continual improvement. --- ### 2.2 The 10 Clauses of ISO 50001:2018 (HLS Structure) | Clause | Title | PDCA Stage | Core Requirement | |:---:|---|:---:|---| | 1 | Scope | — | Defines applicability of the standard. | | 2 | Normative References | — | Refers to ISO/IEC directives. | | 3 | Terms and Definitions | — | Vocabulary used. | | 4 | **Context of the Organization** | **Plan** | Identify internal/external issues, needs of interested parties, scope of EnMS. | | 5 | **Leadership** | **Plan** | Top management commitment, energy policy, roles & responsibilities. | | 6 | **Planning** | **Plan** | Risks & opportunities, energy review, SEUs, EnPIs, EnB, objectives, action plans. | | 7 | **Support** | **Do** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. | | 8 | **Operation** | **Do** | Operational planning & control, design, procurement, SEU management. | | 9 | **Performance Evaluation** | **Check** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation of compliance, internal audit, management review. | | 10 | **Improvement** | **Act** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clauses 4–10 are auditable (the actual EnMS requirements). Clauses 1–3 are introductory. Any KTU question asking for "the structure of ISO 50001" almost always expects this 10-clause table.** --- ### 2.3 Key Concept: The Energy Review (Clause 6.3) The **Energy Review** is the *single most important planning activity* in ISO 50001:2018. It involves: 1. **Analysis of energy use and consumption** based on measured data. 2. **Identification of SEUs** — energy uses with high consumption or high improvement potential. 3. **Determination of EnPIs** — suitable performance indicators for each SEU. 4. **Establishment of EnB(s)** — the reference baseline(s). 5. **Identification of opportunities for improvement** and prioritization. --- ### 2.4 The EnPI–EnB–Energy Performance Relationship (The Core Mathematical Engine) Energy performance is **not** just "lower kWh." It is the relationship between energy consumption and a relevant **normalizing variable** (e.g., tonnes of product, hours of operation, °C-day of cooling load). The simplest normalized EnPI formula is: $$ \text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{Energy Consumption}}{\text{Production Output}} $$ > [!TIP] > **Food industry example:** In a dairy plant, the EnPI is often **kWh per tonne of milk processed** or **MJ per kg of pasteurized milk**. The EnB is the value of this EnPI during a defined baseline period (e.g., the average of 2022–2023). When multiple variables affect energy use (e.g., ambient temperature, throughput, raw material moisture), a **linear regression model** is used to normalize the EnPI: $$ E = a + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \ldots + b_n x_n $$ where: - $E$ = energy consumption in the baseline period, - $a$ = baseline constant, - $b_i$ = regression coefficient for variable $x_i$, - $x_i$ = normalizing variable (e.g., production volume, degree-days). The **Energy Performance Improvement** is then calculated as: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline period}} - \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting period}}}{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline period}}} \times 100 $$ --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | One-line Meaning | Food Industry Example | |---|---|---| | **EnMS** | The total management system for energy. | Whole-company energy policy of an F&B manufacturer. | | **Energy Policy** | Top-level commitment statement. | "Reduce specific energy consumption by 10% in 3 years." | | **Energy Review** | First-time deep analysis of energy. | Mapping every boiler, chiller, oven, motor. | | **SEU** | Energy uses worth tracking. | Steam boiler, ammonia refrigeration plant, hot oil heater. | | **EnPI** | The metric to track energy performance. | MJ / kg of bakery product; kWh / m³ of cold storage. | | **EnB** | The reference point for EnPI. | Average EnPI of FY 2022–2023. | | **Energy Objective** | What you want to achieve. | "Install VFD on cooling tower pumps." | | **Energy Target** | The numeric value of the objective. | "Achieve 0.85 kWh/tonne-cooling by FY 2026." | | **PDCA** | The cycle that drives continual improvement. | Plan a project → Execute → Measure → Improve. | | **HLS** | The 10-clause structure of all modern ISO MSS. | Same as ISO 9001 / ISO 14001 clause structure. | | **Continual Improvement** | Recurring process of enhancing EnPI. | Year-on-year reduction in kWh/tonne. | | **Internal Audit** | Self-check against the standard. | Annual audit by trained internal auditors. | | **Management Review** | Top management evaluates the EnMS. | Quarterly review by MD/Plant Head. | | **Energy Action Plan** | Documented plan to achieve objectives. | Action plan with deadline, owner, budget. | | **Normalizing Variable** | Variable used to make EnPI comparable. | Production volume, degree-day, humidity. | --- ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering In a food industry context, ISO 50001:2018: - Provides a **structured methodology** to reduce Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in high-load areas like **dairy, brewing, sugar, bakery, edible oil, and meat processing**. - Enables **integration with ISO 14001 (Environment)** and **ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 (Food Safety)** — a single audit, one management system, reduced cost. - Qualifies an organization for **government incentives, tax rebates, and carbon credits** in many jurisdictions. - Is a **legal/contractual enabler** — many multinational buyers demand ISO 50001 certification from suppliers. - Aligns with **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** reporting, increasingly required by stock exchanges and global buyers. - Facilitates **Net-Zero roadmaps** and **Scope 1, 2, 3 GHG accounting**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this is a high-yield KTU topic:** The food industry is energy-intensive, and the course itself (*Energy Management in Food Industry*) is essentially an applied ISO 50001 module. Expect a 14-mark question linking EnPI/EnB/SEU to a real food plant scenario. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: EnMS Establishment & EnPI Normalization ### 3.1 The Step-by-Step Process of Establishing an EnMS in a Food Plant Below is the **exhaustive 12-step roadmap** a food processing organization must follow to implement ISO 50001:2018. Each step is mapped to the relevant clause. **Step 1 — Define the Context and Scope (Clause 4):** Identify the physical/organizational boundary (e.g., "Dairy Plant A, including reception, pasteurization, homogenization, packaging, and cold storage, but excluding the administration block and outsourced logistics"). **Step 2 — Identify Interested Parties and Their Needs (Clause 4.2):** These may include: top management, employees, statutory bodies (Pollution Control Board, Electrical Inspector), customers (who demand low-carbon products), suppliers of energy, and the community. **Step 3 — Determine the Scope and Boundaries of the EnMS (Clause 4.3):** Define what is *in scope* (e.g., boiler house, refrigeration plant, compressed air) and what is *out of scope* (e.g., residential colony of the company). **Step 4 — Establish the Energy Policy (Clause 5.2):** The Managing Director signs a one-page policy committing to: - Energy performance improvement. - Provision of information and resources. - Compliance with legal and other requirements. - Continual improvement. **Step 5 — Assign Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities (Clause 5.3):** Appoint a **Management Representative (MR)** — usually the Chief Engineer — and an **Energy Manager** with a cross-functional Energy Team. **Step 6 — Conduct the Energy Review (Clause 6.3):** - Collect at least 12 months of energy data (electricity bills, fuel bills, steam meter logs). - Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) for every process. - Identify **SEUs**. - Determine initial **EnPIs** and **EnB**. **Step 7 — Identify and Evaluate Risks & Opportunities (Clause 6.1):** - Risks: increasing electricity tariff, equipment failure, fuel supply disruption. - Opportunities: VFD installation, waste heat recovery, solar PV, biomass boiler. **Step 8 — Establish Energy Objectives and Targets (Clause 6.4):** - Objective: "Reduce specific energy consumption in pasteurization." - Target: "From 0.45 kWh/tonne to 0.38 kWh/tonne within 24 months." **Step 9 — Develop Energy Action Plans (Clause 6.5):** Each target gets a plan with: actions, resources, responsibilities, timeline, and method of verification. **Step 10 — Implement and Operate (Clause 8):** - Operational controls for SEUs. - Maintenance schedules. - Calibration of meters. - Design and procurement criteria for energy-efficient equipment. **Step 11 — Check — Performance Evaluation (Clause 9):** - Monitor and measure EnPIs. - Evaluate legal compliance. - Conduct internal audits. - Conduct management review. **Step 12 — Act — Improvement (Clause 10):** - Address nonconformities. - Take corrective actions. - Drive continual improvement. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: EnPI Normalization Using Linear Regression **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):** > A dairy plant records the following monthly data for its **pasteurization section** for the *baseline year (2023)*. The plant wants to establish an **Energy Baseline (EnB)** and an **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** for the section. | Month | Energy $E$ (MWh) | Production $P$ (tonnes) | Avg. ambient temp $T$ (°C) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Jan | 320 | 1100 | 18 | | Feb | 305 | 1080 | 20 | | Mar | 340 | 1200 | 24 | | Apr | 360 | 1280 | 28 | | May | 395 | 1350 | 32 | | Jun | 420 | 1400 | 35 | | Jul | 445 | 1420 | 36 | | Aug | 440 | 1400 | 35 | | Sep | 400 | 1320 | 31 | | Oct | 360 | 1250 | 28 | | Nov | 330 | 1150 | 22 | | Dec | 310 | 1100 | 19 | A simple **linear regression model** is proposed: $$ E = a + b_1 P + b_2 T $$ Using least-squares regression (or, in exam practice, assuming the regression has already been solved and the coefficients are given), the result is: $$ a = 50.0, \quad b_1 = 0.20, \quad b_2 = 2.50 $$ So the regression model is: $$ E = 50.0 + 0.20 \cdot P + 2.50 \cdot T $$ Units: - $E$ in MWh, - $P$ in tonnes of milk, - $T$ in °C, - $a$ in MWh (intercept), - $b_1$ in MWh per tonne, - $b_2$ in MWh per °C. **Now, in the reporting year (2024), one particular month (say, May 2024) has:** - Actual energy consumed: $E_{\text{actual}} = 380$ MWh, - Production: $P = 1380$ tonnes, - Avg. ambient temp: $T = 33$ °C. **Step 1 — Calculate the expected (baseline-model-predicted) energy:** $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 50.0 + (0.20)(1380) + (2.50)(33) $$ $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 50.0 + 276.0 + 82.5 $$ $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 408.5 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the adjusted energy performance (Energy Performance Improvement %):** The **% improvement in energy performance** is calculated by: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{408.5 - 380}{408.5} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{28.5}{408.5} \times 100 \approx 6.98\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** Because $E_{\text{actual}} < E_{\text{expected}}$, the plant consumed **less energy than the baseline model predicted** for the same production and ambient conditions. This is a **6.98 % energy performance improvement** — the plant has genuinely improved, not merely produced less. **Step 3 — Verify by checking the EnPI (specific energy consumption):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}}}{P} = \frac{408.5}{1380} = 0.296 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P} = \frac{380}{1380} = 0.275 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{0.296 - 0.275}{0.296} \times 100 \approx 7.09\% $$ The small difference between 6.98 % and 7.09 % arises from rounding. Both methods are correct and accepted under ISO 50001:2018. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two methods?** > - **Method 1** (expected vs actual energy) accounts for the effect of the normalizing variables ($P$ and $T$) on energy. > - **Method 2** (EnPI ratio) is simpler and acceptable when the EnPI is well-chosen. > In KTU answers, **show both** for full marks. --- ### 3.3 Common KTU Pitfalls in EnPI Calculations (and how to avoid them) | Pitfall | Why it loses marks | Correct approach | |---|---|---| | Using **total energy** as the EnPI. | Ignores changes in production volume. | Use **specific energy** (energy / production) as EnPI. | | **Forgetting normalizing variables** (e.g., ambient temp for cooling load). | Overstates or understates improvement. | Use **regression** or **CUSUM** techniques. | | Computing improvement on a **non-representative baseline**. | A bad month becomes the comparison point. | Use **12-month rolling average** or **average of 3 years**. | | **Conflating energy use reduction with energy performance improvement.** | Energy use can drop simply because production dropped. | Energy performance must be **normalized**. | | Using **one EnPI for the whole plant** when SEUs differ. | A single EnPI hides poor performance in some areas. | Define **separate EnPIs for each SEU**. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PDCA Cycle Mapped to ISO 50001:2018 Clauses ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PLAN
Clause 4 Context
Clause 5 Leadership
Clause 6 Planning"] --> B["DO
Clause 7 Support
Clause 8 Operation"] B --> C["CHECK
Clause 9 Performance Evaluation"] C --> D["ACT
Clause 10 Improvement"] D --> A A:::plan B:::do C:::check D:::act classDef plan fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 classDef do fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef check fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef act fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 EnMS Architecture: From Policy to Continual Improvement ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Energy Policy
Top Management Commitment"] --> R["Roles and Responsibilities
MR, Energy Manager, Team"] R --> ER["Energy Review
Data Collection and Analysis"] ER --> SEU["Identify Significant Energy Uses SEU"] SEU --> ENPI["Define EnPIs and EnB"] ENPI --> OBJ["Energy Objectives and Targets"] OBJ --> AP["Energy Action Plans"] AP --> OP["Operation and Operational Controls"] OP --> MON["Monitoring and Measurement"] MON --> AUD["Internal Audit"] AUD --> MR2["Management Review"] MR2 --> CI["Continual Improvement"] CI --> OBJ ER2["Risks and Opportunities"] --> AP classDef policy fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef review fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 classDef act fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef monitor fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 class P,R policy class ER,SEU,ENPI,OBJ,AP,ER2 review class OP,MR2,CI act class MON,AUD monitor ``` --- ### 4.3 EnPI/EnB/SEU Relationship Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR EU["Energy Use
kWh, MJ, m3 gas"] --> SEU1["SEU 1
Boiler"] EU --> SEU2["SEU 2
Refrigeration"] EU --> SEU3["SEU 3
Compressed Air"] SEU1 --> ENPI1["EnPI 1
MJ per kg steam"] SEU2 --> ENPI2["EnPI 2
kWh per tonne cooling"] SEU3 --> ENPI3["EnPI 3
m3 per kWh generated"] ENPI1 --> ENB1["EnB 1
Baseline value of EnPI 1"] ENPI2 --> ENB2["EnB 2
Baseline value of EnPI 2"] ENPI3 --> ENB3["EnB 3
Baseline value of EnPI 3"] ENB1 --> CI["Compare to EnB
Compute Improvement"] ENB2 --> CI ENB3 --> CI classDef seuc fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000 classDef enpic fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000 classDef enbc fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000 classDef resultc fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class SEU1,SEU2,SEU3 seuc class ENPI1,ENPI2,ENPI3 enpic class ENB1,ENB2,ENB3 enbc class CI resultc ``` --- ### 4.4 ISO 50001:2018 Integration with Other Food Industry Standards ```mermaid flowchart TB ISO50001["ISO 50001:2018
Energy Management"] --> ISO14001["ISO 14001:2015
Environmental Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO9001["ISO 9001:2015
Quality Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO22000["ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000
Food Safety Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO45001["ISO 45001:2018
Occupational Health and Safety"] ISO14001 --> HLS["Common HLS Framework
10 Clause Structure"] ISO9001 --> HLS ISO22000 --> HLS ISO45001 --> HLS HLS --> IMS["Integrated Management System IMS
Single Audit, Lower Cost, Holistic View"] classDef en fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef fs fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef hls fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 classDef ims fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 class ISO50001,ISO14001,ISO9001 en class ISO22000,ISO45001 fs class HLS hls class IMS ims ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Define the term "Energy Management System (EnMS)" as per ISO 50001:2018. List any four elements of an EnMS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements used by an organization to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives (definition – 1.5 marks). > > Four elements of an EnMS are (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks, list any four): > 1. Energy policy > 2. Energy objectives and targets > 3. Energy review (including SEUs, EnPIs, EnB) > 4. Energy action plans > 5. Operational controls and procedures > 6. Monitoring, measurement, and analysis > 7. Internal audit and management review > 8. Continual improvement process --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Distinguish between EnPI and EnB. Give one example from the food industry.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator) | EnB (Energy Baseline) | |---|---|---| | Meaning | Quantitative **measure** of energy performance. | Quantitative **reference value** providing a basis for comparison. | | Role | Tracks energy performance over time. | The benchmark against which EnPI is compared. | | Example (Dairy) | **kWh per tonne of pasteurized milk.** | **0.42 kWh/tonne** (the average of FY 2022–2023). | (2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for example.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice > **Module 3 Choice Question (14 marks)** > Attempt **any one** of the following: --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to ISO 50001:2018. Map each PDCA stage to the relevant clauses of the standard. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)** of ISO 50001:2018. Why is this structure important for integrating EnMS with other ISO management systems in a food industry? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 50001:2018 (7 marks):** The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle** is the foundational management methodology of ISO 50001:2018. It ensures a structured and repeatable approach to energy performance improvement (1 mark). | PDCA Stage | Activity in EnMS | ISO 50001 Clause | |---|---|---| | **Plan** | Establish energy policy, conduct energy review, identify SEUs, determine EnPIs and EnB, set objectives and targets, develop action plans. | Clauses **4, 5, 6** (Context, Leadership, Planning). | | **Do** | Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information; implement operational controls, design, procurement. | Clauses **7, 8** (Support, Operation). | | **Check** | Monitor & measure energy performance, evaluate compliance, conduct internal audit, conduct management review. | Clause **9** (Performance Evaluation). | | **Act** | Address nonconformities, take corrective action, drive continual improvement. | Clause **10** (Improvement). | (1 mark per row + 1 mark for explanation of iterative nature = 7 marks.) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Many students *list* PDCA but forget to **map the clauses explicitly**. That costs 2–3 marks. Always include a clause-to-stage mapping table. **(b) Harmonized Structure (HLS) of ISO 50001:2018 (7 marks):** The **Harmonized Structure (HLS)** — sometimes called Annex SL — is a **10-clause template** adopted by ISO for all its management system standards (MSS). ISO 50001:2018, ISO 9001:2015, ISO 14001:2015, and ISO 45001:2018 all use the *same* 10-clause structure (1 mark). **The 10 Clauses (2 marks):** 1. Scope 2. Normative References 3. Terms and Definitions 4. Context of the Organization 5. Leadership 6. Planning 7. Support 8. Operation 9. Performance Evaluation 10. Improvement **Importance for food industry integration (4 marks):** 1. **Single audit, lower cost** — A dairy or bakery unit can integrate ISO 50001 with ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 (food safety) and ISO 14001 (environment) and conduct a single combined audit. This saves 30–40 % of certification cost. 2. **Unified documentation** — Common structure means common procedures, common forms, common manuals. Reduces paperwork for a food plant where documentation is already heavy (FSSAI, HACCP, etc.). 3. **Holistic improvement** — Energy, environment, quality, safety, and food safety become a **single Integrated Management System (IMS)**. Decisions (e.g., changing a refrigerant) can simultaneously address GHG reduction, ozone depletion, energy efficiency, and food safety. 4. **Common language for top management** — The HLS forces all standards to use identical terms like "context," "risks and opportunities," "leadership," "documented information." A single Management Review can cover all systems. 5. **Easier continual improvement** — Since all systems follow PDCA, a single corrective action can address energy, quality, environment, and safety issues at once. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Students often write "HLS has 7 clauses" or "HLS is in ISO 14001 only" — both are wrong. State clearly: **HLS has 10 clauses, applies to all modern ISO MSS, and was introduced via ISO/IEC Directives Part 1 Annex SL.** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Define **SEU, EnPI, and EnB**. Explain the **Energy Review** process as required by Clause 6.3 of ISO 50001:2018. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food processing unit has the following data for its **refrigeration section** for the baseline year: - Total energy consumed (E) = 1200 MWh, - Total production = 8000 tonnes of frozen product, - Energy Baseline (EnB) = 0.15 MWh/tonne. In the reporting year, with production = 8500 tonnes and energy consumed = 1180 MWh: 1. Calculate the **EnPI for both years** and the **% improvement** in energy performance. 2. Explain whether this reflects a **genuine energy performance improvement**. If not, suggest the correct method to assess improvement when production and other variables change. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions + Energy Review (7 marks):** **Definitions (3 marks = 1 mark each):** - **Significant Energy Use (SEU):** An energy use that accounts for substantial energy consumption and/or offers considerable potential for energy performance improvement. - **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI):** A quantitative value or measure of energy performance, as defined by the organization (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg). - **Energy Baseline (EnB):** A quantitative reference providing a basis for comparison of energy performance. **Energy Review Process (Clause 6.3) — 4 marks:** 1. **Analysis of energy use and consumption** — collect historical data (minimum 12 months), map all energy sources (electricity, fuel oil, LPG, biomass, steam). 2. **Identification of SEUs** — rank energy uses by consumption and improvement potential. 3. **Determination of EnPIs** — select appropriate EnPIs for each SEU, including normalizing variables (production, throughput, ambient temperature, humidity). 4. **Establishment of EnB(s)** — define the baseline period (e.g., average of last 3 years or a representative year) and document the baseline EnPI values. 5. **Identification of opportunities for improvement** — list energy-saving opportunities, rank them by payback period, technical feasibility, and energy-saving potential. (Allocate 0.8 mark per step.) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Do **not** write a generic "energy audit" procedure. ISO 50001:2018 calls it the **Energy Review**, and it must include **SEU identification + EnPI selection + EnB establishment + opportunity identification**. Missing any one of these 4 elements costs 1 mark. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Calculate EnPI for baseline year (1 mark):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{E_{\text{baseline}}}{P_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{1200 \text{ MWh}}{8000 \text{ tonnes}} = 0.150 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ (This equals the EnB, as expected.) **Step 2 — Calculate EnPI for reporting year (1 mark):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting}} = \frac{E_{\text{reporting}}}{P_{\text{reporting}}} = \frac{1180 \text{ MWh}}{8500 \text{ tonnes}} = 0.1388 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate % improvement (1 mark):** $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting}}}{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 $$ $$ = \frac{0.150 - 0.1388}{0.150} \times 100 = \frac{0.0112}{0.150} \times 100 \approx 7.47\% $$ **Step 4 — Is this genuine improvement? (2 marks):** > **Yes, this is a genuine improvement.** The EnPI has dropped from 0.150 to 0.1388 MWh/tonne. Importantly, the production **increased** from 8000 to 8500 tonnes, yet the absolute energy consumption **decreased** from 1200 to 1180 MWh. Both indicators point to the same conclusion — the refrigeration section has become more energy-efficient per tonne of product. **Step 5 — Method when multiple variables change (2 marks):** If, in a different scenario, production had decreased (say, 7000 tonnes → 6000 tonnes) and energy had also decreased, the **simple EnPI would falsely show improvement**. In that case, ISO 50001:2018 recommends: 1. **Use multiple normalizing variables** in the EnPI — e.g., kWh per tonne AND kWh per degree-day of cooling load. 2. **Use a linear regression model** to predict expected energy at the new operating conditions, and then compare actual energy with predicted energy: $$ E_{\text{expected}} = a + b_1 P + b_2 T $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ 3. **Use CUSUM (cumulative sum) techniques** for tracking persistent changes. 4. **Re-baseline** if there is a permanent change in process, equipment, or product mix — but **only** after documenting the change and re-establishing the EnB. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout (numerical):** Two very common mistakes: > 1. Students **subtract EnPIs directly** (0.150 − 0.1388 = 0.0112) and call that the "improvement." The correct method is to **divide by the baseline EnPI** to get a *percentage*. A 0.0112 MWh/tonne improvement means different things at different baselines. > 2. Students ignore the production increase and call it a "raw energy reduction." Always emphasize **specific** energy, not absolute energy. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **The 15 must-remember points for ISO 50001:2018 in the food industry:** - [x] **ISO 50001:2018** is the **second edition**, published in **August 2018**, replacing ISO 50001:2011. - [x] It is built on the **PDCA cycle** (Deming cycle) and the **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)**. - [x] **Clauses 4–10 are the auditable requirements**; Clauses 1–3 are introductory. - [x] **Plan** = Clauses 4, 5, 6. **Do** = Clauses 7, 8. **Check** = Clause 9. **Act** = Clause 10. - [x] **EnMS** = Energy Management System — the totality of the management system for energy. - [x] **Energy Policy** must be signed by **top management** (a Clause 5 leadership requirement). - [x] **Energy Review** (Clause 6.3) is the foundation of the entire EnMS — it produces **SEUs, EnPIs, and EnB**. - [x] **SEU** = Significant Energy Use — the energy uses worth tracking and managing. - [x] **EnPI** = Energy Performance Indicator — the *metric* (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg). - [x] **EnB** = Energy Baseline — the *reference value* of the EnPI in the baseline period. - [x] Energy performance = **normalized energy use** — never just absolute kWh. - [x] The standard allows **regression-based normalization** for multiple variables: $$ E = a + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \ldots + b_n x_n $$ - [x] Improvement % is calculated as: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ - [x] ISO 50001:2018 **integrates seamlessly** with **ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, and ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000** through the common HLS — a major advantage for food industry. - [x] Food industry SEUs typically include: **steam boiler, refrigeration plant, compressed air, electric motors, ovens, dryers, and cold storage**. - [x] **Continual improvement** is the *intended outcome* of the entire EnMS — measured by a sustained year-on-year reduction in EnPIs. - [x] **Internal audit + Management Review** (Clause 9) are mandatory checkpoints, and **nonconformity + corrective action** (Clause 10) are mandatory improvement mechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-mile memory hook for the exam hall:** > *"**Policy → Review → SEU/EnPI/EnB → Objectives/Targets → Action Plans → Operate → Measure → Audit → Review → Improve.*** > *This is the **10-step arrow of ISO 50001:2018**. Trace it once on your answer sheet before writing the answer."*

topics.title: Implementation Steps and Requirements for EnMS in Food Processing Units
Monitoring, Measurement, and Verification Techniques for EnMS Effectiveness
Case Studies on EnMS Implementation and Certification in the Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Studies on EnMS Implementation and Certification in the Food Industry # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Energy Management System (EnMS)? An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a structured, documented, and continually improving management framework that allows an organization — specifically a food processing unit — to take a systematic approach to achieving continual improvement of **energy performance**, including **energy efficiency**, **energy use**, and **energy consumption**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** *An Energy Management System is a set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives, in accordance with the ISO 50001 international standard.* The reference international standard governing EnMS is **ISO 50001:2018** — *"Energy management systems — Requirements with guidance for use"*. It supersedes the earlier ISO 50001:2011. ## 1.2 What is EnMS Certification? **EnMS Certification** is the formal, third-party audit-based attestation by an accredited Certification Body (CB) that an organization's energy management system conforms to all the mandatory clauses (Clauses 4–10) of **ISO 50001:2018**. Successful certification results in the issuance of an **ISO 50001 certificate** valid for **3 years**, subject to annual surveillance audits. ## 1.3 The Need for EnMS in the Food Industry The food industry is one of the most **energy-intensive** manufacturing sectors globally, accounting for roughly **30% of global industrial energy consumption**. Key drivers for adopting EnMS in food processing include: - High thermal energy demand for cooking, baking, pasteurization, sterilization, drying, and evaporation. - High electrical energy demand for refrigeration, cold-chain logistics, motors, pumps, fans, compressed air, and lighting. - Escalating cost of fossil fuels and grid electricity. - Carbon-emission reduction commitments and **Net-Zero 2070** targets. - Customer and retailer (e.g., Walmart, Tesco, IKEA) **Scope-3 emission** reporting demands. - Compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act (EC Act)**, **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme**, and BEE Star Rating for industries. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Regimen for a Factory" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of an EnMS exactly like a *personal health regimen* for a factory. > > 1. **Doctor's check-up** = Initial **Energy Review (Clause 6.3)** to identify "energy ailments." > 2. **Prescribed diet & exercise plan** = **Energy Objectives, Targets, and Action Plans (Clause 6.2)**. > 3. **Daily routine / discipline** = **Operational Controls (Clause 8.1)** to follow the plan. > 4. **Weighing scale & blood report** = **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) and Monitoring (Clause 9.1)**. > 5. **Quarterly doctor visit** = **Internal Audit (Clause 9.2)** and **Management Review (Clause 9.3)**. > 6. **Re-assessment each year** = **Continual Improvement (Clause 10.3)**. > > Just as a person without a regimen can deteriorate, a factory without an EnMS will keep wasting energy and money. ## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Board Exam Favorites) | Term | Meaning (as per ISO 50001:2018) | | :--- | :--- | | **EnMS** | Energy Management System | | **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator — a quantitative measure of energy performance | | **EnB** | Energy Baseline — quantitative reference for energy performance | | **SEU** | Significant Energy Use — energy use accounting for substantial consumption / savings potential | | **Energy Review** | Determination of current energy performance, SEUs, and improvement opportunities | | **Energy Performance** | Measurable result(s) related to energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption | | **M&V** | Measurement and Verification — quantifying savings using IPMVP protocols | | **CxO Commitment** | Top-management commitment — Clause 5 leadership requirements | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 ISO 50001:2018 — Clause-Wise Architecture The ISO 50001:2018 standard is built on the **Annex SL High-Level Structure (HLS)** — a 10-clause universal framework shared by all modern ISO management system standards (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001). This makes integration with **Quality (QMS)** and **Environmental (EMS)** management systems seamless. | Clause | Title | Purpose in EnMS Context | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **1** | Scope | Defines the boundaries of the EnMS. | | **2** | Normative References | References ISO 50002 (audit), ISO 50003 (certification), ISO 50004–50015. | | **3** | Terms & Definitions | Defines EnMS, EnPI, EnB, SEU, etc. | | **4** | Context of the Organization | Internal / external issues, needs of interested parties, scope of EnMS. | | **5** | Leadership | Top management commitment, energy policy, roles & responsibilities. | | **6** | Planning | Energy review, EnPIs, EnB, objectives, action plans, **energy risk & opportunity assessment**. | | **7** | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. | | **8** | Operation | Operational planning & control, design, procurement, **SEU control**. | | **9** | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation, internal audit, management review. | | **10** | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, **continual improvement**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory clauses for certification: 4 to 10.** Clauses 1–3 are *informative*. A missing Clause 4–10 requirement = automatic **Major Nonconformity** and rejection of certification. ## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle in EnMS ISO 50001 follows the classic **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, perfectly aligning with NEP-2020 outcome-based learning: - **Plan (Clauses 4, 5, 6):** Establish context, leadership, energy policy, energy review, EnB/EnPI, objectives, and action plans. - **Do (Clauses 7, 8):** Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication; implement operational controls and SEU management. - **Check (Clause 9):** Monitor, measure, analyze EnPIs; conduct internal audits; hold management review. - **Act (Clause 10):** Address nonconformities, drive continual improvement. ## 2.3 EnMS Certification — The 3-Stage Audit Process Stage-1 Audit (Documentation Review): The Certification Body (CB) auditor reviews all **mandatory documented information** (energy policy, energy objectives, EnB, EnPI, energy review report, competence matrix, audit reports, management review minutes). Any gap = *finding* to be closed. Stage-2 Audit (On-site Certification Audit): On-site verification of implementation, operational control, SEU management, and competence. Auditor selects **SEUs** and traces them through the system. Stage-3: Annual Surveillance + Re-certification (every 3 years): Confirms continued conformance and continual improvement. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) EnMS questions, viva voce, and energy audit computations. | Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{prod}}$ | Total energy used per unit of production (e.g., kWh per tonne of milk processed). | $\dfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{tonne}}$ | | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | $EnPI = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{actual}}$ | Ratio of energy consumed to a production variable. | $\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{unit}}$ | | **Energy Savings (kWh/year)** | $ES = E_{baseline} - E_{reported}$ | Difference between baseline and reporting-period energy use. | kWh / year | | **Percentage Energy Savings** | $\%ES = \left( \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{reported}}{E_{baseline}} \right) \times 100$ | Normalized percentage reduction in energy. | \% | | **Energy Cost Savings (₹)** | $ECS = ES \times C_{unit}$ | Monetary value of energy saved. | ₹ / year | | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{I_{capex}}{ECS}$ | Time to recover the capital investment. | years | | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{ECS}{I_{capex}} \times 100$ | Annualized return on the energy-efficiency investment. | \% | | **Carbon Dioxide Equivalent Reduction** | $CO_{2e} = ES \times EF_{grid}$ | Where **EF\_grid = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh** (CEA 2023, India grid average). | tonnes CO₂e | | **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Drift** | $CUSUM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_{i} - \overline{E})$ | Detects long-term drift in EnPI. | kWh | | **Normalised Baseline Regression** | $E_{adj} = a \cdot P + b$ | Linear EnB model — energy adjusted for production level. | kWh | > **Engineering Constants (Indian context, memorize for KTU exams):** > - **Grid Emission Factor (India, CEA 2023):** **0.716 kg CO₂e / kWh** > - **NCV of furnace oil:** **10,200 kcal/kg** (= 42.47 MJ/kg) > - **NCV of natural gas:** **8,500 kcal/m³** (= 35.59 MJ/m³) > - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal** ## 2.5 Utility of EnMS in Food Industry Engineering 1. **Cost reduction** — typical 5–25% energy-cost reduction in 2 years. 2. **Regulatory compliance** — EC Act, PAT scheme, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) for packaging. 3. **Brand value** — ISO 50001 certification improves ESG ratings. 4. **Supply-chain access** — many MNCs (Nestlé, Unilever, PepsiCo) now mandate ISO 50001 from their suppliers. 5. **Carbon trading** — generates **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** under the PAT scheme. 6. **Integration** — easy to merge with **ISO 14001 (EMS)** and **ISO 9001 (QMS)** into an **Integrated Management System (IMS)**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — EnPI Calculation for a Dairy Plant (14-Mark Standard) > **Problem:** A dairy plant processing **50,000 tonnes/year of milk** consumed **18,000 MWh** of electricity and **3,000 tonnes of furnace oil** in the reporting year. In the baseline year, the same plant processed **45,000 tonnes/year of milk** and consumed **20,000 MWh** of electricity and **3,300 tonnes of furnace oil**. Calculate: > (a) Baseline SEC, (b) Reporting SEC, (c) Energy savings in MWh and percentage, (d) Annual cost saving (assume ₹8/kWh blended tariff and ₹65/kg furnace oil), (e) Tonnes of CO₂e avoided. ### Step 1 — Convert all energy to a common unit (MJ). $$E_{electricity,\;baseline} = 20{,}000 \; \text{MWh} \times 3.6 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kWh}} \times 1000 = 7.20 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{furnace\;oil,\;baseline} = 3{,}300 \; \text{tonnes} \times 1000 \; \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{tonne}} \times 42.47 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} = 1.4015 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;baseline} = 7.20 \times 10^{7} + 1.4015 \times 10^{8} = 2.1215 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ ### Step 2 — Baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). $$SEC_{baseline} = \dfrac{2.1215 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}}{45{,}000 \; \text{tonnes}} = 4{,}714.4 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne\;milk}}$$ ### Step 3 — Reporting year energy in MJ. $$E_{electricity,\;report} = 18{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 1000 = 6.48 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{furnace\;oil,\;report} = 3{,}000 \times 1000 \times 42.47 = 1.2741 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;report} = 6.48 \times 10^{7} + 1.2741 \times 10^{8} = 1.9221 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ ### Step 4 — Reporting SEC. $$SEC_{report} = \dfrac{1.9221 \times 10^{8}}{50{,}000} = 3{,}844.2 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne\;milk}}$$ ### Step 5 — Absolute energy savings. $$\Delta E = E_{total,\;baseline} - E_{total,\;report} = 2.1215 \times 10^{8} - 1.9221 \times 10^{8} = 1.994 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ Converting to MWh: $$\Delta E_{MWh} = \dfrac{1.994 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{3}} = 5{,}538.9 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 6 — Percentage energy savings. $$\%ES = \left( \dfrac{2.1215 \times 10^{8} - 1.9221 \times 10^{8}}{2.1215 \times 10^{8}} \right) \times 100 = 9.40 \;\%$$ ### Step 7 — Annual cost savings (electricity + furnace oil). $$ECS_{elec} = (20{,}000 - 18{,}000) \; \text{MWh} \times 1000 \times 8 = ₹1{,}60{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.60 \; \text{Crore}$$ $$ECS_{oil} = (3{,}300 - 3{,}000) \times 1000 \times 65 = ₹1{,}95{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.95 \; \text{Crore}$$ $$ECS_{total} = ₹1.60 + ₹1.95 = ₹3.55 \; \text{Crore / year}$$ ### Step 8 — CO₂ equivalent avoided (using Indian grid EF = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh). $$CO_{2e} = 5{,}538.9 \times 1000 \times 0.716 = 3{,}965{,}852 \; \text{kg} \approx 3{,}966 \; \text{tonnes \; CO}_{2}\text{e}$$ ### Step 9 — Tabulated result. | Metric | Baseline Year | Reporting Year | Savings | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Milk processed (tonnes) | 45,000 | 50,000 | — | | Total energy (MJ) | $2.1215 \times 10^{8}$ | $1.9221 \times 10^{8}$ | $1.994 \times 10^{7}$ MJ | | SEC (MJ/tonne) | 4,714.4 | 3,844.2 | 870.2 MJ/tonne | | Cost (₹ Crore) | 4.55 | 4.55 ≈ | ₹3.55 Crore | | CO₂e (tonnes) | 14,320 | 12,930 | **3,966 t CO₂e** | ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Linear Regression EnB for a Beverage Plant > **Problem:** A beverage plant has the following historical annual data. Develop the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** linear regression model $E = a \cdot P + b$ and predict the energy use at a future production level of **60,000 kL**. | Year | Production P (kL) | Energy E (MWh) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Y1 | 30,000 | 12,000 | | Y2 | 35,000 | 13,500 | | Y3 | 40,000 | 14,800 | | Y4 | 45,000 | 16,300 | | Y5 | 50,000 | 17,700 | ### Step 1 — Compute means. $$\bar{P} = \dfrac{30{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 50{,}000}{5} = 40{,}000 \; \text{kL}$$ $$\bar{E} = \dfrac{12{,}000 + 13{,}500 + 14{,}800 + 16{,}300 + 17{,}700}{5} = 14{,}860 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute slope **a**. $$a = \dfrac{\sum (P_i - \bar{P})(E_i - \bar{E})}{\sum (P_i - \bar{P})^2}$$ Compute term by term: $$(30{,}000 - 40{,}000)(12{,}000 - 14{,}860) = (-10{,}000)(-2{,}860) = 2.86 \times 10^{7}$$ $$(35{,}000 - 40{,}000)(13{,}500 - 14{,}860) = (-5{,}000)(-1{,}360) = 6.80 \times 10^{6}$$ $$(40{,}000 - 40{,}000)(14{,}800 - 14{,}860) = 0$$ $$(45{,}000 - 40{,}000)(16{,}300 - 14{,}860) = (5{,}000)(1{,}440) = 7.20 \times 10^{6}$$ $$(50{,}000 - 40{,}000)(17{,}700 - 14{,}860) = (10{,}000)(2{,}840) = 2.84 \times 10^{7}$$ $$\sum (P_i - \bar{P})(E_i - \bar{E}) = 2.86 \times 10^{7} + 6.80 \times 10^{6} + 0 + 7.20 \times 10^{6} + 2.84 \times 10^{7} = 7.30 \times 10^{7}$$ Sum of squared deviations: $$\sum (P_i - \bar{P})^2 = 10^{8} + 2.5 \times 10^{7} + 0 + 2.5 \times 10^{7} + 10^{8} = 2.5 \times 10^{8}$$ $$a = \dfrac{7.30 \times 10^{7}}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 0.292 \; \dfrac{\text{MWh}}{\text{kL}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute intercept **b**. $$b = \bar{E} - a \cdot \bar{P} = 14{,}860 - (0.292)(40{,}000) = 14{,}860 - 11{,}680 = 3{,}180 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 4 — Final EnB model. $$E_{adj} = 0.292 \cdot P + 3{,}180 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 5 — Predicted energy at P = 60,000 kL. $$E_{pred} = 0.292 \times 60{,}000 + 3{,}180 = 17{,}520 + 3{,}180 = 20{,}700 \; \text{MWh}$$ If the plant actually consumes only **18,000 MWh** at that production level, the normalized savings are: $$E_{saved} = 20{,}700 - 18{,}000 = 2{,}700 \; \text{MWh/year}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask *"Why use a regression-based EnB instead of a single-year baseline?"* — The single-year baseline fails when production varies; the regression EnB **normalises** energy to a common production level, giving a fair comparison. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ISO 50001:2018 EnMS — Full Implementation Flow (PDCA) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Step 1 Context Analysis
Clause 4 Identify Issues
Interested Parties Scope"] node2["Step 2 Leadership
Clause 5 Energy Policy
Top Management Commitment"] node3["Step 3 Energy Review
Clause 6.3 SEU EnB EnPI"] node4["Step 4 Objectives and
Action Plans Clause 6.2"] node5["Step 5 Support Resources
Competence Awareness
Communication Clause 7"] node6["Step 6 Operational Control
SEU Management
Design Procurement Clause 8"] node7["Step 7 Monitoring and
Measurement EnPI
Internal Audit MR Clause 9"] node8["Step 8 Continual
Improvement NC CAPA
Clause 10"] node9["Mature EnMS
ISO 50001 Certified
Energy Performance Up"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node8 -.Feedback.-> node1 node7 -.Feedback.-> node3 ``` ## 4.2 Three-Stage Certification Audit Process ```mermaid flowchart LR stageA["Stage 1 Documentation
Review by CB Auditor
Mandatory Clauses 4 to 10"] stageB["Stage 2 On Site
Certification Audit
SEU Trace Operational"] stageC["Stage 3 Annual
Surveillance Plus
3 Year Recertification"] cert["ISO 50001 Certificate
Issued Valid 3 Years"] stageA -->|Any Major NC| ncFix1["Close NC Within 90 Days"] ncFix1 --> stageA stageA -->|Compliant| stageB stageB -->|Any Major NC| ncFix2["Close NC Within 90 Days"] ncFix2 --> stageB stageB -->|Compliant| cert cert --> stageC stageC -->|Any Major NC| ncFix3["Close NC Within 30 Days"] ncFix3 --> stageC stageC -.Recert Every 3 Yr.-> stageA ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an EnMS in a Food Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD top["Top Management
Energy Policy and Leadership"] enr["Energy Manager
ISO 50001 Champion"] team["Cross Functional Energy Team
Production Maintenance Utilities
QA Purchase Finance"] seumodule["SEU Registry
Boilers Compressors
Refrigeration Motors HVAC"] meter["Smart Metering and
SCADA Energy Data
Acquisition System"] enpi["EnPI Dashboard
KPI Monitoring
CUSUM Drift Detection"] audit["Internal Audit and
Management Review
PDCA Check Phase"] ims["Integrated Management
System ISO 9001 14001
45001 Interface"] top --> enr enr --> team team --> seumodule seumodule --> meter meter --> enpi enpi --> audit audit --> ims ims -.Continual Improvement.-> top ``` ## 4.4 Typical EnMS Project Timeline (12–18 Months) ```mermaid gantt title EnMS Implementation Timeline Food Industry dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD axisFormat %b section Plan Gap Analysis and Context :a1, 2024-01-01, 45d Energy Policy and Leadership :a2, after a1, 30d Energy Review and SEU Mapping :a3, after a2, 60d EnB and EnPI Definition :a4, after a3, 30d section Do Objectives Targets and Action Plans :b1, after a4, 45d Competence Training and Awareness :b2, after b1, 60d Operational Controls and SOPs :b3, after b2, 60d section Check Monitoring and Measurement :c1, after b3, 60d Internal Audit :c2, after c1, 30d Management Review :c3, after c2, 15d section Act Stage 1 Documentation Audit :d1, after c3, 15d Stage 2 On Site Certification Audit :d2, after d1, 30d Certification Issued :milestone, after d2, 0d ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define Energy Management System (EnMS) and state any four mandatory documented information required by ISO 50001:2018. **Model Answer:** An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements used to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives, as defined by **ISO 50001:2018**. Four mandatory documented information requirements: 1. **Scope of the EnMS** (Clause 4.3) — boundaries and applicability. 2. **Energy Policy** (Clause 5.2) — commitment to continual improvement. 3. **Energy Review Report** (Clause 6.3) — SEUs, EnB, EnPI determination. 4. **Energy Objectives and Action Plans** (Clause 6.2) — measurable targets with responsibilities, resources, and timelines. *(Other acceptable: roles/responsibilities, criteria for evaluation of energy performance, internal audit programme, results of management review.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Energy Baseline (EnB) and Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI). Why is a regression-based EnB preferred over a single-year baseline? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Energy Baseline (EnB) | Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Quantitative reference that serves as a comparison point for energy performance. | Quantitative measure of energy performance as defined by the organization. | | Purpose | Defines *what "normal" was* before improvement actions. | Tracks *current performance* against the baseline. | | Form | A line, equation, or fixed number. | A ratio (e.g., MJ/tonne, kWh/kl). | | Update | Reviewed and updated when SEUs, processes, or EnPI change. | Monitored continuously. | **Why regression EnB is preferred:** A single-year baseline is **sensitive to production volume**; if production falls, energy falls too, falsely showing "savings." A regression-based EnB mathematically **normalises** energy to a chosen production variable, isolating *true* efficiency improvements from volume effects. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle as applied to ISO 50001:2018 EnMS. Map each PDCA stage to the relevant ISO 50001 clauses. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A bakery producing **20,000 tonnes/year of bread** consumed **6,500 MWh** of electricity and **500 tonnes of LPG** in the baseline year. After implementing EnMS-driven measures (VFD on motors, heat recovery from oven exhaust, LED lighting), the same plant produced **22,000 tonnes/year** and consumed **5,800 MWh** of electricity and **420 tonnes of LPG**. Calculate the **(i) baseline and reporting SEC in MJ/tonne, (ii) energy savings in MWh, (iii) percentage savings, (iv) annual cost saving (₹9/kWh, ₹95/kg LPG)**, and **(v) tonnes of CO₂e avoided (grid EF = 0.716 kg/kWh; NCV of LPG = 47 MJ/kg)**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — PDCA Mapping | PDCA Stage | ISO 50001:2018 Clauses | Activities | | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **Plan** | 4, 5, 6 | Context analysis, leadership, energy policy, energy review (SEU, EnB, EnPI), objectives, action plans, risk & opportunity assessment. | | **Do** | 7, 8 | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information; operational controls, design, procurement, SEU management. | | **Check** | 9 | Monitoring & measurement of EnPIs, evaluation of compliance, internal audit, management review. | | **Act** | 10 | Nonconformity and corrective action, continual improvement of energy performance. | The PDCA cycle ensures **continual improvement** and prepares the plant for **ISO 50001 certification**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Step 1 — Convert baseline energy to MJ.** $$E_{elec,\;bl} = 6{,}500 \times 1000 \times 3.6 = 2.34 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{LPG,\;bl} = 500 \times 1000 \times 47 = 2.35 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;bl} = 2.34 \times 10^{7} + 2.35 \times 10^{7} = 4.69 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 2 — Baseline SEC.** $$SEC_{bl} = \dfrac{4.69 \times 10^{7}}{20{,}000} = 2{,}345 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne}}$$ **Step 3 — Reporting energy in MJ.** $$E_{elec,\;rep} = 5{,}800 \times 1000 \times 3.6 = 2.088 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{LPG,\;rep} = 420 \times 1000 \times 47 = 1.974 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;rep} = 2.088 \times 10^{7} + 1.974 \times 10^{7} = 4.062 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 4 — Reporting SEC.** $$SEC_{rep} = \dfrac{4.062 \times 10^{7}}{22{,}000} = 1{,}846.4 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne}}$$ **Step 5 — Energy savings in MJ and MWh.** $$\Delta E = 4.69 \times 10^{7} - 4.062 \times 10^{7} = 6.28 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$\Delta E_{MWh} = \dfrac{6.28 \times 10^{6}}{3.6 \times 10^{3}} = 1{,}744.4 \; \text{MWh}$$ **Step 6 — Percentage energy savings.** $$\%ES = \dfrac{6.28 \times 10^{6}}{4.69 \times 10^{7}} \times 100 = 13.39 \;\%$$ **Step 7 — Cost savings.** $$ECS_{elec} = (6{,}500 - 5{,}800) \times 1000 \times 9 = ₹63{,}00{,}000 = ₹63 \; \text{Lakh}$$ $$ECS_{LPG} = (500 - 420) \times 1000 \times 95 = ₹76{,}00{,}000 = ₹76 \; \text{Lakh}$$ $$ECS_{total} = ₹63 + ₹76 = ₹1.39 \; \text{Crore / year}$$ **Step 8 — CO₂e avoided (electricity only — electricity is grid-connected).** $$CO_{2e} = (6{,}500 - 5{,}800) \times 1000 \times 0.716 = 5{,}012 \; \text{tonnes CO}_{2}\text{e / year}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Do *not* convert CO₂e for LPG/LNG/natural gas using the grid emission factor of 0.716. LPG is a *fuel*, and its CO₂e must be calculated from its **NCV (47 MJ/kg) × emission factor (~63 kg CO₂/GJ of fuel)**. Using the grid EF for fuels is a common mistake costing 1–2 marks. --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Q-A* **(a)** Discuss **five case studies** of EnMS implementation in different food industry sub-sectors (dairy, bakery, brewery, edible oil, sugar).
