Highlight the SEUs identified, EnPIs used, and the percentage energy saved in each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **three-stage certification audit process** of ISO 50001. List any **eight mandatory documented information** that a food company must maintain to be eligible for certification. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — Five Food Industry Case Studies | Sub-sector | Plant (illustrative) | SEU Identified | EnPI Used | Energy Saved | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | **Dairy (Amul, Banas, Mother Dairy)** | 10 Lakh LPD dairy | Pasteurizer, chilling, boiler, CIP | kWh / tonne milk | **18 – 22 %** | | **Bakery (Britannia, ITC, Parle)** | Bread & biscuit plant | Tunnel oven, dough mixer, refrigeration | MJ / tonne product | **12 – 15 %** | | **Brewery (Kingfisher, United Breweries)** | 1 lakh KL/yr plant | Boil kettle, fermenter, glycol chiller, CO₂ compressor | MJ / kL beer | **10 – 14 %** | | **Edible Oil (Adani Wilmar, Cargill)** | Solvent extraction | De-solventizer, steam economy, dryer | MJ / tonne oil | **14 – 18 %** | | **Sugar (EID Parry, Balrampur Chini)** | 5,000 TCD plant | Cane preparation, evaporators, centrifuges, boilers | MJ / tonne sugar | **8 – 12 %** | **Key SEU common across food plants:** 1. **Steam boiler + turbine cogeneration** (largest fuel consumer). 2. **Ammonia / Freon refrigeration plant** (largest electricity consumer). 3. **Compressed air system** (often 30% leaky — huge saving potential). 4. **Motors + pumps + fans** (Variable Frequency Drives / VFDs). 5. **Heat recovery** from exhaust flue gases, hot condensate, and oven exhaust. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Certification Audit & Documented Information **Three-Stage Certification Audit (Clause references):** 1. **Stage 1 — Documentation Review (Readiness Review):** Auditor verifies all Clauses 4–10 documented information is in place; checks energy review, EnB, EnPI, and that internal audit and management review have been completed at least once. Result: *ready / not ready for Stage 2*. 2. **Stage 2 — On-site Certification Audit:** Auditor walks the plant, interviews staff, traces SEUs through the system, verifies operational control, competence, monitoring, and CAPA effectiveness. Result: *recommend / not recommend certification*. 3. **Stage 3 — Surveillance (annually) and Re-certification (every 3 years):** Confirms continual improvement, addresses minor NCs, and re-validates the EnB. **Eight Mandatory Documented Information Items:** 1. Scope of the EnMS (Clause 4.3). 2. Energy Policy (Clause 5.2). 3. Roles, responsibilities, and authorities (Clause 5.3). 4. Results of the **Energy Review** (Clause 6.3) — SEUs, EnB, EnPI. 5. Energy Objectives and Action Plans (Clause 6.2). 6. Criteria and methodology for **EnPI evaluation** (Clause 9.1.1). 7. Internal Audit Programme and Results (Clause 9.2). 8. Management Review Results (Clause 9.3). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write *only 4–5 items* in this question. A 14-mark question expects **8 items with proper clause numbers**. Writing numbers like "Clause 6.2" gets you full credit. Also note that, unlike ISO 14001, ISO 50001 has **no requirement for documented procedures** — only *documented information* (i.e., any format: paper, digital, video). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 30-second revision bullets before the exam:** - **EnMS** is governed by **ISO 50001:2018**, with 10 clauses (Clauses 4–10 are mandatory for certification). - **PDCA cycle** maps perfectly: Plan (4-6), Do (7-8), Check (9), Act (10). - **EnB (Energy Baseline)** = reference; **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** = metric. Both are *mandatory*. - **SEU (Significant Energy Use)** = the energy uses that account for the majority of consumption and offer the greatest savings. - **3-Stage Certification** = Stage 1 docs review → Stage 2 on-site audit → Stage 3 annual surveillance + 3-year re-cert. - **Linear regression EnB** $E = aP + b$ is the most common normalisation model in food plants. - **India grid emission factor = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh (CEA 2023)** — use this in numerical questions. - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal** — keep conversions handy. - **Typical food industry EnMS savings = 8–25%** over 2–3 years, with payback of 1–3 years. - **Energy Review (Clause 6.3)** must produce: energy consumption profile, SEUs, EnB, EnPI, and an Energy Action Plan. - **Common SEUs in food plants:** boilers, refrigeration, compressed air, motors/pumps, HVAC, ovens, driers, CIP systems. - **Continual improvement (Clause 10.3)** is the *heart* of ISO 50001 — every year the EnPI must show measurable improvement. - **Internal Audit (Clause 9.2)** + **Management Review (Clause 9.3)** must be conducted at least once before certification. - **Integrate** ISO 50001 with ISO 9001 (QMS) and ISO 14001 (EMS) using the common Annex SL structure — saves documentation effort. - **Indian regulatory drivers:** EC Act 2001, **PAT Scheme** (BEE), ESG reporting (SEBI BRSR), Net-Zero 2070. - **Certification cycle:** 3 years; surveillance audits every 12 months. - **Major NC** in Stage 2 = certificate *not issued*; **Minor NC** = closed within 90 days. > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Document it. Measure it. Improve it. Repeat it."* — This is the ISO 50001 way of life in every certified food plant.

topics.title: Assessment of Carbon Footprint in Food Processing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment of Carbon Footprint in Food Processing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Footprint (CF) in Food Processing** is formally defined as the **total mass of Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** expressed in **Carbon Dioxide equivalent (CO₂e)** units, emitted directly or indirectly throughout the **life cycle of a food product** — from agricultural raw material production, through processing, packaging, distribution, consumption, and end-of-life disposal.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
CF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)
$$

Where:
- $AD_i$ = Activity Data (e.g., kWh of electricity, litres of diesel, kg of waste)
- $EF_i$ = Emission Factor (kg CO₂e per unit activity)
- $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of the $i^{th}$ GHG (dimensionless multiplier)

The **standard reference unit** is **kg CO₂e / tonne of finished product** or **kg CO₂e / kg of product**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weight of Your Breath"

Imagine every food product you manufacture carries an invisible "**weight tag**" — not in kilograms of food, but in kilograms of CO₂ released to produce it.

- A **1 kg packet of biscuits** carries roughly **1.5–2.5 kg CO₂e** of hidden climate weight.
- A **1 litre of packaged milk** carries roughly **0.9–3.2 kg CO₂e**.

Just like a person on a weighing scale, the planet is "weighed down" by these accumulated emissions. **Carbon footprint assessment is therefore the act of putting the food industry on a planetary weighing scale.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Major GHGs covered under UNFCCC/Kyoto Protocol:**
> Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Methane (CH₄), Nitrous Oxide (N₂O), Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), Perfluorocarbons (PFCs), and Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆). For food processing, **CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O are most relevant**.

### 1.3 The Three Scopes of Carbon Footprint (GHG Protocol)

The **Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard** classifies emissions in food processing into:

| Scope | Description | Examples in Food Industry |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Scope 1 — Direct Emissions** | Owned/controlled sources | Boilers (natural gas), diesel generators, refrigerant leaks, on-site wastewater treatment |
| **Scope 2 — Indirect (Energy)** | Purchased electricity/steam/heat | Grid electricity for motors, lighting, refrigeration, compressed air |
| **Scope 3 — Value Chain Emissions** | Upstream + downstream indirect | Agricultural inputs (fertilizer, livestock), packaging materials, transport, retail refrigeration, consumer use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical food processing plant, **Scope 3 contributes 60–80%** of total carbon footprint, **Scope 2 contributes 15–30%**, and **Scope 1 contributes 5–15%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standards & Frameworks for CF Assessment

The following international standards govern food carbon footprint assessment:

| Standard | Scope | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **ISO 14064-1** | Organizational level | Quantify and report GHG inventories |
| **ISO 14064-2** | Project level | Quantify GHG reductions from specific projects |
| **ISO 14067** | Product level | Carbon footprint of products (CFP) |
| **PAS 2050** | Product level | Spec for assessment of goods/services life-cycle GHG |
| **GHG Protocol** | Corporate | Three-scope methodology |
| **Product Category Rules (PCR)** | Specific products | Defines rules for product-level CF |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology (Cradle-to-Gate Approach)

A typical **Carbon Footprint Assessment (CFA)** in a food processing plant follows:

1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — Define product system, functional unit, reference flow.
2. **System Boundary Setting** — Cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, or gate-to-gate.
3. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Collect all input/output data (energy, materials, waste).
4. **Emission Factor Selection** — Use national databases (e.g., DEFRA, IPCC, CEA).
5. **Impact Assessment** — Convert GHG to CO₂e via GWP.
6. **Interpretation** — Identify hotspots, propose reduction strategies.

### 2.3 Sources of Carbon Emissions in Food Processing

#### A. Direct (Scope 1) Sources:
- **Steam boilers** burning coal, furnace oil, natural gas, biomass.
- **Diesel forklifts** and **DG sets** for backup power.
- **Refrigerant leaks** from cold storage (HFC-R134a, R404a are very high GWP).
- **On-site effluent treatment** (CH₄ from anaerobic digesters).
- **Biomass combustion** in driers (CO₂ considered neutral if renewable).

#### B. Indirect (Scope 2) Sources:
- Purchased **grid electricity** — major contributor in bakery, dairy, beverage plants.
- Purchased **steam** from external cogeneration plants.

#### C. Indirect (Scope 3) Sources:
- **Agriculture**: fertilizer production (N₂O), enteric fermentation (CH₄), rice cultivation.
- **Packaging**: PET, aluminium, glass, paperboard production.
- **Transport**: inbound raw materials + outbound finished goods.
- **End-of-life**: landfill CH₄, packaging recycling emissions.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are critical for KTU ESE numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $CO_2e = \sum (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$ | Universal emission calculation |
| 2 | $E_{CO_2} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{CO_2} \times 10^{-3}$ | CO₂ from fuel combustion (tonnes) |
| 3 | $E_{CH_4} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{CH_4} \times 10^{-3}$ | CH₄ from combustion |
| 4 | $E_{N_2O} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{N_2O} \times 10^{-3}$ | N₂O from combustion |
| 5 | $E_{elec} = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid}$ | Scope 2 emissions |
| 6 | $CF_{per\_unit} = \dfrac{CF_{total}}{M_{finished\_product}}$ | Per-kg or per-litre CF |
| 7 | $CF_{Scope\_1+2+3} = CF_1 + CF_2 + CF_3$ | Total corporate CF |
| 8 | $\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{CF_{base} - CF_{new}}{CF_{base}} \times 100$ | % reduction in CF |
| 9 | $E_{refrigerant} = m_{leak} \times GWP_{ref}$ | Fugitive emissions from cooling |
| 10 | $E_{transit} = D \times M \times EF_{truck}$ | Transport emissions (kg CO₂e) |

### 2.5 Global Warming Potential (GWP-100) Reference Values

| Gas | Chemical Formula | GWP (100-year, AR5) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **1** |
| Methane | CH₄ | **28** |
| Nitrous Oxide | N₂O | **265** |
| HFC-134a | CH₂FCF₃ | **1,300** |
| HFC-404a | Blend | **3,922** |
| Sulphur Hexafluoride | SF₆ | **23,500** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The values above use the **IPCC Fifth Assessment Report (AR5)** with **GWP-100 horizon**. Always check whether the question specifies **AR4 or AR5** — values differ significantly (e.g., CH₄ GWP = 25 in AR4 vs. 28 in AR5).

### 2.6 Standard Emission Factors (Indicative)

| Activity | Emission Factor (kg CO₂e per unit) |
|:---|:---:|
| Grid electricity (India, CEA 2022) | **0.716** kg CO₂e / kWh |
| Natural gas combustion | **2.02** kg CO₂e / m³ |
| Furnace oil | **3.20** kg CO₂e / litre |
| Diesel combustion | **2.68** kg CO₂e / litre |
| PNG combustion | **2.05** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Coal (Indian grade) | **2.42** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Truck transport (diesel) | **0.1067** kg CO₂e / tonne-km |
| Refrigerant R-134a leak | **1,300** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Water supply | **0.344** kg CO₂e / m³ |

> [!TIP]
> Always refer to the **latest Central Electricity Authority (CEA) baseline database** or **DEFRA conversion factors** for KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Carbon footprint assessment in food processing enables:
- **Regulatory compliance** with EU CBAM, India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS).
- **Eco-labelling** and **green marketing** for products.
- **Supply chain optimization** to reduce Scope 3 emissions.
- **Carbon-neutral certification** for premium products.
- **Identification of energy efficiency hotspots** — directly linking to energy management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dairy Processing Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Numerical Pattern):**
> A dairy plant processes **50,000 litres of milk per day** into pasteurized packaged milk. Annual data:
>
> - Furnace oil consumption: **180,000 litres/year**
> - Grid electricity: **1.2 million kWh/year**
> - Diesel (forklift + DG): **12,000 litres/year**
> - Refrigerant R-404a leakage: **18 kg/year**
> - Inbound raw milk transport: **150,000 tonne-km/year (diesel truck)**
>
> Compute the **total annual carbon footprint** and **per-litre CF**.

#### Step 1 — Identify Scope-Wise Emissions

- Furnace oil (boiler) → **Scope 1** (direct combustion)
- Diesel (forklift/DG) → **Scope 1**
- Refrigerant leak → **Scope 1** (fugitive)
- Grid electricity → **Scope 2**
- Inbound transport → **Scope 3**

#### Step 2 — Scope 1 Calculations

**A. Furnace Oil Combustion CO₂:**

$$
E_{CO_2}^{FO} = V_{FO} \times EF_{FO} = 180{,}000 \times 3.20 = 576{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**B. Diesel Combustion (Combustion only — CH₄ and N₂O negligible for liquid fuels in CO₂ terms):**

$$
E_{CO_2}^{diesel} = V_{diesel} \times EF_{diesel} = 12{,}000 \times 2.68 = 32{,}160 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**C. Refrigerant R-404a Leakage:**

$$
E_{ref} = m_{leak} \times GWP_{R404a} = 18 \times 3{,}922 = 70{,}596 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Total Scope 1:**

$$
CF_1 = 576{,}000 + 32{,}160 + 70{,}596 = 678{,}756 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 3 — Scope 2 Calculations

**Grid Electricity Emissions:**

$$
E_{grid} = E_{kWh} \times EF_{grid} = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 0.716 = 859{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
CF_2 = 859{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 4 — Scope 3 Calculations

**Inbound Transport (Diesel Truck):**

$$
E_{transit} = D \times M \times EF_{truck} = 150{,}000 \times 0.1067 = 16{,}005 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
CF_3 = 16{,}005 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 5 — Total Annual Carbon Footprint

$$
CF_{total} = CF_1 + CF_2 + CF_3 = 678{,}756 + 859{,}200 + 16{,}005
$$

$$
\boxed{CF_{total} = 1{,}553{,}961 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year} \approx 1{,}554 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e / year}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Per-Unit (Per Litre) CF

Annual production:

$$
M_{annual} = 50{,}000 \times 365 = 18{,}250{,}000 \text{ litres / year}
$$

$$
CF_{per\_litre} = \dfrac{CF_{total}}{M_{annual}} = \dfrac{1{,}553{,}961}{18{,}250{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{CF_{per\_litre} = 0.0852 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Benchmarking Insight:** Global average for pasteurized packaged milk is **0.9–3.2 kg CO₂e/litre** (cradle-to-grave including farm). Our plant value of **0.085 kg CO₂e/litre** is **processing-only** (gate-to-gate), which is consistent with literature.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bakery Plant Reduction Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A bakery plant has baseline $CF_{base} = 4{,}200$ tonnes CO₂e/year. After installing a biomass boiler (replacing 60% of furnace oil) and a 100 kWp solar PV (offsetting 12% grid power), calculate the **% reduction in carbon footprint** and **new CF**.

**Step 1 — Reduction from biomass boiler (replaces 60% FO):**

$$
\Delta CF_{biomass} = 0.60 \times 4{,}200 \times \alpha
$$

Assuming biomass displaces only the Scope-1 furnace-oil portion:

$$
\Delta CF_{biomass} = 0.60 \times 1{,}008 = 604.8 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e (avoided)}
$$

(Where 1,008 = Scope 1 share assumed = 24% of total.)

**Step 2 — Reduction from solar PV (12% of grid electricity):**

$$
\Delta CF_{solar} = 0.12 \times 0.30 \times 4{,}200 = 151.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e (avoided)}
$$

(Assuming Scope 2 = 30% of total.)

**Step 3 — Total Avoided Emissions:**

$$
\Delta CF_{total} = 604.8 + 151.2 = 756 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — New Carbon Footprint:**

$$
CF_{new} = 4{,}200 - 756 = 3{,}444 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{4{,}200 - 3{,}444}{4{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{reduction} = 18.0\%}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reduction Strategies

| Strategy | Scope Affected | Typical Reduction | Capex (Indicative) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Biomass boiler (replace FO) | 1 | 50–70% of boiler emissions | High |
| Solar PV rooftop | 2 | 10–25% of grid | Medium |
| Heat recovery from exhaust | 1, 2 | 5–15% | Medium |
| Refrigerant retrofit (NH₃/CO₂) | 1 | 80–90% of refrigerant CF | High |
| LED + VFD retrofit | 2 | 8–15% | Low |
| Packaging lightweighting | 3 | 5–12% | Low |
| Local sourcing of raw materials | 3 | 10–20% transport CF | Nil |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carbon Footprint Assessment Workflow (Cradle-to-Gate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Goal and Scope Definition] --> B[Step2: System Boundary Setting]
    B --> C[Step3: Functional Unit Selection]
    C --> D[Step4: Life Cycle Inventory Data Collection]
    D --> E[Step5: Emission Factor Mapping]
    E --> F[Step6: GHG Calculation CO2e]
    F --> G[Step7: Hotspot Identification]
    G --> H[Step8: Interpretation and Reporting]
    H --> I[Step9: Reduction Strategy]
    I --> J[Step10: Verification by Third Party]
    J --> K[Certified CF Label and Disclosure]
```

### 4.2 Scope-Wise Emission Sources in Food Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Scope1 Direct]
        S1A[Boiler Fuel Combustion]
        S1B[Diesel Forklift and DG]
        S1C[Refrigerant Leakage]
        S1D[Onsite Wastewater CH4]
    end
    subgraph S2[Scope2 Indirect Energy]
        S2A[Purchased Grid Electricity]
        S2B[Purchased Steam]
    end
    subgraph S3[Scope3 Value Chain]
        S3A[Agricultural Production]
        S3B[Packaging Manufacturing]
        S3C[Inbound and Outbound Transport]
        S3D[Retail Cold Chain]
        S3E[End of Life Disposal]
    end
    S1 --> SUM[Total Carbon Footprint CO2e]
    S2 --> SUM
    S3 --> SUM
```

### 4.3 Carbon Footprint Reduction Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Food Plant Carbon Footprint] --> L1A[Energy Efficiency]
    ROOT --> L1B[Fuel Switching]
    ROOT --> L1C[Renewable Energy]
    ROOT --> L1D[Process Optimization]
    ROOT --> L1E[Supply Chain]
    L1A --> L2A1[VFD on Motors]
    L1A --> L2A2[Heat Recovery]
    L1A --> L2A3[LED Lighting]
    L1B --> L2B1[FO to PNG]
    L1B --> L2B2[Coal to Biomass]
    L1C --> L2C1[Solar PV]
    L1C --> L2C2[Wind Power]
    L1D --> L2D1[Heat Integration]
    L1D --> L2D2[Refrigerant Retrofit]
    L1E --> L2E1[Local Sourcing]
    L1E --> L2E2[Lightweight Packaging]
    L1E --> L2E3[Modal Shift Rail]
```

### 4.4 Hotspot Identification Matrix (Pareto Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Raw Milk Production: 55 percent] --> TOTAL
    H2[Plant Electricity: 18 percent] --> TOTAL
    H3[Packaging Materials: 12 percent] --> TOTAL
    H4[Boiler Fuel: 8 percent] --> TOTAL
    H5[Transport: 4 percent] --> TOTAL
    H6[Refrigerants: 2 percent] --> TOTAL
    H7[Wastewater: 1 percent] --> TOTAL
    TOTAL[Total Plant CF: 100 percent CO2e]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Principle in CF:** Typically, **3–4 emission sources contribute >80%** of a food plant's total carbon footprint. Identifying these hotspots is the **first deliverable** of any CF assessment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
**Define Carbon Footprint. List any four greenhouse gases with their GWP values.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Carbon Footprint is the total greenhouse gas emissions caused directly and indirectly by a food product, expressed in **kg CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)** per functional unit (KTU: 1 Mark).

Four GHGs with GWP (100-year, IPCC AR5) (KTU: 2 Marks for any four):

| GHG | GWP-100 |
|:---:|:---:|
| CO₂ | 1 |
| CH₄ | 28 |
| N₂O | 265 |
| HFC-134a | 1,300 |
| SF₆ | 23,500 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Explain the three scopes of GHG emissions with two examples each from a food processing plant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Greenhouse Gas Protocol** classifies emissions into three scopes (KTU: 1 Mark definition):

- **Scope 1 (Direct):** Owned sources. Examples — natural gas in boilers, refrigerant leaks from cold storage (KTU: 0.67 Mark).
- **Scope 2 (Indirect Energy):** Purchased electricity/steam. Examples — grid electricity for motors, purchased steam from cogeneration plant (KTU: 0.67 Mark).
- **Scope 3 (Value Chain):** Upstream + downstream indirect. Examples — agricultural fertilizer use, outbound truck transport of finished goods (KTU: 0.66 Mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step methodology** for assessing the carbon footprint of a packaged fruit juice using **ISO 14067 framework**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A beverage plant consumes **150,000 m³ of natural gas/year** and **2.4 million kWh/year** of grid electricity. Compute the **annual Scope 1 + Scope 2 carbon footprint**. Also find the **per-litre CF** if annual production is **20 million litres**. Use EF: PNG = 2.05 kg CO₂e/m³; Grid = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

ISO 14067 methodology for packaged fruit juice (Cradle-to-Gate):

1. **Goal Definition** (KTU: 1 Mark): Quantify CFP per 1 litre of packaged juice.

2. **Scope Definition** (KTU: 1 Mark): Cradle-to-gate boundary — fruit cultivation → juice processing → packaging → factory gate.

3. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** (KTU: 2 Marks): Data on:
   - Agricultural inputs (fertilizers, pesticides, water for orchards).
   - Energy in plant (steam, electricity, refrigeration).
   - Packaging materials (PET bottles, caps, labels, shrink-wrap).
   - Wastewater & solid waste generated.

4. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** (KTU: 1 Mark): Convert each GHG to CO₂e using emission factors and GWP.

5. **Interpretation** (KTU: 1 Mark): Identify hotspots (e.g., orange cultivation = 40%, PET packaging = 25%, processing electricity = 20%, transport = 15%).

6. **Reporting & Critical Review** (KTU: 1 Mark): Document per ISO 14067 reporting format; undergo third-party verification for credibility.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Scope 1 (Natural Gas Combustion):**

$$
CF_1 = V_{NG} \times EF_{NG} = 150{,}000 \times 2.05 = 307{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks)

**Step 2 — Scope 2 (Grid Electricity):**

$$
CF_2 = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid} = 2{,}400{,}000 \times 0.716 = 1{,}718{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks)

**Step 3 — Total CF (Scope 1 + 2):**

$$
CF_{total} = 307{,}500 + 1{,}718{,}400 = 2{,}025{,}900 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Per-Litre CF:**

$$
CF_{per\_litre} = \dfrac{2{,}025{,}900}{20{,}000{,}000} = 0.1013 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks for final answer with units)

$$
\boxed{CF_{per\_litre} \approx 0.101 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **major sources of carbon emissions** in a typical food processing plant. Categorize them under Scope 1, 2, and 3 with specific examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A frozen seafood plant has the following annual data: Refrigerant R-404a leak = **25 kg/year**; Diesel = **20,000 L/year** (forklifts); Furnace oil = **100,000 L/year**; Grid electricity = **800,000 kWh/year**. Calculate the **total CF** and the **percentage contribution of refrigerants** to Scope 1 emissions. Use: GWP(R-404a) = 3,922; EF(diesel) = 2.68 kg CO₂e/L; EF(FO) = 3.20 kg CO₂e/L; EF(grid) = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Major Carbon Emission Sources in Food Processing:**

**1. Scope 1 — Direct Emissions (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Steam boilers**: combustion of natural gas, furnace oil, coal, biomass.
- **Diesel generators and forklifts**: backup power, material handling.
- **Refrigerant leakage** from cold storage — high GWP gases (HFC-134a, R-404a) have very high CF impact.
- **On-site wastewater treatment**: methane from anaerobic digestion.
- **Process emissions**: e.g., CO₂ from fermentation in breweries.

**2. Scope 2 — Indirect from Purchased Energy (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Grid electricity** for motors, pumps, compressors, lighting, refrigeration.
- **Purchased steam** from cogeneration/CHP plants.

**3. Scope 3 — Value Chain Emissions (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Agriculture**: fertilizer manufacture (N₂O), livestock enteric CH₄, paddy CH₄.
- **Packaging**: PET, aluminium, paperboard — virgin material production.
- **Transport**: inbound raw materials + outbound finished goods (truck, rail, ship).
- **Retail cold chain**: refrigerated display at retail outlets.
- **End-of-life**: landfill CH₄, recycling energy, incineration CO₂.

**Conclusion (KTU: 1 Mark):** Scope 3 typically dominates (60–80%) but is hardest to control; Scope 2 is the most actionable via energy efficiency and renewable energy procurement.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Refrigerant R-404a Emissions:**

$$
CF_{ref} = 25 \times 3{,}922 = 98{,}050 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 2 — Diesel Emissions:**

$$
CF_{diesel} = 20{,}000 \times 2.68 = 53{,}600 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 3 — Furnace Oil Emissions:**

$$
CF_{FO} = 100{,}000 \times 3.20 = 320{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Total Scope 1:**

$$
CF_1 = 98{,}050 + 53{,}600 + 320{,}000 = 471{,}650 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 5 — Scope 2 (Grid Electricity):**

$$
CF_2 = 800{,}000 \times 0.716 = 572{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 6 — Total CF:**

$$
CF_{total} = 471{,}650 + 572{,}800 = 1{,}044{,}450 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 7 — Refrigerant % of Scope 1:**

$$
\%_{ref} = \dfrac{98{,}050}{471{,}650} \times 100 = 20.79\%
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

$$
\boxed{CF_{total} \approx 1{,}044.45 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e / year}; \text{ Refrigerants} = 20.79\% \text{ of Scope 1}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by GWP for refrigerants and CH₄/N₂O** — losing 2–3 marks. Always convert to CO₂e.
> 2. **Mixing Scope 1 and Scope 2** — these MUST be reported separately per GHG Protocol. Combining them = –2 marks.
> 3. **Using wrong emission factor units** — e.g., using kg CO₂e/kWh instead of kg CO₂e/MWh, or vice versa. Always check.
> 4. **Not stating the standard (AR4 vs. AR5)** when quoting GWP values. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Final answer without units** (e.g., writing "0.085" instead of "0.085 kg CO₂e/litre") = –1 mark guaranteed.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the system boundary** (cradle-to-gate vs. gate-to-gate). Always state it in methodology.
> 7. **Not showing the formula before substituting values** — KTU wants the formula explicitly written.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Carbon Footprint Formula:** $CF = \sum (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$
- ✅ **Three Scopes:** Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), Scope 3 (value chain).
- ✅ **GWP-100 Values (AR5):** CO₂ = 1; CH₄ = 28; N₂O = 265; HFC-134a = 1,300; R-404a = 3,922; SF₆ = 23,500.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** ISO 14064 (organization), ISO 14067 (product), PAS 2050, GHG Protocol.
- ✅ **India Grid Emission Factor (CEA 2022):** 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh.
- ✅ **Common Fuel EFs:** PNG = 2.05 kg CO₂e/m³; Furnace oil = 3.20 kg CO₂e/L; Diesel = 2.68 kg CO₂e/L; Coal = 2.42 kg CO₂e/kg.
- ✅ **Pareto Principle:** ~80% CF from ~20% sources — always identify hotspots first.
- ✅ **Per-Unit Reporting:** Always normalize to functional unit (kg, litre, tonne-km).
- ✅ **Reduction Order:** Energy efficiency → Fuel switching → Renewables → Process integration → Supply chain → Offsets.
- ✅ **Food Industry Specific Hotspots:** Refrigerants (high GWP!), agricultural upstream, packaging, transport.
- ✅ **System Boundaries:** Cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, gate-to-gate — MUST be explicitly stated.
- ✅ **Always include units** in final answers — KTU is strict on this.
- ✅ **Biomass CO₂ is climate-neutral** under accounting rules but CH₄ from incomplete combustion is NOT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Mitigating Greenhouse Gas Emissions Through Energy Efficiency
Case Studies on Successful Implementation of Emission Reduction Initiatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on Successful Implementation of Emission Reduction Initiatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Emission Reduction Initiatives in the Food Industry** refer to a structured portfolio of operational, technological, energy-efficiency, renewable-energy, and supply-chain strategies deployed by food manufacturing enterprises to systematically lower their **Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3** greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions as defined by the *Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formal Definitions (Syllabus Anchors):**
> - **Scope 1 Emissions:** Direct GHG emissions from sources owned or controlled by the company (boilers, furnaces, on-site vehicles, refrigeration leaks).
> - **Scope 2 Emissions:** Indirect GHG emissions from purchased electricity, steam, heat, or cooling consumed by the company.
> - **Scope 3 Emissions:** All other indirect emissions occurring in the value chain (upstream agriculture, packaging, transport, downstream retail, consumer use, end-of-life).
> - **Carbon Neutrality:** Achieving a state where net CO₂-equivalent emissions released into the atmosphere are zero, typically via a mix of reductions and verified offsets.
> - **Net-Zero:** A deeper commitment where residual emissions are matched by removals (not offsets alone), aligned with the *Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi)*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Diet"**
>
> Imagine a large restaurant kitchen that produces three types of "mess":
> 1. **Smoke from its own stoves and ovens** (Scope 1 — direct on-site combustion).
> 2. **Steam leaks from the central heating pipes the landlord provides** (Scope 2 — bought energy).
> 3. **Food waste from customers' plates and packaging from suppliers** (Scope 3 — value chain).
>
> The restaurant cannot just clean its own stoves and ignore the rest. A *successful emission reduction initiative* is like a holistic "kitchen diet": it installs induction stoves (electrification), switches to renewable electricity contracts, sources local ingredients, composts waste, redesigns menus for plant-forward options, and tracks every gram of food waste. The chef measures progress weekly — just like a real factory measures CO₂e (CO₂-equivalent) in **tonnes of CO₂e per year**.

The standard metric used to express emission performance is **tonnes of CO₂-equivalent (tCO₂e)** per year, with **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** factors published by the **IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlighted Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **GWP of CO₂ = 1** (reference gas)
> - **GWP of CH₄ = 28** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR5)
> - **GWP of N₂O = 265** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR5)
> - **Standard emission factor for grid electricity in India ≈ 0.72 kg CO₂e/kWh** (CEA, Central Electricity Authority, 2023 baseline)
> - **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)** and **SBTi (Science Based Targets initiative)** are the principal global reporting frameworks cited in KTU module outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework for Implementing Emission Reduction Initiatives

A successful emission reduction program in the food industry follows a closed-loop **5-Phase Implementation Cycle**:

1. **Baseline Accounting (Measure)**
   - Quantify Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol.
   - Establish the *baseline year* (e.g., Nestlé uses **2018**; Unilever uses **2010**).
   - Why: *"You cannot reduce what you cannot measure."*

2. **Target Setting (Commit)**
   - Set *Science-Based Targets* aligned with the **1.5 °C pathway** of the Paris Agreement.
   - Why: *Ensures the company's reduction trajectory is consistent with global climate stabilization.*

3. **Initiative Identification & Prioritization (Plan)**
   - Use **Marginal Abatement Cost Curve (MACC)** analysis to rank interventions by cost per tCO₂e avoided.
   - How: *Plot cost (₹/tCO₂e) versus potential reduction (tCO₂e/year) to identify "negative-cost" wins first.*

4. **Implementation (Act)**
   - Deploy technological, operational, and procurement interventions.
   - Cross-functional teams: Engineering, Procurement, R&D, Sustainability, Finance.

5. **Verification & Disclosure (Verify)**
   - Third-party audit by accredited bodies (e.g., **DNV, SGS, Bureau Veritas**).
   - Disclose via **CDP Climate Change Questionnaire** and **GRI Standards**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂-equivalent emission** | $E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | tCO₂e | Convert mixed GHGs into a common unit |
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | kWh/kg or GJ/tonne | Energy intensity of food process |
| **Specific Emission Intensity (SEI)** | $SEI = \dfrac{E_{CO_2e}}{M_{product}}$ | kg CO₂e/kg product | Carbon footprint per unit product |
| **Emission Reduction Percentage** | $\%R = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{current}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Progress against baseline year |
| **Cumulative Avoided Emission** | $C_{avoided} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (E_{baseline,t} - E_{actual,t})$ | tCO₂e | Lifetime project impact |
| **Marginal Abatement Cost** | $MAC = \dfrac{C_{annual} - S_{annual}}{E_{avoided}}$ | ₹/tCO₂e | Prioritize cheapest wins |
| **Renewable Energy Fraction** | $f_{RE} = \dfrac{E_{renewable}}{E_{total}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Share of green power |
| **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{CapEx}{Annual\ Cash\ Savings}$ | years | Investment viability |
| **Carbon Price Sensitivity** | $\Delta P = P_{carbon} \times E_{CO_2e}$ | ₹/year | Risk exposure under carbon tax |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** In all equations above, $\vert$ inside absolute value expressions has been replaced by the standard math notation `\` `vert` ` or contextually clear phrasing to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

These formulas are not academic — they are the *exact tools* corporate sustainability teams use:
- **SEC** drives ISO 50001 energy reviews in dairy plants.
- **MAC curves** decide whether a company invests in biomass boilers vs. solar PV first.
- **SEI** is what an FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods) buyer demands from suppliers like Nestlé and Mondelez under their *Supplier Climate Program*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Baseline Emissions to Reduction Percentage

**Problem Context:** A food processing plant had a baseline emission of **12,500 tCO₂e** in 2020. By 2024, after installing a biomass boiler (replacing a coal boiler), a 2 MW rooftop solar PV system, and a heat-recovery system on the spray dryer, actual emissions dropped to **8,750 tCO₂e**. Calculate the **percentage reduction** and the **cumulative avoided emissions** over the 4-year period, assuming linear year-on-year reduction.

---

#### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
E_{baseline,2020} = 12{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
E_{actual,2024} = 8{,}750 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
\Delta t = 2024 - 2020 = 4 \ \text{years}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Compute Annual Reduction in tCO₂e

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = E_{baseline,2020} - E_{actual,2024}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 12{,}500 - 8{,}750 = 3{,}750 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Interpretation:** Over the 4-year window, the plant avoided **3,750 tCO₂e** in 2024 versus the baseline year.

---

#### Step 3: Compute Year-on-Year Linear Reduction Slope

$$
m = \dfrac{\Delta E_{annual}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{3{,}750}{4} = 937.5 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/year}
$$

---

#### Step 4: Derive the Emission Trajectory Equation

The emission in any year *t* after 2020 is given by:

$$
E_{actual}(t) = E_{baseline,2020} - m \cdot t
$$

$$
E_{actual}(t) = 12{,}500 - 937.5 \cdot t \quad \text{[t in years after 2020]}
$$

---

#### Step 5: Calculate Percentage Reduction

$$
\%R = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%R = \dfrac{12{,}500 - 8{,}750}{12{,}500} \times 100
$$

$$
\%R = \dfrac{3{,}750}{12{,}500} \times 100 = 0.30 \times 100 = 30\%
$$

---

#### Step 6: Calculate Cumulative Avoided Emissions (Linear Assumption)

For a linear trajectory, the cumulative avoided emission is the area of the trapezoid under the reduction curve:

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{(E_{baseline,2020} + E_{actual,2024})}{2} \times \Delta t
$$

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{(12{,}500 + 8{,}750)}{2} \times 4
$$

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{21{,}250}{2} \times 4 = 10{,}625 \times 4 = 42{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

---

#### Step 7: Validate Against an Arithmetic Series

The yearly avoided emissions form an arithmetic series:
**Year 1:** 937.5, **Year 2:** 1,875, **Year 3:** 2,812.5, **Year 4:** 3,750 (cumulative).

$$
S_n = \dfrac{n}{2} \times (a_1 + a_n) = \dfrac{4}{2} \times (937.5 + 3{,}750) = 2 \times 4{,}687.5 = 9{,}375 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

> **Note:** The two methods differ because *Trapezoid (Step 6)* integrates the **emission levels** themselves, while the *Arithmetic Series (Step 7)* integrates the **incremental reductions**. Both are correct under their stated definitions and must not be confused in examiner answers.

---

#### Step 8: Final Interpretation for the KTU Board Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{Percentage Reduction} = 30\% \quad ; \quad \text{Cumulative Avoided Emissions} = 42{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Four Canonical Case Studies (FMCG Food Sector)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix distills the **four most cited case studies** in the KTU syllabus and IPCC/CDP literature. Each row is exam-ready.

| **#** | **Company** | **Initiative Headline** | **Baseline / Target** | **Key Levers** | **Outcome Achieved** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Nestlé (Global)** | *Net-Zero Roadmap by 2050* | Baseline: 2018 = ~92 MtCO₂e; Target: -50% by 2030, Net-Zero by 2050 | Regenerative agriculture in dairy & cocoa, renewable electricity via PPA (Power Purchase Agreement), thermal heat-pumps, low-carbon logistics | **-13.5% absolute Scope 1+2+3** reduction by 2023; **>50% renewable electricity** globally |
| **2** | **Unilever (Global)** | *Climate Transition Action Plan* | Baseline: 2010 = ~155 kg CO₂e per consumer use; Target: Net-Zero by 2039 | Refrigerant transition to R-744 (CO₂) and HFOs (Hydrofluoroolefins), plant-based formulation (Hellmann's vegan), supplier engagement via *Unilever Climate Promise* | **~33% reduction** per consumer use by 2023; **100% renewable grid electricity** in 5 European factories |
| **3** | **Britannia Industries (India)** | *Sustainable Manufacturing Program* | Baseline FY2018; Target: 50% renewable electricity, 100% waste recycling | 16 MW solar-wind hybrid, biomass briquette boilers at Khurda & Haridwar, ETP (Effluent Treatment Plant) biogas-to-boiler, rainwater harvesting | **~70% renewable electricity** by FY24; **~40% specific energy reduction** in Top 10 SKUs (Stock Keeping Units) |
| **4** | **Tyson Foods (USA)** | *Protein Industry Methane Reduction* | Baseline 2020; Target: -50% Scope 1+2 by 2030 | *Sustainable Feed* innovation (seaweed/3-NOP feed additives for cattle), anaerobic digestion of processing wastewater, fleet electrification (electric forklifts) | **~26% reduction in operational emissions** by 2023; first major meat company to publish SBTi-validated targets |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Britannia Khurda Plant Boiler Retrofit

**Scenario:** Britannia replaced a **2 TPH (Tonnes Per Hour) coal-fired boiler** with a **3 TPH biomass briquette boiler** at its Khurda (Odisha) facility.

**Given Data:**
- Annual operating hours: **5,400 h/year**
- Coal GCV (Gross Calorific Value): **4,200 kcal/kg**; Coal consumption: **250 kg/h**
- Biomass GCV: **3,800 kcal/kg**; Biomass consumption: **285 kg/h**
- Coal emission factor: **2.42 kg CO₂e/kg** (Indian CPCB, Central Pollution Control Board standard)
- Biomass emission factor: **0.04 kg CO₂e/kg** (treated as carbon-neutral per IPCC for sustainably sourced biomass)
- Boiler thermal efficiency unchanged: **70%**

**Step A: Annual Coal Mass Consumed (Baseline)**

$$
M_{coal} = 250 \ \text{kg/h} \times 5{,}400 \ \text{h/yr} = 1{,}350{,}000 \ \text{kg/yr} = 1{,}350 \ \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step B: Baseline CO₂ Emissions**

$$
E_{baseline} = M_{coal} \times EF_{coal} = 1{,}350{,}000 \ \text{kg} \times 2.42 = 3{,}267{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

$$
E_{baseline} \approx 3{,}267 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step C: Annual Biomass Mass Consumed (Post-Retrofit)**

$$
M_{biomass} = 285 \ \text{kg/h} \times 5{,}400 \ \text{h/yr} = 1{,}539{,}000 \ \text{kg/yr}
$$

**Step D: Post-Retrofit CO₂ Emissions**

$$
E_{actual} = 1{,}539{,}000 \times 0.04 = 61{,}560 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} \approx 61.6 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step E: Annual Avoided Emissions**

$$
E_{avoided} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual} = 3{,}267 - 61.6 = 3{,}205.4 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step F: % Reduction**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{3{,}205.4}{3{,}267} \times 100 \approx 98.1\%
$$

**Step G: Carbon Price Exposure Saved (Assume ₹1,500/tCO₂e under India's emerging CBT — Carbon Border Tax)**

$$
\Delta P = 3{,}205.4 \times 1{,}500 = \text{₹}48{,}08{,}100/\text{yr} \approx \text{₹}48.08 \ \text{lakh/yr}
$$

> **Result:** The retrofit avoids **~3,205 tCO₂e/year** (a **~98% reduction**) and protects the plant against **~₹48 lakh/year** of future carbon cost exposure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions with one food-industry example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Scope 1** (1 mark): Direct emissions from owned or controlled sources. *Example:* CO₂ from an on-site coal-fired boiler in a dairy plant; refrigerant leak (HFC, Hydrofluorocarbon) from a cold storage chiller.
- **Scope 2** (1 mark): Indirect emissions from purchased electricity, steam, heat, or cooling. *Example:* Grid electricity used to run mixers, conveyors, and packaging lines in a bakery.
- **Scope 3** (1 mark): All other indirect emissions in the value chain (upstream and downstream). *Example:* Methane from a dairy farm supplying milk to a cheese factory; emissions from consumer disposal of packaged food.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is the Marginal Abatement Cost Curve (MACC) and why is it useful in prioritizing emission reduction initiatives?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** MACC is a graphical tool that plots the *cost of avoiding one additional tonne of CO₂-equivalent* (₹/tCO₂e) on the y-axis against the *potential emission reduction* (tCO₂e/year) on the x-axis, for a portfolio of abatement technologies.
- **Construction (1 mark):** Initiatives are ranked from most cost-effective (left) to least (right). Initiatives below the x-axis are "negative-cost" — they save money while reducing emissions (e.g., LED lighting, VFD — Variable Frequency Drive on pumps).
- **Utility (1 mark):** It helps sustainability managers prioritize low-hanging fruit, build investment cases, and align capital expenditure with both climate and financial targets.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE, End Semester Exam]*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Discuss the emission reduction initiatives implemented by **Britannia Industries** at its Khurda plant under its Sustainable Manufacturing Program. Highlight at least **four specific initiatives**, their technical approach, and quantitative outcomes achieved. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A dairy plant consumes **1,200 kWh/day** from the grid at an emission factor of **0.72 kg CO₂e/kWh**. The plant installs a **250 kWp rooftop solar PV system** that generates **1,500 kWh/day** (assuming 6 sun-hours equivalent) and operates **300 days/year**. Calculate:
   (i) Annual grid emissions (baseline).
   (ii) Annual solar generation in kWh/year.
   (iii) Annual avoided emissions in tCO₂e/year.
   (iv) Percentage reduction in Scope 2 emissions.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Britannia Khurda Plant Initiatives (7 marks):**

1. **Solar-Wind Hybrid Renewable System (2 marks):** Britannia installed a hybrid renewable energy system covering wind + solar capacity across its manufacturing units, contributing to its target of 50%+ renewable electricity. At Khurda, this reduced grid dependence during peak solar hours and lowered daytime Scope 2 emissions.

2. **Biomass Briquette Boiler Retrofit (2 marks):** Replacement of coal-fired boilers with biomass briquette-fired boilers (as computed in §3.3 above) avoided ~3,205 tCO₂e/year per boiler — a ~98% reduction in direct combustion emissions for that asset.

3. **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) Biogas Recovery (1.5 marks):** Wastewater is processed through anaerobic treatment, generating biogas that is piped back to the boiler as supplementary fuel — closing a circular loop and reducing both fossil-fuel demand and methane (a high-GWP gas) emissions.

4. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Reduction (1.5 marks):** Through process optimization (VFDs on motors, heat recovery from oven exhaust, LED lighting), Britannia achieved a ~40% reduction in SEC for its top 10 SKUs versus FY2018 baseline.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Annual Grid Emissions (Baseline) — (2 marks):**

$$
E_{grid} = 1{,}200 \ \text{kWh/day} \times 300 \ \text{days/yr} \times 0.72 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/kWh}
$$

$$
E_{grid} = 360{,}000 \times 0.72 = 259{,}200 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

$$
E_{grid} = 259.2 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**(ii) Annual Solar Generation — (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{solar} = 1{,}500 \ \text{kWh/day} \times 300 \ \text{days/yr} = 450{,}000 \ \text{kWh/yr}
$$

**(iii) Annual Avoided Emissions — (2 marks):**

Solar generation exceeds daily grid consumption (1,500 > 1,200 kWh/day). For conservatism, grid emissions avoided = min(solar, grid) × EF.

$$
E_{avoided} = 1{,}200 \times 300 \times 0.72 = 259{,}200 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} = 259.2 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

(The remaining 300 kWh/day of solar is exported to the grid or stored — for the dairy's net Scope 2, this gives 100% reduction during those hours.)

**(iv) Percentage Reduction in Scope 2 — (1.5 marks):**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{259.2}{259.2} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

(The plant becomes Scope-2-zero for its operational load during the 300 solar-producing days.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse *gross solar generation* with *net grid displacement*. If solar generation (1,500 kWh/day) exceeds load (1,200 kWh/day), the maximum Scope 2 avoided emission is **capped at the load** unless the surplus is exported and credited. **Do not overstate reductions beyond the plant's actual consumption baseline.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.** *(a)* With the help of **Nestlé's Net-Zero Roadmap**, explain how a global food company integrates Scope 1, 2, and 3 reduction levers. Cite specific technologies and quantified outcomes where possible. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A brewery operates **3 boilers** each rated **1.5 TPH** consuming **FO (Furnace Oil)** with an emission factor of **3.2 kg CO₂e/kg**. Annual FO consumption = **900 tonnes/year**. The brewery installs a **biogas recovery system** from its spent-grain ETP that generates **180 tonnes/year of biogas** (substitution rate 1:1 by mass). Calculate:
   (i) Baseline annual emissions in tCO₂e.
   (ii) Post-retrofit emissions in tCO₂e.
   (iii) Annual avoided emissions.
   (iv) % Reduction achieved.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nestlé's Net-Zero Roadmap — Lever Integration (7 marks):**

| **Scope** | **Lever** | **Technology / Approach** | **Quantified Outcome** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope 1** (2 marks) | Thermal energy transition | Electric boilers, biomass, heat pumps replacing natural-gas furnaces in factories | ~30% absolute Scope 1 reduction in European factories by 2023 |
| **Scope 2** (2 marks) | 100% renewable electricity | On-site solar PV, wind PPAs, RECs (Renewable Energy Certificates) | 50%+ renewable electricity globally in 2023; target 100% by 2025 |
| **Scope 3** (3 marks) | Regenerative agriculture & supply chain | Working with 500,000+ farmers; sourcing low-carbon dairy, cocoa, wheat; supplier engagement via Nestlé Cocoa Plan | ~13.5% absolute reduction across Scopes 1+2+3 by 2023; target -50% by 2030 |
| **Residual** | Nature-based solutions | Reforestation, soil-carbon sequestration, mangrove restoration | Offsetting residual ~10% via verified removals |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Baseline Emissions (2 marks):**

$$
E_{baseline} = 900{,}000 \ \text{kg} \times 3.2 = 2{,}880{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} = 2{,}880 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**(ii) Post-Retrofit Emissions (2 marks):**

Biogas replaces 180 tonnes FO; remaining FO = 900 − 180 = 720 tonnes.

$$
E_{actual} = 720{,}000 \times 3.2 = 2{,}304{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} = 2{,}304 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Note:** We assume biogas has near-zero CO₂e factor (carbon-neutral under IPCC for sustainably sourced biomass/biogas from organic waste). If methane leakage from the digester is non-zero, an EF of ~0.04 kg CO₂e/kg biogas would apply.

**(iii) Annual Avoided Emissions (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{avoided} = 2{,}880 - 2{,}304 = 576 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**(iv) Percentage Reduction (1.5 marks):**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{576}{2{,}880} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For biogas/methane-based fuels, students often **omit the upstream methane leakage** in ETP-to-boiler systems. A more rigorous answer would include a 1–3% leakage adjustment. Also, students mistakenly use *mass* substitution (1:1) without verifying that boiler GCV is comparable — a calorific-value-based substitution is technically more accurate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Recap for Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> - **Scope 1, 2, 3** must be addressed for any credible food-industry emission program — citing only Scope 2 is incomplete.
> - **Baseline year** is non-negotiable: Nestlé = 2018; Unilever = 2010; Tyson = 2020.
> - **SBTi (Science Based Targets initiative)** validates 1.5 °C-aligned targets — mention in any "target-setting" answer.
> - **Top 4 levers** to memorize for KTU: **Energy efficiency → Renewable electricity → Process electrification → Value-chain engagement**.
> - **Formulas:** Memorize the **%R** and **Cumulative Avoided** formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU numerical question.
> - **Carbon neutrality vs. Net-Zero** — neutrality allows offsets; net-zero requires removals for residuals. *Do not confuse in answers.*
> - **India-specific:** Central Electricity Authority grid factor = **0.72 kg CO₂e/kWh** (approximate, varies by year).
> - **CDM (Clean Development Mechanism) / PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade)** — Indian regulatory frameworks for carbon credits; relevant if examiner asks about "carbon market mechanism."
> - **Case study recall:** Know at least **2 companies in detail** (one global — Nestlé/Unilever; one Indian — Britannia).
> - **Numerical traps:** Always check units (kWh vs MWh, kg vs tonnes); always cap emission avoidance at the load or generation, whichever is smaller.
> - **Avoid these words in answers:** "approximately" without numbers, "significant reduction" without %, "sustainable" without specifying scope.
> - **Citation standard:** When citing companies, mention the **baseline year** and **target year** together (e.g., "Britannia baseline FY2018, target 50% renewable electricity by FY2030").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Sustainable Food Processing** is defined as a holistic, systems-engineering approach to transforming agricultural raw materials into value-added food products using processing technologies, energy resources, and waste management strategies that simultaneously **minimize environmental impact**, **conserve non-renewable resources**, and **ensure long-term economic viability and social responsibility** across the entire food value chain.

**Energy Management in Sustainable Food Processing** integrates emerging digital technologies, renewable energy systems, circular bioeconomy principles, and advanced process intensification techniques to achieve net-zero carbon emissions, energy self-sufficiency, and optimal exergy utilization in food manufacturing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Core Theme:** The convergence of **Industry 4.0**, **Renewable Energy Systems**, **Circular Economy**, and **Advanced Process Technologies** to transform conventional energy-intensive food processing into a smart, low-carbon, and resilient industrial ecosystem.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Domains of Future Trends

The future of energy management in food processing can be classified into **six interconnected innovation pillars**:

1. **Digital Transformation & Industry 4.0** — IoT, AI/ML, Digital Twins, Blockchain
2. **Renewable Energy Integration** — Solar, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Green Hydrogen
3. **Advanced Process Technologies** — Non-thermal processing, Microwave, PEF, Ultrasound
4. **Energy Storage Innovations** — Batteries, Thermal Storage, Hydrogen Storage
5. **Circular Bioeconomy & Waste-to-Energy** — Anaerobic Digestion, Biorefineries
6. **Smart Grids & Energy Communities** — Microgrids, Peer-to-Peer Energy Trading

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Smart, Self-Sufficient Kitchen":**
> Imagine a futuristic kitchen that:
> - **Senses** what ingredients you have (IoT sensors)
> - **Decides** the most energy-efficient cooking method (AI optimization)
> - **Stores** solar energy captured from its roof in a battery (energy storage)
> - **Converts** vegetable peels into biogas to power the stove (waste-to-energy)
> - **Shares** excess energy with the neighbor's kitchen (peer-to-peer energy trading)
> - **Tracks** its carbon footprint in real-time (digital twin + blockchain)
>
> This "kitchen" is exactly what a **future sustainable food processing plant** will look like — intelligent, self-sufficient, zero-waste, and climate-positive.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Sustainable Food Processing:**
> - **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption):** typically **1–10 MJ/kg** of product
> - **Carbon Footprint:** measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent per kg product** (Global Warming Potential – GWP)
> - **Exergy Efficiency (η_ex):** dimensionless ratio, ideally **> 60%**
> - **Energy Return on Investment (EROI):** ratio of energy output to input
> - **Renewable Energy Share (RES):** percentage of total energy from renewables
> - **Process Integration Index (PII):** degree of heat/work integration
> - **Water-Energy-Food (WEF) Nexus Coefficient**

**Physical Constants used in calculations:**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_0 = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Biogas:** $\approx 20 \, \text{MJ/m}^3$
- **Solar Constant (AM 1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Six Innovation Pillars — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

#### **Pillar 1: Industry 4.0 & Digital Technologies**

**Internet of Things (IoT)**
- Network of interconnected sensors, actuators, and controllers monitoring real-time energy parameters (temperature, pressure, flow, power quality) in food processing lines.
- Uses protocols like **MQTT**, **Modbus**, **OPC-UA** for data communication.
- **Smart meters** record 15-minute interval energy data for granular load profiling.

**Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning (AI/ML)**
- **Predictive Energy Models** using regression, Random Forest, LSTM neural networks.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** for real-time process control optimization.
- **Digital Twin** — a virtual replica of the physical plant enabling simulation, what-if analysis, and predictive maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Twin Equation (Simplified):**
> $$S_{t+1} = f(S_t, U_t, D_t)$$
> where $S_t$ = system state, $U_t$ = control inputs, $D_t$ = disturbances, and $f$ = physics-based + data-driven model.

**Blockchain Technology**
- Distributed ledger for **energy traceability**, **renewable energy certificates (RECs)**, and **carbon credit trading**.
- Each kWh of energy can be tokenized and tracked from source to consumption.

#### **Pillar 2: Renewable Energy Integration**

**Solar Thermal & Photovoltaic (PV)**
- **PV systems** for direct electricity generation.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** for process heat up to **400 °C** — suitable for sterilization, evaporation, drying.
- **Parabolic Trough Collectors (PTC):** thermal efficiency 60–75%.

**Biomass & Biogas**
- **Anaerobic Digestion (AD):** converts organic food waste into biogas.
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** captures both electricity and heat from biogas combustion.

**Geothermal Energy**
- Ground-source heat pumps (GSHP) for low-temperature process heating/cooling with **COP = 3–5**.

**Green Hydrogen**
- Produced via **electrolysis** powered by renewable electricity.
- Used in fuel cells for clean electricity or as reducing agent in food processing.

#### **Pillar 3: Advanced Process Technologies**

| Technology | Mechanism | Energy Advantage |
|------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** | Non-thermal cell membrane electroporation | **70–80% less energy** vs. thermal pasteurization |
| **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** | 400–600 MPa isostatic pressure | Retains nutrients, **30% energy savings** |
| **Ultrasound (US)** | Acoustic cavitation | Enhances heat/mass transfer |
| **Microwave (MW)** | Dielectric heating | **50% faster drying**, volumetric heating |
| **Ohmic Heating** | Direct Joule heating of food | **90%+ energy transfer efficiency** |
| **Membrane Filtration** | Micro/Ultra/Nano-filtration | Replaces evaporation, **10× less energy** |

#### **Pillar 4: Energy Storage Systems (ESS)**

- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS):** Li-ion, Na-S, Flow batteries.
- **Thermal Energy Storage (TES):** Sensible (water, molten salt), Latent (PCM), Thermochemical.
- **Power-to-Gas (P2G):** Excess renewable electricity → H₂ → CH₄ → stored in gas grid.
- **Supercapacitors** for short-duration power quality improvement.

#### **Pillar 5: Circular Bioeconomy & Waste-to-Energy**

- **Biorefinery Concept:** Food waste → multiple products (biogas, bioethanol, bioplastics, biofertilizers).
- **Cascading Use:** Material → Energy recovery hierarchy.
- **Industrial Symbiosis:** By-products of one industry become feedstock of another.
- **Exergy Recovery:** Capturing usable work potential from waste heat streams.

#### **Pillar 6: Smart Grids & Energy Communities**

- **Microgrids:** Localized grids with own generation, storage, and load.
- **Virtual Power Plants (VPP):** Aggregated DERs acting as single dispatchable unit.
- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Energy Trading:** Blockchain-based direct energy transactions.
- **Demand Response (DR):** Adjusting processing schedules to match renewable generation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{m_{product}}$ | **MJ/kg** | Benchmarking process energy |
| 2 | **Carbon Footprint** | $CF = \sum_i (E_i \times EF_i)$ | **kg CO₂e/kg** | GHG accounting |
| 3 | **Exergy Destruction (Gouy-Stodola)** | $\dot{E}_{D} = T_0 \times \dot{S}_{gen}$ | **kW** | Irreversibility analysis |
| 4 | **Exergy Efficiency** | $\eta_{ex} = \dfrac{\dot{E}_{useful}}{\dot{E}_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Second-law efficiency |
| 5 | **PV Power Output** | $P_{PV} = A \times G \times \eta_{PV} \times PR$ | **W (peak)** | Solar system sizing |
| 6 | **Wind Power** | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | **W** | Wind turbine output |
| 7 | **CHP Electrical Efficiency** | $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{W_{el}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | Cogeneration design |
| 8 | **Heat Pump COP** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{heat}}{W_{input}}$ | dimensionless | GSHP performance |
| 9 | **Anaerobic Digestion Yield** | $Y_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4}$ | **MJ/kg VS** | Biogas energy potential |
| 10 | **Energy Return on Investment** | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{output}}{E_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Net energy gain |
| 11 | **Thermal Storage Capacity** | $Q_{TES} = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | **kJ or kWh** | Sensible TES sizing |
| 12 | **Latent Heat Storage** | $Q_{latent} = m \times h_{sl}$ | **kJ** | PCM-based storage |
| 13 | **Payback Period** | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{C_{annual\,savings}}$ | **years** | Project economics |
| 14 | **Levelized Cost of Energy** | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum C_t / (1+r)^t}{\sum E_t / (1+r)^t}$ | **$/kWh** | Energy source comparison |
| 15 | **Pinch Analysis (ΔT_min)** | $\Delta T_{min} = T_{hot,supply} - T_{cold,target}$ | **°C or K** | Heat exchanger network design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always specify **units** and **assumptions** (steady-state, ambient conditions $T_0 = 25\,°C$, $P_0 = 1\,atm$) when solving numerical problems. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **energy, exergy, and economic (3E) analysis** combined.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Solar PV System Sizing for a Food Processing Plant

> **Problem:** A dairy processing plant consumes **5,000 kWh/day** with a peak load of **400 kW**. The plant is located in Kerala (average daily solar insolation **5.5 kWh/m²/day**). Design a grid-tied rooftop solar PV system.

**Given:**
- Daily energy demand: $E_{daily} = 5{,}000$ kWh/day
- Peak load: $P_{peak} = 400$ kW
- Solar insolation: $G = 5.5$ kWh/m²/day
- PV module efficiency: $\eta_{PV} = 18\%$
- Performance Ratio (system losses): $PR = 0.80$
- Module area (1 kWp): $A_{mod} = 5.5$ m²/kWp
- System lifetime: $n = 25$ years
- Grid emission factor: $EF_{grid} = 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh (Kerala grid)

**Step 1: Daily Energy Output per kWp of Installed PV Capacity**

The energy output from 1 kWp of installed capacity per day is calculated using the standard PV energy equation:

$$E_{kWp,day} = G \times \eta_{PV} \times PR$$

Substituting the numerical values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{kWp,day} &= 5.5 \times 0.18 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.792 \text{ kWh/kWp/day}
\end{aligned}$$

This means **1 kWp of installed PV capacity produces 0.792 kWh per day** under Kerala conditions.

**Step 2: Required Installed PV Capacity**

To meet the daily demand of 5,000 kWh, we need to find the total kWp rating. This is obtained by dividing the daily energy demand by the daily energy produced per kWp:

$$P_{PV,required} = \dfrac{E_{daily}}{E_{kWp,day}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{PV,required} &= \dfrac{5{,}000}{0.792} \\
&= 6{,}313.13 \text{ kWp} \\
&\approx 6.32 \text{ MWp}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Total Module Area Required**

The total roof area required for installation is found by multiplying the required capacity by the area per kWp of standard modules:

$$A_{total} = P_{PV,required} \times A_{mod}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= 6{,}313.13 \times 5.5 \\
&= 34{,}722.2 \text{ m}^2 \\
&\approx 3.47 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Annual Energy Generation**

Multiplying daily output by 365 days gives the total annual energy generated by the system:

$$E_{annual} = P_{PV,required} \times E_{kWp,day} \times 365$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= 6{,}313.13 \times 0.792 \times 365 \\
&= 1{,}825{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} \\
&= 1.825 \text{ GWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Annual CO₂ Emission Reduction**

The annual CO₂ reduction equals the annual energy generated multiplied by the grid emission factor, since this energy displaces grid electricity:

$$CO_2_{reduced} = E_{annual} \times EF_{grid}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2_{reduced} &= 1{,}825{,}000 \times 0.82 \\
&= 1{,}496{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/year} \\
&= 1{,}496.5 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: 25-Year Lifetime CO₂ Avoidance**

Total CO₂ emissions avoided over the project's 25-year operational lifetime:

$$CO_2_{lifetime} = CO_2_{reduced} \times 25$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2_{lifetime} &= 1{,}496.5 \times 25 \\
&= 37{,}412.5 \text{ tonnes CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **6.32 MWp solar plant** covering **3.47 hectares** can meet the entire daily energy demand of this dairy plant, avoiding **37,413 tonnes of CO₂** over its 25-year life — equivalent to taking **~8,100 cars off the road** for one year.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Biogas Energy Potential from Food Waste

> **Problem:** A food processing unit generates **10 tonnes/day** of mixed food waste. Estimate the **biogas yield**, **electricity generation potential**, and **economic value**.

**Given:**
- Daily waste: $m_{waste} = 10{,}000$ kg/day
- Volatile Solids (VS) content: $VS\% = 85\%$
- VS destroyed in digester: $\eta_{VS} = 70\%$
- Biogas yield: $Y_{biogas} = 0.5$ m³/kg VS
- Methane content: $CH_4\% = 60\%$
- LHV of methane: $LHV_{CH_4} = 35.8$ MJ/m³
- CHP electrical efficiency: $\eta_{el,CHP} = 35\%$
- Tariff: $C_{elec} = ₹6/\text{kWh}$

**Step 1: Total Volatile Solids Available per Day**

Multiplying the total waste by the volatile solids percentage gives the amount of biodegradable organic matter available for digestion:

$$VS_{available} = m_{waste} \times \dfrac{VS\%}{100}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
VS_{available} &= 10{,}000 \times \dfrac{85}{100} \\
&= 8{,}500 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Volatile Solids Actually Destroyed in the Digester**

Not all VS are converted to biogas; the destruction efficiency of the digester is applied:

$$VS_{destroyed} = VS_{available} \times \eta_{VS}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
VS_{destroyed} &= 8{,}500 \times 0.70 \\
&= 5{,}950 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Daily Biogas Production Volume**

The volume of biogas produced per day is obtained by multiplying the destroyed VS by the specific biogas yield:

$$V_{biogas} = VS_{destroyed} \times Y_{biogas}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{biogas} &= 5{,}950 \times 0.5 \\
&= 2{,}975 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Methane Volume per Day**

Only the methane fraction of biogas contributes to energy; we extract the methane content:

$$V_{CH_4} = V_{biogas} \times \dfrac{CH_4\%}{100}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= 2{,}975 \times 0.60 \\
&= 1{,}785 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Thermal Energy Content of Methane**

The chemical energy stored in the methane is calculated from its volume and lower heating value:

$$E_{thermal} = V_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{thermal} &= 1{,}785 \times 35.8 \\
&= 63{,}903 \text{ MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Electrical Energy Generated by CHP**

Converting thermal energy to electrical energy using the CHP's electrical efficiency:

$$E_{electrical} = E_{thermal} \times \eta_{el,CHP}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{electrical} &= 63{,}903 \times 0.35 \\
&= 22{,}366.05 \text{ MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting MJ to kWh using the conversion factor $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{electrical} &= \dfrac{22{,}366.05}{3.6} \\
&= 6{,}212.79 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Annual Economic Value**

Multiplying the daily electrical generation by 365 days and the electricity tariff gives the annual revenue:

$$Rev_{annual} = E_{electrical} \times 365 \times C_{elec}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Rev_{annual} &= 6{,}212.79 \times 365 \times 6 \\
&= ₹1.36 \text{ crores/year} \\
&\approx ₹13{,}605{,}810/\text{year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **10 TPD food waste to biogas** system can power **~620 homes** continuously and earn **₹1.36 crores/year** — a perfect example of **circular bioeconomy** converting waste liability into energy asset.
